This is an excellent monitor which can serve multiple purposes.  I have briefly tried it for gaming and general computer use and found it to be excellent there.  However I primarily use it as a security camera monitor where it is showing live video transmission from multiple cameras.  The detail is outstanding and the color saturation is excellent.  There are a number of settings available to adjust brightness and other settings but I have found the standard settings, for the most part, to be excellent without being adjusted.

Very nice quality. I love using the 21:9 aspect ratio for games that support it. However, I would advise getting a bigger size as the vertical screen size can occasionally be stifling when trying to use other applications.

Full disclosure: I'm not an avid gamer nor videophile. I needed a larger, good quality monitor to replace an aging 19" screen for basic telecommuting purposes. I work from home often and this is a decent quality monitor that performs well for my needs. The brightness and clarity are both a big step up from my last monitor. I received the item in perfect condition and there were no dead pixels nor annoying "light bleed". I'm using two of these on a dual-monitor arm and the VESA mounting made attaching them a simple process. After using this monitor for 8-10 hours a day for the past few weeks, I have zero complaints. It is my personal opinion that this is an excellent monitor for the price.

Different cables, different ports, different computers. Nothing works. I'm reaching out to LG support. Amazon support said the only thing they would do is a refund because they're out of stock.

EXTERNAL QUALITY:

I chose this one over the competing Samsung XL-2370 model even though they were similarly priced (approximately $280) because I like (1) the Acer's shape - which is a sleek, plain rectangular shape, (2) the sturdy aluminum stand, and (3) because the screen was an inch bigger (24") than the Samsung model (23"). Also, I didn't like Samsung's casing - an acryllic plastic which looks rather "cheapy" (although it does look a bit fancy).

With regard to the speaker, I haven't used it yet because I rely on my external speakers, but I will say it looks to be cheaply made - I can gently push my finger on the speaker face and it will sink in some because the face of the speaker is covered by a thin weak sheet of metal/plastic (can't tell). Had the speaker been of better build-quality I would have given the Acer a better rating.

One other highlight: I like how the screen doesn't get warm even with extended use. Good ol' LED technology!

INTERNAL QUALITY:

The quality of the screen is good, but a caveat is that I'm not too picky about picture quality because I use it for basic computing and the occasional movie. It has worked perfectly fine for performing those basic tasks.

CONCLUSION:

If I could go back in time I would buy it again.

This product is a great value for a display at this resolution.

Unless you've got hawk-eye vision, even at 27" wide and sitting on a desk 30-36" away from my face, the maximum resolution is really too fine to be able to read text well, so I ended up dialing it back a notch and it works great at that setting.

My suggestion would be that, if you want to go super-high resolution, I think you probably need a 30"+ monitor to get real value out of it..

Overall I am very pleased with this monitor and would definitely recommend it to anyone on a budget. You'd be hard pressed to beat it for the price. However, it is not perfect...

PROS

- Screen is very bright (I had to turn it down), and very clear at native resolution

- Mine has no dead pixels

- "Ghosting" effect while paying fast-paced games is very minimal

- Comes with a DVI cable (many don't)

- GridVista software included is a handy tool that allows you to arrange open windows in a grid on your screen so you don't have to constantly switch between them.

CONS

- I can notice a *slight* change in brightness from the top of the screen to the bottom, even when looking straight at the screen. When I tilt the screen up or down, this difference becomes more apparent. But it isn't so bad as to be distracting as long as you have the monitor facing you perfectly.

- The screen is only 6-bit, which is below the ideal 8-bit. What this means is the monitor can only display a limited number of colors. Most users may not notice the minor dithering this causes, but to those who are into professional photo or video editing, you should probably cough up more cash for a monitor with an 8-bit screen.

- The stand is difficult to adjust and doesn't allow for much customization.

- The connectors are located in an inconvenient spot behind the stand.

For me, the pros far outweigh the cons. If you aren't looking to spend a boatload but want a large, impressive widescreen then this is the monitor you want!

The monitor itself is great- nice build and image/picture quality. As others have noted- there are two things that will be seen as a negative, if you are expecting something better:

First, the stand is flimsy plastic, and the monitor sits very low compared to others. I had written earlier that it didn't tilt, but have found out since that it does- it is just really hard to get it right where you want it, especially if you use two of these side-by-side, because the stand is so flexible. The monitor will jiggle with very little movement of the desk/table it is on- it might as well be a spring. The stand just snaps on with plastic clips- no screws. That said- these monitors are perfect if you want to mount them using an after-market articulated desk, multi-monitor, wall, or other mount. You will probably be disappointed if you use it with the included stand.

Second, the speakers are really only suitable for notification sounds/beeps. I have yet to actually buy a monitor that has speakers that are even remotely good, so in that respect- this doesn't surprise me. If you are trying to get rid of desktop speakers- this monitor is not going to help you.

Also- take note that this only ships with a VGA cable. I suspect most people have either graduated to DVI or already have a drawer or box full of old VGA cables. I'd almost rather see them drop the cable entirely and put some money into the stand, otherwise- ship it with a DVI cable if not both.

Buy this as a quality 24" monitor that is perfect for a multi-monitor desk mount. Don't count on good speakers in ANY monitor, and if you do want a better stand- pay $10-$20 more for a higher-up model from ViewSonic. they really do make good monitors, so the price difference is usually on the extras.

Exceeded expectations on use. It helps reduce eye strain and is very clear.

Pros:
Nice look and feel. The clear plastic spacer on the bottom keeps the working surface up high enough for comfortable touchscreen use. Glass of from edge to edge makes Windows 8 edge swipes simple and easy. Variable tilt support arm is just right for upright use, or laying down and using system from above.

Cons:
I could not get the touchscreen to wake up any computer from sleep. When the computer goes to sleep, the mouse and keyboard would wake it, but touching the screen does nothing. Could not find any information from Acer or by searching the internet. Had to resort to black screensaver and never going to sleep.

Also, sometimes the touchscreen would not recognize, but gave a USB Device Descriptor Request failed error. I tried 3 computers, running both 32 and 64 bit windows. After a week of emails to Acer tech support, I resolved the issue by unplugged the power and holding down the power button for 10 seconds. When plugged back in, it was immediately recognized and began working. It did this twice while it was on the bench in my office. The device is now in production in a classroom, so we will see how it does in the future.

Lastly, when using more then one touchpoint, say 3 to 5 fingers when swiping in a game, the touch really lags. While this may be a product of CPU usage on the computer, it means this touchscreen should not be connected to anything below a i3 processor if you want good touchscreen responsiveness.

The Acer Predator X34 GS is an incredible ultrawide monitor. The brightness reaches 550nits. It has 180hz of room if you don't notice the difference between 10bit and 8 bit (for 10bit you're limited to 144hz) Now there are other more expensive bigger ultrawides out there. There's also that new OLED ultrawide from samsung. But that thing after tax is $1400. Hey if you have that amount to spend on it more power to you. But if you're looking to stay under $1000 for a monitor this one is it! Yes there are cheaper VA alternatives but you will be losing that crispyness that you get with IPS. The IPS panel in the X34 GS is super crisp, bright and vibrant. All in all this is the best ultrawide under $1000 and i'm surprised Hardware Unboxed( great Youtube resource for monitor reviews) hasn't reviewed this one yet (it was only on Linus Tech Tips as a overview-also on Youtube)

Overall it's the best monitor I've ever had, and completely puts my 60hz 4k LG OLED to shame for gaming. The color isn't as rich but for the price this is a steal. I'd buy two more but they'd be sticking off the sides of my desk.

I've only had this for a day, but I'm very happy with it. I use it with a MacBook Pro 15" retina, so I have full Thunderbolt 2 connectivity. Contrary to some others' experience, I had no trouble getting the computer to recognize it at full 3440x1440, 60 Hz. Again contrary to others, I find no disturbing backlight bleed in the corners: no doubt due to the fact that I don't use it with black backgrounds, but when I did display something with black on the sides, I didn't notice anything in the corners.
It's wonderful to have so much screen real estate without the discontinuity between two monitors. I use this for "office" tasks: the way I write, it's ideal to have four full windows open side by side, and with this display I can do that while leaving Mail open on the MBP's screen at the same time.
The curve is hardly noticeable, but probably contributes to the impression of a uniform big screen.
On the mildly negative side, this isn't of course 4K resolution, and the difference between what I see on the big screen and the resolution on the retina MBP screen next to it is noticeable. If I were doing video editing or the like, this might be a problem, but for me this really isn't an issue: the screen is not so big that the pixelation is noticeable. The experience is at least as good as on the 30" cinema display in my office, and I'm quite happy with it.
It would be nice to have height adjustment. Not sure why that isn't here, given that the flat screen equivalent has it.
The plastic cover over the cables on the back is no problem for me, since I don't plan on changing the cable connections often. The one thing that might produce problems in the long run would be if I had to get to the on/off switch often. So far, it looks like the display sleeps nicely when the computer does, so no apparent problem there.
Overall, this seems to be the display I've been looking for. I do wish Apple had produced a 4K TB display, but even if they had, it probably wouldn't have met my needs (screen real estate) as well as this one does.

Update, 10 December: I have downgraded my review from 5 to 2 stars now, not because of the monitor itself but because of Quality Control issues. When the monitor arrived and I set it up, I was delighted with all that screen real estate, and the initial experience was very positive. My MacBook Pro-Retina recognized the monitor and its ideal resolution (3440x1440, 60 Hz) immediately, and only a tiny bit of calibration seemed necessary. On the second day of use, though, I began to notice display artifacts on the right half of the screen: a pattern of closely spaced vertical lines that appeared in solid color areas such as window titles, solid areas in the background, etc. Just how much and where varies with which windows are in focus, and maybe other things. The effect is subtle, but once you notice it, obvious and wrong. I contacted Amazon and they arranged to ship a replacement right away. Great, I thought: wonderful customer service (and I have no reason to retract that). But when the replacement arrived, it was DOA: wouldn't do anything when powered on. So now I had two of them to return, and since the second one had come in a box that was too big and that therefore got destroyed in shipment, I only had one carton in which to return them. I sent the DOA one back, and I'm still hoping to find appropriate material to send back the other defective one. But now Amazon is out of stock, so they can't send me another one (they have arranged a refund), and the only source they list wants a significant premium over list price and doesn't offer Prime shipping. Phooey. I really like this monitor, in theory, but it does not look as if LG is doing a great job producing it.

After reading the reviews for this monitor, I realized I had a gamble on my hands. At the price, the specs can't be beat, but would I get one from a good production line, or would I get a broken one from the lower QC line? Somehow my luck held out and I got a perfect monitor first try. No dead pixels, no other quality issues to speak of. The colors are tremendously sharp, the 4k makes it difficult to see any pixellation and the blacks are pretty deep for a monitor with no intentional blacking out. The view angle is also incredibly wide, with true color at extreme angles. Additionally, while I will be hard mounting this to my desk, I must say that the adjustability of the stand is quite lovely. This monitor only works without problems using DP connections on the monitor & the graphics card. So far I'm pleased with it, greater than 60 Hz would have been nice but I knew this before I bought it.

This monitor is amazing! Clear and high quality display. I went with the 17 inch version as a personal preference but have coworkers with the smaller version who also love it. It is super fast adjustment by the computer when you plug it in and haven’t had any problems with it recognizing the second display. The only thing that someone may consider bothersome is every once in a while, I’ll open a window on my main screen and the font will be smaller. Once I move that window from one screen to another or minimize then restore it, it is fixed. This is a rare occurrence and may just be my computer as well. Overall, I would highly suggest this for people on the move a lot or even to use at home. Make sure it can fit in your bag, I have a laptop backpack for mine, but a smaller laptop briefcase-type bag may not fit this display.

I ended up buying a second to have matching dual screen.

Overall this is a good quality touchscreen monitor that is portable and works great plugging into any usb-c port on your laptop for the most part as I said the picture quality is excellent touch screen is very responsive although it does have one negative drawback it does not come with an included case which I consider a very negative point because storing this in your backpack without a case or some type of sleeve could very easily damage the screen via scratches or other damage much easier such as breakage other than that it's a very good screen I just wish they would have included a case or a sleeve to stored in.

I'm a graphic designer and needed a second monitor at home for work. I didn't have a huge budget, so I spent a lot of time looking through reviews of monitors around the same price, and this is the one I ended up getting.

It was shipped in the LG box, but luckily I was home when I arrived and it didn't sit outside for very long. Everything was packed nicely in the box and there was no damage to the box or the monitor.

It came with an HDMI cable, which was a nice surprise but I had already bought one.

It also came with a stand but I planned on mounting it onto an arm attached to my desk. The monitor is lightweight so mounting it was not difficult at all. It fits the standard 75mm mount so finding one I could use with it was not difficult, and it fit perfectly.

It has a button on the bottom to access settings, where you can change the brightness, etc.

There are no speakers in the monitor itself, so you'll need speakers or Bluetooth headphones if you don't already have them.

I primarily do graphic design with this monitor, but I also play video games, and the picture is good for both.

Overall I'm very pleased with this purchase. Having dual monitors has increased my productivity at work, and has also made doing graphic design easier, in that I can see things better. If you're looking for a decent monitor but don't want to break the bank, get this one.

UPDATE 12/14/20

I couldn't deal with the backlight bleed. It got worse over time and I just wanted something else. The LG Ultragear 27GN850-B is about $70-120 more expensive but it's considerably better and I will be keeping that one. Just a heads up.

This monitor's refresh rate and response time are a massive improvement over the old monitor and I greatly appreciate that. The animations are incredibly fluid when paired with my laptop and video from Netflix, Youtube, etc. look amazing. I have zero complaints there. The screen can also get quite bright. I am typing this on the screen right now at 60% brightness in a brightly lit room and it is almost too bright. The build quality is top-notch and I would not expect anything less from Dell. It does not feel cheap whatsoever. The stand is a blend of metals and plastics and has a great weight to it.

However, I have one minor gripe with the monitor. After setting it up, messing around with settings, and watching some 1440p video, I noticed it has one dead pixel. Thankfully, it is not in the central area of the screen and is in the bottom-left area of the screen close to the bezel. I reached out to Amazon Support and I have until 1/31/21 to return it. If more dead pixels start appearing, I will be returning it. I may or may not repurchase it, I cannot really make a call on that yet since I have only used it for a day. But for me, with video editing aspirations, and many intense programs including photoshop, the CJ89 43" is absolutely necessary to my sanity. The picture quality is crisp and stunning, and I have noted no unevenness and almost no vignetting in the corners. As a monitor, this is a superb achievement. I am NOT a gamer, but if any monitor could turn me into one, this would be it.

The only thing that troubles me is the very annoying on-off switch, which is located under the monitor and cumbersome to use and get at. It's not only an on-off button but it is also a "toggle-type" tiny switch. Turning the monitor power off requires three cumbersome finger movements. There are only three other buttons underneath the unit to access the controls, such as splitting the screen into two computers. I liked Samsung's old-style on-off switch much better.

The monitor has its own speakers, which, if you don't care too much about high-fidelity, are extremely convenient.

Let me proclaim loud and clear -- Samsung monitors are the world's best! But why this crazy on-off switch?

Update - I've had the Asus LCD for 18 months now. The last time I tried to use this, it did not work at all. After a dedicate hour trying to figure this out I followed these steps to make it work with Mac OS High Sierra 10.13.6 on my MacBook 11" (bought early 2014):

1-Search for the driver version 4.3 (for MB 168), download it.
2-Install the software/driver from the dmg (just start dbl clicking until the disk image opens, dbl click it, then able click the install icon)
3-Open Mac settings. Click Security and Privacy, click Accessibility on the left. Give the DisplayLinkUserAgent permissions (have to click the lock at the lower left to unlock for changes - note - having dropbox also enabled may spoil the part, and dropbox works without it).
4-Restart your Mac
5-HERE IS THE KICKER - when you plug in the USB cable for the MB168 ALSO PLUG IN A THUNDERBOLT ADAPTER. You don't need anything connected to the other end, just a VGA or HDMI thunderbolt display adapter clues the Mac in that something is going on with displays, and it finds the MB168. After the LCD monitor comes on you can plug the thunderbolt adapter. Weird.

It's hard wrapping hardware and software around someone else's product because product software constantly changes; my company did this with an add-on to Outlook, and it is a support nightmare. I applaud Asus for making this work, and wish the Mac would still detect the LCD monitor without the dongle wake up call regardless of who's "fault" this is. At least it works.

ORIGINAL REVIEW:

Very useful monitor. I carry it in my breifcase for off site work or meetings. The brightness buttons do not seem to work with my Mac, although some have reported that they do for ther Macs. I installed the drivers from the web site (I Googled it) and plugged it into my Macbook Air 11. I recommend this product.

The stand/cover could work better if the folded configuration stuck together more securely, but it works. That and the brightness buttons would make it a 5/5. I still recommend it, and would buy it again.

The display has a subtle flicker. I have tested on Macbook Pro (DVI), Window 7 PC with high end graphics card (HDMI) and Acer chrombook HDMI. The flicker is not blatantly obvious but my entire family starts to get dizzy after looking at this monitor for more than 1 minute. I have used many 24" ViewSonic monitors in the past. I currently use two at work and one in my home office. All of these monitors are easy on my eyes. But... not this one. Could this be just a defective unit? Sure. But I have no way of knowing that and I do not have the time or patience to get this one replaced with the same model to investigate further. I chose to send this one back defective and purchase an equivalent from another manufacturer. This is really a shame because I will now think twice (as I just did) before purchasing another ViewSonic monitor.

I am totally in love with this portable monitor. I used this to play my Nintendo switch while my father is watching television. It is super lightweight and very easy to carry with. I can easily bring this to school and it is super easy to set up. It came with a nice leather protective case which also serve as monitor stand. I am very happy with my purchase.

This was a good buy -- BUT there is a Major Glitch. I use this as an external VGA & HDMI monitor on my two laptop computers. I changed my Windows display settings to make it my 'primary' monitor. Nevertheless -- when I boot-up these laptops they do not recognize the monitor in the early stages of the boot, so I cannot use this monitor if I need to access a "safe boot" option or if I need to access the F-8 bios settings -- because this monitor can't see anything at the DOS level. It displays an "out of range" error message. LG support is both clueless and utterly indifferent about the problem.

Worth pointing out -- This is NOT a "bios settings" problem. Both of my prior ASUS 21" monitors that were connected in exactly the same way (as external monitors) are able to view all stages of the boot-up. The problem is that LG needs to reconfigure the monitor to automatically and temporarily adjust the settings as needed to display everything during the early stages of the boot-up (when in the DOS boot phase).

LG support (phone clerks) is so profoundly clueless and callously indifferent that they likely will never correct this problem -- which probably has a fairly simple, easy solution -- if only they cared.

At a price of $700 it surely is the best value in its class with a wide range of connections for just about anything and not having DVI is not an issue since it comes with a DVI to HDMI adapter. That is a definite strength. It's 27.5 size is large enough for bedroom HDTV viewing. It is also ridiculously bright. Too bright is never a bad thing. Not bright enough is the kiss of death for a monitor. That is very hard to compensate for. As far as I'm concerned an overly bright monitor is just fine. You will have to tone down the brightness all the way and adjust the gamma settings in either your video card control panel or in each game where applicable for the best black levels. With tweaking this is a very good gaming monitor.

On the negative side, do not expect to be able to use the preset color controls such as "Cinema". I discovered this while watching HDTV broadcasts via the component video input. Cinema mode offers tremendous color that looks so great for sports but god awful for most movies. I think it is funny that they even call it Cinema mode because you will most certainly not be able to see any shadow detail thus making movies impossible to enjoy. So be prepared to use only your custom settings for all sources and generally I recommend turning the brightness all the way down. This is not a monitor for people who want great results right out of the box. Be prepared to tweak.

Another quirk is that the viewing angle is a bit narrow. I can see that as I move from side to side even just a little bit causes some apparent lightening or darkening of the edges of the screen on each side. That is not a big deal for a PC Monitor but if you want it to double as a 1080p HDTV it will be somewhat unforgiving unless you sit right in front of the monitor. While other reviewers might think the processing is poor for 1080i broadcasts I found the picture quality to be very worthy of watching especially for anything like CGI or cartoons. Now the monitor did struggle with Monsters HD, an all horror movie channel, but that is to be expected for any LCD with just an 800:1 contrast ratio. Horror movies are very dependant on black levels so that is to be expected.

Of note, I wanted to mention that this LCD monitor is a 6 bit panel meaning that it will not offer the color accuracy of the 8 bit panels like Dell and others have in 24" sizes. Yes, the 24" monitors are using higher quality panels. I can say first hand that the choice is yours. I have one of those Dell 24" monitors and I can say that I think that the color accuracy is close enough be offset by the fact that the extra 3.5" of viewable area more than makes up for it. That is why my main gaming rig is viewed on the Viewsonic VX2835wm. From several feet away I can read this review at 1920 * 1200 without squinting. Oh and the 3 millisecond response time aint too shabby.

This monitor has almost everything going for it. It turns on quickly, doesn't need to warm up, holds a spot on color profile (once calibrated I got slightly better than sRGB gamut), has a very fine brightness control, looks great when viewed at all angles, is simple (I don't like OSDs), and has an unobtrusive bezel. The monitor arrived with only one stuck red pixel (which due to the small pixel pitch, can't be seen unless your a only couple inches away).

However, HP really ruined a good thing by adding such an obnoxious anti-glare coating. I do a lot of text editing and internet browsing (with white backgrounds) and the coating provides a rainbow graininess that causes text to not appear as crisp and solid colors to not seem clean. I suspect that HP decided to use the same anti-glare coating they use on lower resolution monitors (where the scattering of light from each pixel isn't a problem due to their size). With the tiny pixels in this display, the anti-glare just makes everything seem dirty and fuzzy. It's a real shame that they went overboard with the coating.

After some research, it appears that other manufacturers have the same anti-glare grainy problem with their high resolution displays, and the pickings are slim for those of us who want that kind of resolution in an IPS panel without spending the extra money on an Apple cinema display, as well as the necessary adapters to make it work with a PC and allow it to attach to a VESA desk mount.

The only way I found to mitigate the anti-glare issue was to apply an anti-reflective (different from anti-glare) screen protector (MXA sheet from Photodon). While it doesn't completely take out the sparkle, it makes it less noticeable (at least text is clearer now). Aside from a bit of dust I wasn't able to keep out when applying the sheet, installing it was not too bad of a price to pay. The blacks are now blacker, solid colors are cleaner, and the text is finally sharp. I only wish the monitor came this way.

If not for the aggressive anti-glare coating, the monitor would have gotten 5 stars. It would be nice to see a glossy option from HP in the future.

If you have a Macbook and you want an external display, skip this. (I assume this is true if you have a Mac mini as well) I bought this for my relatively new macbook pro 2019, and I'm returning it.
The monitor resolution, which is 4K (3840 x 2160 pixels) on a 27", is not suited for apple computers. Mac OS works well within the 200 dpi range, the so called RETINA. To have this on a 27'', you need a 5k (5120 × 2880) screen, OR just need to buy a 24'' monitor with a 4k resolution . The 4k resolution is too low to be stretched on a 27'' panel, to work well with Mac Os.
If you use this monitor with its native 4K resolution (the "more space" option in the Mac settings), this monitor doesn't have enough pixels for not a RETINA resolution on a 27'' (219 ppi) , and everything on the screen will be reeeeaaally small. The other option is to use the monitor at 1080p, and that's a "Retina" option , this happens to be the "default" in the settings, for this very reason. This way everything is clear but too big, and you lose the advantage of a big screen, real estate.
You can choose a different resolution, such as 2560x1440, so you have a compromise between text size and real estate, but there is a huge problem: the monitor doesn't really use its full 4K potential, so you wasted your money on a 4K monitor, and you have to force your graphics card to do scaling. Scaling means your graphic card has to scale everything for the size you picked, and that results in blurry text, and not so smooth scrolling.

So to summarize: if you purchased this monitor and you are using it with an Apple computer, you have 3 options:
1) use the native 4K resolution and you will take advantage of the max potential of your screen but everything will be super small and somewhat clear, but not super clear.
2) use the default retina setting, which is 1080p, and everything will be super extra crisp and smooth, but you are underusing your monitor, everything will be super big, and you'll waste a lot of real estate.
3) use an in-between resolution, and you will gain some real estate and the text on the screen will be readable, but it's not gonna be crisp and super clear, and it's not gonna be smoot. Something will look "wrong".

Bottom line, if you have a Mac, buy a 24'' 4K monitor , or a 27'' 5K monitor. DON'T buy this one

I bought three of these monitors at the same time, as well as 3 DisplayPort 1.2 cables (I TRIPLE checked this because of how I wanted to configure the system). My setup is somewhat unique, so this review is primarily for those that want to have a single displayport 1.2 cable driving all three monitors in a daisy chain setup. The idea is relatively simple: Your graphics card output going into monitor 1 input. Then you connect a cable on monitor 1's displayport OUTPUT to monitor 2's displayport input. Repeat for up to a total of FOUR monitors. Sounds like an easy way to rid yourself of wires and have a clean setup right? Well, here's my experience.

Some background. This monitors are revision A01, my graphics card is a Gigabyte 7870. It has two mini DPs, a DVI out and am HDMI out. I used the supplied adapter from the graphics card for a mini DP to full sized DP, then hooked in the Cables Matters Displayport 1.2 cable to my first monitor. I daisy chained the other two according to Dell's instructions. BY DEFAULT the Dell's DisplayPort 1.2 capability is OFF. With it off you will definitely get no signal in a daisy chain configuration, from any of the monitors. To fix this, on monitor 1 (the monitor with the direct input from the graphics card) touch the menu button directly to the left of the power button. Make sure the monitor registers your touch- you will know if it does because the monitor will display a message saying it has no signal and you can select a different input by clicking the menu button. Once the menu comes up with different inputs, make sure the DisplayPort option is selected and HOLD the green checkmark button for approximately 8 seconds. A menu will come up asking if you would like to switch to DisplayPort 1.2. Make sure enable is selected and click the green checkmark. Repeat these steps on each monitor EXCEPT the last monitor in your DisplayPort daisy chain configuration. This one should stay in DP 1.1a mode.

Alright! So now everything is set up correctly and it should be smooth sailing from here on out right? Well.. not in my case. I experience a plethora of issues: one of more of the monitors will flicker, one or more of the monitors not coming on at ALL, one of the monitors will randomly shut off while the other two work properly and wont reinitialize. Essentially what it feels like to me is that the monitors aren't able to adequately send the signals to each other. I can't tell if this is a throughput issue, some type of handshake issue, graphics card drivers issue, etc. It's somewhat confusing honestly; in my experience all three monitors will initialize and display (albeit in a mirror mode NOT an extended mode) before Windows loads. My theory is that post-Windows, the signal sent to the monitors is displayport 1.1. The monitors don't seem to have a problem sharing a displayport 1.1 signal and displaying in mirror mode, but what's the point of that? That's not why people are getting three monitors! We want monitor extension, eyefinity, etc. When Windows loads, I THINK that the graphics card switches to DP 1.2 mode. So it appears like its a Windows issue or drivers issue but I think its a DP 1.2 issue. This could be two things. A cable/throughput issues or a monitor issue. AMD software has a displayport troubleshooting program, and when I can get all three monitors to actually work and display, the displayport program shows a total bandwidth of 20gb/s and each monitor is taking approximately 4.5 gb/s. So that says to me that its not throughput.. which leads to the only thing left.. the monitors themselves.

I believe these monitors have a serious issue with DP, specifically 1.2. I think DP 1.1a works fine.. but if you want to daisy chain you are REQUIRED to use DP 1.2 because of the throughput limitation of 1.1a. In this monitor's current state, I would not recommend this type of configuration. You can still use the HDMI inputs or the mini or full-sized DP (make sure 1.2 is turned off) and everything should work fine.. but for the sake of this review, I wanted to speak directly to the issue of DP 1.2 and daisy chaining.

I will update my review after I get my setup hooked up via HDMI for all three monitors. My plan is to use two Mini DP to HDMI adapters and hook two monitors up using that configuration, and my third monitor will simply be an HDMI connection from my graphics card to monitor.

So should you be concerned that this monitor doesn't really function properly in DP 1.2 mode? Only if you MUST run a daisy chain configuration. The main difference between DP 1.1a and 1.2 is throughput. You need that extra throughput to send enough signal through the cable to allow all three monitors to get a "chunk" of data. 1.1a cant push enough data through the cable, so you can't daisy chain. DP1.2 is also needed for high resolutions, for the same throughput reasoning. More data, more throughput required. The dell is a 1080P monitor so that's not an issue.

I've had this monitor for about a month and wanted to review it for others who may want to use it on a new Mac Mini M1. I'm using the default HDMI connections and I have made a number adjustments which have resulted in a very nice user experience.  Resolution is set to 2560x1440 @ 75Hz.  Here are my settings: In the Mac OS Big Sur System Preferences, Accessibility - Reduce Transparency and Large Menu Bar Size.  In the Finder Desktop preferences, I changed the default text size to 12 pt. . I also adjusted the icon size and text size in each Finder window that opens - Large Icon and 14 point finder font size.  This has worked out very well.  For under $300, this is a very usable Mac monitor.  Everything looks sharp, especially when compared to a 1080p monitor.  If you want to upgrade to a 4k monitor, you'll spend at least $350 to $500.  The main difference, as I see it, between this WQHD monitor and a 4k monitor is pixels per inch.  This monitor delivers 108 ppi and most 4k monitors are in the 163 ppi range.  This will make a difference at the same 2560x1440 resolution but if you only have $300 to spend, this monitor will work for you with the settings I've outlined.

Short upshot:
Compact, good viewing angle, touch and smart case mounting work. Is is too dim, blurs badly, colors and contrasts are meh, bad audio and too low-volume.

My impression:
It's no good for gaming, watching movies, viewing graphics and NOT a good idea for public presentations involving still or moving pictures or audio.

Fine for office applications, browsing on-the-go. Ok as well for any secondary settings involving smart phones (monitor, touch and audio all connect via USB C, but the monitors internal battery will discharge).

You should consider buying this, but really only as a secondary or emergency office option. I got it for $220 and think for what it is, that's above what I should have paid. May be the best option if you require touch, though!

Details:
Audio is sorry quality and speakers too low-volume (way too low for use in public presentations or even movie watching) - you can expect more from any upscale mobile phone or $25 boom-box. Brightness is too low to use in daylight conditions (indoors but monitor shaded, window blinds open on a sunny day) and running it at full setting typically means the battery discharges despite both USB (attach to PC/laptop for touch support) and HDMI dual cables in use. Also, motion blur is evil, colors are rather dull, blacks and contrast are meh.

That said, it's compact, it works silently, has a pleasantly wide viewing angle and multi-touch works well enough.

To change settings, there's a joystick-like five-way nibble at the bottom left that takes a lot of pushing to get you where you need to go (like, oh, change brightness or volume)... works, but just not practical for frequent use. Bring back buttons!

Once you get the hang, it's done quickly and works fine horizontally and vertically -- as long as you have a stable surface (not your bed), no kids/cats/cable-stumblers. You should reconsider other mountable options if you have a history of neck issues and plan to use this frequently.

Cables are on the short side, plugs are bulky, consider investing in 180° versions online. Micro-HDMI is what you want, not mini or standard-sized HDMI. Haven't used the pen, can't comment.

These Monitors are fantastic. The IPS glow is very minor and a little angling makes a huge difference. That being said, it is important to understand the difference between IPS glow and Back light bleed, more commonly known as BLB, as they are two different things, with two completely different causes.

Most if not all of the one star reviews on this product complain about the same thing, Back light bleed. Some are very obviously back light bleed, others not so much. So I will explain the difference and how to tell if your monitor is defective with BLB, or is simply just IPS glow. Most people deal with TN panels, in fact almost all commonly used electronics use TN panels, as VA and IPS panels are far more expensive with some notable drawbacks. That being said, if you are coming from a TN panel, do know it is going to look different, and you may feel like your product is defective when it truly isn't.

IPS Glow is due to the angle of the pixel relative to your eyes. This is why the glow will move when you view it at different angles. If you sit in front of the monitor at a certain distance with your eyes even with the top portion of your screen, you will notice more IPS glow in the bottom and bottom corners of your screen. This is not a defect, this is normal operation of an IPS panel. Remedies for this situation is to raise the monitor, lower your eyes, sit further back, tweak settings in the monitor itself, etc.

BLB is a defect in the panel itself where the back light (the light behind the panel) bleeds out from a defect due to poor craftsmanship, damage, poor materials, etc. Bleed WILL NOT MOVE with your perspective because it is a defect from a fixed point. With that being said, If you have BLB, you should return your monitor, and get a new one.

People often confuse BLB and IPS Glow as they both sound like the same thing and not very many people come into contact with IPS panels often. However, they are not, and you shouldn't worry about a minor glow that moves with you. You should understand that monitors don't function the same at certain viewing angles because TN panels are notorious for not being able to see anything correctly at sharp angles. IPS panels do not have that issue. You will be able to see your image with perfect clarity from any angle you so desire with perhaps a minor bit of IPS glow.

There are plenty of websites that allow you to do a full monitor test to make sure your monitor is properly made. You can also use them to tune your color, brightness, etc.

After doing some research I decided to buy this model Dell Monitor, U2518D. The monitor is supposed to have a very accurate RGB color reproduction which is great for my photography. Also, the higher resolution of 1440p is a really nice step up from 1080, especially at this size.

I received the monitor in a Dell box that seems to have been previously opened, as the box was taped with packing tape. In some areas, the packing tape had already been cut open. This had me very concerned as the product was listed new on Amazon.

However after opening the package, the monitor, and all of its accessories, including the stand, mDP cable, power cable, and USB cable were all included. The monitor even had the original shipping foam and plastic wraps over the display. I am not sure why the package had been opened already, but it seems the monitor hadn't been used. This is the sole reason I left off a star.

Colors on this monitor are bright, and crisp. Definitely not over saturated. The "Standard" mode out of the box is pretty bright. I turned it down to about 50 and it seems much more reasonable. The colors are still very accurate and this display does have stunning contrast. However like some other reviewers have pointed out, on an all black screen, there is some noticeable light bleed from the panel. It really isn't any worse that most other IPS panels.

This is not a gaming monitor after all and for the color accuracy I don't mind this. I am not often staring at a black screen anyway.

Hope this review helps someone.

I wanted to share my experience as others may run into the same issues. I learned several things that may help others:
1) This monitor has two different ratings for the two HDMI ports. HDMI #2 is better as it supports HDMI 2.0. HDMI #1 supports HDMI 1.4. See the manual for more information on this.
2) The Intel 620 display adapter in my laptop does not support 3440x1440 @ 75 Hz over HDMI, but it does support it over DisplayPort.
3) The Dell Latitude 5490 laptop I am using has that has the Intel HD 620 integrated GPU only has an HDMI port, not a DisplayPort. However, this laptop does not have what Dell calls a DisplayPort over USB C port. I purchased a UniAccessories USB C to DisplayPort Cable, and after I connect the monitor with that cable, I was able to get the 75 Hz refresh rate.
4) Download the display drivers from the Samsung website. I had installed these previously, but after I disconnected the HDMI cable and connected the DisplayPort cable, a new display appeared in the Device Manager in Windows 10, which was using generic display drivers. I had to open the DisplayPort version of this monitor, select Update Drivers, and then direct Windows 10 to search the correct folder for the drivers from the Samsung website. Windows installed the Samsung drivers and I could immediately see the colors were much brighter.

[[VIDEOID:67b2652aac5e3f1991e80f10c09dee11]] This monitor came in an oversized box, but nicely and securely packaged. Looking at the box, I wondered if it was really a 27” monitor or if they shipped me a bigger monitor / TV. It comes with cables (DisplayPort, HDMI & USB-C), a huge power adapter with good length cords, a well written user manual and color calibration report.

Assembling the stand to the monitor took just a couple of minutes and did not require any tool. The stand is well made and sturdy. The stand has an opening through which you can pass the cables. The monitor is thick (~2.5”), almost twice thicker than my few years old 27” HP monitor. With the stand it weighs ~6.6 kg (~14.5 lbs).

After powering it on, I selected the language and was good to go. The menu system is easy to navigate and easy to update the settings. For now, I am happy with the factory calibration, I may tweak it in the future as needed. I love how bright it can get. My desk is next to a window, with the monitor facing the sunlight in the AM. The brightness is so great that I can easily see or read the content on the screen. Not much information in the manual about the mini LED. The only reference I found was in the cover page, elsewhere, the product is referred to as an LCD monitor including the spec sheet.

I wish the monitor had USB-A ports, most monitors at this price point have them. I own a couple of 24” HP EliteDisplay monitors, they have a couple of USB-A ports which I use. The USB-C port is great, when connected to my 2016 & 2020 MacBook Pro, it charges the battery as well. This monitor has all the tilt, rotate, height adjustment features normally seen in a top end monitor. My EliteDisplay monitors do not have the rotate feature, but this one has which I find really useful to slideshow images to my family when they are seated close by.

I am impressed with the brightness and sharpness of the monitor. The LED display is wonderful, I can see great details displayed. I would say it is at least 3-4 times sharper than the 24” & 27” HP monitors I own. The wide range of the color gamut meets my needs. This is very helpful when I edit my images using Lightroom and/or Photoshop. I watched a 4K video on this monitor and on the other 27” side by side, I could not believe how vibrant the colors are displayed on this new monitor. But of course, this monitor costs twice the 24” and 4-5 times more than my 27” monitor.

Auto brightness is turned off by default. I did enable it and had to turn it off after a few minutes as it was changing the brightness almost every second or two. Watch the google search page at the end of the video showing the brightness changing quite often when auto brightness is on.

I use this monitor with my work HP EliteBook (Win 10), personal Lenovo IdeaPad (Win 11) gaming laptop and 2020 MackBook Pro. The application icons look super sharp but a bit saturated too. On the HP laptop, I had to scale the text size from 150% recommended to 175%. The text is too small for me at 150%. It takes around 10 seconds for the monitor to wake up from sleep or to switch input source.

The monitor comes with a one-year warranty. I am not a gamer, so not sure if this would be a good fit for gaming with 60 Hz refresh rate. This is a great monitor to own for photo/video editors if you can afford it.

This is a first impression, which I intend to update as I gain more time using the product. I don't normally post reviews this quickly, but now that I have the product I can answer some pre-sales questions for others that were not clear to me from the product page.

I am a computer engineer using a 17" mobile workstation for 3D interactive development (rendering, simulation, visualization, etc.) as well as software development and occasional video editing. Two screens are almost essential for the kind of work I do, and these tools work much better if all screens have the same native resolution. Since my primary display is 4K, I wanted to upgrade my portable monitor to match this.

Some clarification of technical specs, based on questions I had before ordering:

1. If using the USB-C port for combined power and data, you do NOT necessarily need a separate power supply (connected to the USB-C "power only" port on the monitor), BUT this depends on how much current your host system can supply. On my mobile workstation, this monitor works perfectly with just one USB-C cable connected for power and data, even with the brightness at 8 or above. Presumably, the manufacturer's comments about needing a separate power connection at higher brightness refer to using this product with a cell phone, tablet, or smaller laptop that can't supply enough USB-C power. Check the manufacturer's specifications for your system to see how much USB-C power it can deliver.

2. By default, the brightness level is reset to minimum every time the monitor is disconnected or powered down, but there is a menu option to "save brightness". If this is set to ON, the monitor remembers your brightness preference between uses. They probably default to setting it at minimum to prevent it from failing to work if it's moved between devices with different USB-C capabilities.

3. The HDMI port is full-sized, not mini, and it *does* work at full 4K if your computer meets the required HDMI specification level. If the HDMI port and the USB-C data port are connected simultaneously to the same system, Windows (and maybe other o.s., but I haven't tested yet) will detect this (incorrectly) as two different monitors, or at least that's what happened for me. To use the HDMI port for data and USB-C for power, move the USB-C cable to the "power only" port on the monitor.

INITIAL REVIEW AND COMMENTS

* The build quality is high, but lightweight. Components are fit and finished with precision, but the enclosure is thin and light. It's the usual tradeoff between ruggedness and carry weight. So far I think they have struck a good compromise here, but this is not "built like a Sherman tank". Connectors and buttons seem to be typical for mobile devices. I would recommend carrying this monitor in a rigid case, or placing it alongside the laptop or tablet in a backpack or bag, to avoid flexing. Its thin profile is fantastic for mobility but inherently easier to bend -- and flexing or bending is very bad for display panels and the circuit boards that control them.

* The USB-C and HDMI ports have a nice, crisp contact feel when they are connected, which suggests good retention properties and provides a tactile indication of the cable's connector being fully seated.

* The folding protective sleeve is made of a durable-feeling leather-like material and should hold up well in daily use. The edges will probably experience some cosmetic wear effects over time, but I don't see anything here that concerns me about functional durability.

* My system (a Dell Precision mobile workstation with NVidia GPU) instantly recognized this monitor at full 4K native resolution. No drivers were needed under Windows 10 Pro. I'll be testing with Linux on the next couple of weeks, but I do not expect any issues. It "Just Works".

* The color quality and brightness exceeded my expectations. I have a hardware colorimeter but haven't calibrated this monitor yet. Comparing it visually against the calibrated (and excellent) primary display, I would say they are closely matched in color tone. Under cool LED office lighting, I run my main display at about 70%, and the Cocopar can visually match this with a setting of 80%, so there would be a decent amount of "headroom" for someone who prefers a brighter screen or is working in high ambient light (but probably not in direct sunlight).

* The VESA mounting holes (75mm spacing) are threaded into the metal shell of the monitor, but there are no corresponding holes in the folding cover, so it is necessary to remove the cover before VESA mounting the monitor (or drill the VESA spaced holes yourself).

* The cover attaches magnetically, and powerfully so. It almost feels as if the cover is glued in place, but it's actually quite removable. Don't store it next to a payment card with a magnetic stripe, or near your collection of vintage music tapes or floppy disks.

* It's not relevant to product quality, but the attention to detail Cocopar put into their package design gives a very good first impression an suggests (hopefully accurately) an attention to quality in the product itself. The monitor arrived in a lift-top box of heavy card stock, padded by custom-fit closed cell foam inside, and wrapped in a plastic film liner.

* I found the setup menus to be easy to figure out but slightly cumbersome to use because of the close spacing of the tiny buttons. Fortunately, this is a minor issue for most people who aren't constantly fiddling with the settings.

* The package includes two USB-C cables, a small plugin USB-C charger, and an HDMI cable. It doesn't seem to have a Mini DisplayPort cable, probably because there are so many options for what people might need at the other end of that one. There is also a USB to USB-C cable that is clearly labeled "power only" (no video).

SUMMARY

So far, I'm favorably impressed and look forward to using this product. It was expensive, but for my professional requirements the variety of input ports and the display fidelity justify the cost. Assuming its durability holds up under daily use, this was a good choice.

The monitor works fine; multi-touch was the issue. On a standalone kiosk, multi-touch is the kiss of death, because an errant finger can make the display zoom out to the point where it is unusable by the average person who doesn't know about multi-touch. There is no way to disable multi-touch in Windows 10 because this device uses built-in drivers and Micro-shaft forgot to include the capability to manage multi-touch that has been there in Windows 7 and Windows 8.

Anyway, after an extensive search and hair pulling, I found the solution: a Group Policy edit that disables multi-touch. This means that only Windows 10 Pro or Enterprise can be fixed. For anybody else out there facing this problem, the way to disable multi-touch in Windows 10 is to run gpedit, and find the setting ADMINISTRATIVE TEMPLATES--> WINDOWS COMPONENTS--> INTERNET EXPLORER--> "TURN OFF PAGE-ZOOMING FUNCTIONALITY" and set that to Enabled. Yeah, it says Internet Explorer, but it works for the regular desktop, too.

I have a LOT of incredible apps on my phone that I stream a TON of movies and TV Shows on. I bought the lightning to HDMI adapter and have been using the screen quite often for this. In addition to streaming movies and TV shows from my phone onto the screen, I've also played games from my phone on the screen. Unsurprisingly, they look beautiful. It's exponentially nicer to play phone games on a large screen. The ability to see larger things is a huge asset.

Overall, I am extremely happy with this purchase and would absolutely recommend this screen to anybody that's considering buying it. You will not be disappointed!

Very nice monitor for general usage and artwork. I liked it so much I immediately bought a 2nd one. So many reasons to like it, and only one small flaw (see below).

Pros:

- solid build quality. Every detail from the base, the screen, the bezel, the rear plate seems well-designed and well-made. The materials look and feel a cut above.
- base is very stable, and screen position is fully adjustable (height, tilt, swivel, rotate)
- VESA compatible
- many presets and fine adjustments for image/color quality
- 3 inputs (DisplayPort, HDMI, VGA)
- built-in speakers. No one should ever plan to use their monitor speakers as their main sound system, but it's nice in a pinch if you need them. These completely lack any bass, but otherwise sound surprisingly good.
- 5 physical control buttons for the onscreen menu + power button are easy to find and press.
- low price

Con:
- there is only one glaring flaw in these monitors: it takes no less than eight (8) button presses to switch the input. Way too many! What's more ridiculous is it has the ability to assign shortcut functions to 2 of the buttons, but switching inputs is not something can be assigned to them. If it could, that would be perfect. (the functions that can be assigned to the shortcuts are generally useless.) Luckily, I don't plan on switching inputs that often, but if you did that a lot it could be very annoying.

Despite that one flaw in switching inputs, I still think this a 5-star quality monitor, especially given the price point. (under $200) Highly recommend.

I am used to having two 4K flat monitors for work but decided to switch to a curved single monitor for WFH. When I got this monitor, I was pleasantly surprised by how nice it looks: back surface looks very premium and feels good to the touch. However, after plugging it, I was quite disappointed by heavily pixelated picture. If you are used to 4K monitors with 160+ ppi, going back to this monitor is tough. My eyes were hurting for a day or two. I decided to give it a chance for a week and see if I still want to exchange it. Here are a few highlights about this monitor:

Good:
- being ultra-wide and quite large (34"), I really liked the real estate. For programming, you can open 3 code windows at the same time. For typing some MS Word documents, it is a bit of an inconvenience though (vertical size is small).
- 100 Hz and fast response. I tried a few FPS games on it and it worked out quite well. It is not as smooth as 144 Hz gaming monitors but my GPU card is not too fast anyway and it could not give me more than 100 frames per second on native resolution anyway. For occasional gaming sessions, it works well.
- Thunderbolt connection (and charging). Being a PC and Mac user, I enjoyed possibility to use this monitor with a macbook and thunderbolt connection that can charge your laptop. It was a plug-and-play experience on Mac.
- Color reproduction and brightness: I am not a graphical designer but the colors look quite vivid and the monitor is bright.
- Adjustable and solid stand. You can move the monitor up and down and rotate it as much as you want. It feels solid and comfy.

Bad:
- Resolution is low for its size. I believe, it translates to around 110 PPI. I previously used 4K monitors with 160+ PPI and they feel retina-like from a typical working distance that I use. Even after using this monitor for a week, I could not get past a point of seeing everything heavily pixelated (maybe I get used to 4K and retina monitor on Macbook).
- I did not like the VESA mount - you can look at the pictures, it has a strange design.

In the end, I decided to return the monitor and get another Samsung model instead (32" Curved with 4K, LU32R590CWNXZA). Having two monitors side-by-side, it was a clear decision. The 32" does not feel as premium (cheap-looking plastic on the back, very basic stand that does not allow vertical adjustment, no Thunderbolt, 60 Hz only) but it has around 25 % higher PPI that looks quite close to Retina-like and to me, it was a very important decision point.

I still give this monitor a 4-star rating as for some people who do not care about PPI, they may really like this monitor.
It's a monitor that functions within expected parameters and has no apparent defects. I have no complaints as my expectations were met but not exceeded.

I bought this from a local shop and won the panel lottery: no dead pixels, no backlight bleed. The static image quality was great, contrast is out of this world, and it looked calibrated out of the factory, but I decided to return it for a numbers of reasons.

1) The entire screen flashes just a little bit at odd times if gsync is enabled. I got this to happen constantly whenever I pressed a button in an RPG. This was the deal breaker, everything else was so-so.

2) The HDR makes everything look dull, de-saturated and low contrast. I thought with hdr 600 it would be better than my other monitor with hdr 400, I was wrong.

3) The curve is too aggressive. It looks great whenever you're in a full 3d environment, but any full screen elements look very distorted. I think the curve would work great if you always kept your head in one place, front and center, but that would be very uncomfortable to maintain. I found my self wishing it was 1500r or 1800r, I feel like that's the sweet stop for monitor curve.

4) I noticed a checkerboard pattern on bright areas of fast moving scenes, giving everything a weird "video" effect. I think it has something to do with VA panels slow dark transitions interacting with the pixel inverter. (sorry for the techno-babel, I don't have the room to explain it here)

So anyway, it looks great on paper, but all the best features: Gsync, HDR, and the Curve are flawed in serious ways. I suppose it would still be usable if you just disabled gsync (240hz is fast enough without it.) but with all those other flaws, I found it disappointing.

Says it had display port, hdmi and thedvi.. Instead, this monitor has  Hdmi, VGA, and dvi. Came with an HDMI and VGA cable even though it said it would come with an HDMI and Display port cable. Lying to you. Lyign

The panel itself is nice if it weren't for the obnoxiously bad IPS glow. I'm having another one shipped to me as I write this, so I'll provide an update with an improved rating if this issue is resolved. Otherwise, I'll just return it all together.

Picture quality over HDMI is average, DisplayPort somewhat better. I was looking for a monitor with a 3-axis adjustment. This one fit the bill. The picture quality isn't as good as I had hoped, but for $150 I'm pretty happy as I'm comparing it next to monitors that are at least 2-3x as much. I would probably purchase it again as long as I intended to use it with a DisplayPort connection. Over HDMI it runs in 1080i.
This is a great monitor. It makes a slight wine noise - not too loud.

I bought this monitor since I will be working from home more often. Unfortunately, the screen kept flickering (not completely on and off, but between dim and bright). very happy with this item

I ordered this monitor but was sent the 21.5 inch one instead TWICE I would not recommend buying this monitor until the issue is resolved. I have had this monitor for a year and it runs amazingly 100% recomend but just hold off until the situation is fixed.

I thought all the modern monitors came with USB ports, not this one.  I should have looked closer and not assumed what I thought I knew.  Good monitor so far as clarity and such though.  Very competitive price.  ViewSonic pretty consistently makes good stuff based on my experience.
I love this monitor. 17" 2K? What? Attached to my laptop with simply a Type-C cable provided, and I am off. I can stream a video and get some work done with the same compact setup. Worth It.

I ordered this monitor as a second screen for my laptop.  (I use a Microsoft Surface Book.)  I did have to get an adapter for my surface book.  You cannot use a USB port on a surface book, you have to use the mini display port.  With an easy to find $20.00 adapter I was ready to go.  It instantly showed up in my display settings and adjusts between portrait and landscape very easily.  The screen is good quality and large enough that I can easily open and read two documents at a time.
Needed a cheap monitor for a recent build and this fit the bill perfectly. Cheap. Big. and Cheap. Highly recommend.

This monitor is the best for 4K gaming. Coming from a defective Pg27uq, I’ve come to realized that the display stream compression you get with this monitor is much better then chroma subsampling found on the $1500 Pg27uq. DSC offers 4K 144hz HDR while chroma only offers HDR at 98hz. This monitor was manufactured in 2020 meaning a better panel as well. Take note that in order to use DSC you need an ampere card (20 series). I love this monitor and it’s color accuracy, this was definitely the better choice.

I'm on my second of these monitors in a week. The first one had a stuck pixel in the middle of the screen, which was unbearable when looking at spreadsheets all day. The replacement unit seems just fine so far, but my experience with its predecessor makes me doubt its longevity.

Leaving aside Dell's questionable quality control, it's a lot of screen for the price. Viewing angles are great, the 1920x1200 resolution is crisp, and color rendition is excellent. At a low brightness setting (<10; I use 3), it's comfortable to look at for long periods, which is much nicer than aggressively lit screens that feel like staring into a flashlight. Even so, I'm uncomfortable recommending this given that the first one I got was a dud. On the other hand, that may have just been bad luck. At this price point, there's not much competition for IPS monitors with resolution beyond 1080, so no matter my experience, this probably belongs on your shortlist.
Great deal and it still works.
The monitor was severely damaged upon arrival, we had to return it at the UPS store.

My wife loves these 32" curved monitors for doing her work. We don't use them for gaming at all. My wife wears progressives if that helps anyone else make a decision.  She loves the big workspace. This is the second one of this brand although a different model than our privious one.  I like the stand better on this one as it is all medal and I like that the cords can run under the stand for clean cord managment.  There is a mood led light in the back that is red, so some people might not like that at all, but it doesn't affect us and doesn't bother us. Overall this is a huge montior at a great price.
Cant say much more than 5 starts. Inexpensive and just the right size to hook up a laptop to.
I already wrote it.. simply AMAZING!

I'm using this monitor with Ableton Live 10 and Cubase Pro 9.5. I wanted a 32" 1440p IPS panel. I've had it for about 2 hours and am absolutely loving it. No regrets. Running it with a GTX 1050 ti. No issues.

Update - four months later and it’s still working well. I’ve had no issues.

August 2018 - Monitor still fantastic.

Would be one star, but it worked reasonably well about 95% of the time for about 6 months.

Kept it in _two_ cases (the original aoc case inside a padded aftermarket case) as shown in the photo during transport - so I was at least able to avoid the easily-cracked screen many users report.

I was not, however, able to avoid the connector issues also widely reported.

I thought: "those schmucks just don't know how to take care of their equipment". Nope. It's just genuinely not made for daily gentle / careful insertion and removal of the included usb cable.

The connector inside is now loose, and all I get when plugging it into my mac is the screen shown in the first picture. I would get this occasionally before - solvable with a machine restart - but it is now persistent, making the monitor utterly unusable.

If you are going to get this in spite of the ubiquitous bad reviews because you really need it for work, you are traveling a lot, there aren't any better reasonably-priced options and you are willing to stomach the $h!@ quality on a ~$100 disposable monitor: I get it. I was you six months ago.

Here's what I suggest: get a few wire ties and superglue them to the back of the monitor close to the usb input port. Plug the USB cable into the monitor, and secure it tightly in place with the wire ties so that it doesn't move ever. Then, only do your plugging and unplugging on your machine, where someone competent has placed a USB port that was properly-designed to support thousands of uses.
good

Great budget touch screen. We are using two of these as a touch screens in our bar. One controls an a/v marix computer and another controls our streaming music, streaming video, and auxiliary video systems. Plug and play. Multitude of connections. And as always great customer service and fast shopping through Amazon.

Absolutely superb monitor, I used it for gaming as i play competitively. Just. Phenomenal. Amazing quality and even with a 1080p 27 inch, the clarity still is barely scratched. I can’t recommend more.
