Amazing monitor, again, but mine recently had an accident and i’m looking to upgrade to an LG850 Ultra Gear

update: 2 years and 9 months later. wishing i got another one of these as the 850 randomly starting giving me issues with turning on. very inconsistent and inevitably bit the dust.

I ordered the Viewsonic VX2450 for my son for christmas, to be used with his PS3. As usual with Amazon.com, the order arrived on time and well packaged. The first thing buyers need to be aware of is that even though this is a HD monitor, it does not come with an HDMI cable input. You will need an HDMI to DVI cable and a sound adapter to run a PS3 on this monitor. Once we set the monitor up and got it running, we noticed that two of the four control buttons on the front of the monitor were unresponsive. This made it impossible to adjust the sound or picture. The picture quality was outstanding, but without being able to get the sound going and adjust the picture brightness, etc., it was useless. We call Viewsonic tech support, and even though customer service was very friendly, they weren't able to resolve the issue and instructed us to return the product as defective.

Thanks to Amazon.com's great return service, were able to return this monitor (they even scheduled a UPS pickup for us) and got a replacement for it a day later - same brand and model. However, we had the same problem with the new monitor, only that with this one, THREE out of the four control buttons were non-responsive. Needless to say, we are returning the monitor for a refund and getting a different monitor from a brand other than Viewsonic. So one star for the product, five stars for amazon's outstanding return policy.
The picture and quality is good! But it came with a dead pixel :/
It’s a nice product especially considering the price. But please be aware it does not come with HDMI cable, if you do not have one, better order one together with this Monitor.

First panel had bad IPS glow in both lower corners + some bearable light bleed on top left. This seems to be an issue with the 29UM67 and the 34um67/95. Really unacceptable for a $500MSRP monitor. Ordered a replacement and it was even worse. Same issue, IPS glow in lower corners and light bleed in same spot. Definitely should not have passed QC. I'm assuming they just push them through and hope you'll live with it. Because this is a panel defect, especially if its occurring in the same spots. Or perhaps they ship all the good panels for full price sales, then drop the price once they ship shoddy panels.

I really want to keep this monitor, but playing games like WoW or any game with a chat box you notice the glow. It makes the text look metallic and hard to read. Plus it starts to hurt your eyes. It also was making any water that appeared in the corners turn a faint purple. Colors are vibrant and accurate (as long as its not near the lower corners). The 75FPS is a noticeable difference to 60FPS. And having the ability to browse the web and watch a video is nice on one screen, especially if your desk has limited real-estate and cant fit 2 normal 1080p monitors.

I might try my luck with a third replacement. But I guess I'll just have to wait till another freesync ultra wide comes out.

To anyone thinking of still getting this, if your video card is flashed with a custom bios, make sure the bios supports 2560x1080 because my 7950s F43 bios didn't. I had to revert to stock bios.

This was my first monitor I ever spent over 300 on. My first LCD panel was a Acer 22" an it cost my 260 in 2008. Had no light bleed and no dead pixels but was a TN panel. The monitor before this LG was a standard Asus V-series 23" 1080p, with light bleed but I paid only 100 for it so I accepted the defects. This however, is WORSE then the Asus TN panel and cost over 3x as much, nope not happening.

Minor Update: After doing some more delving is appears people reporting bad backlight bleed/ IPS glow on the 29/34um67 and um 95 panels are getting panels from China manufactured around 2015. Panels made in 2014 were made in Korea and dont have and panel issues. SO most likely LG shifted production to china to save money after they got stellar reviews. We've seen this in the power supply markets and changing out components for cheap ones to save money but not changing model numbers.

Highly disappointed. I got the monitors after hearing high reviews from multiple sources (to be fair most of them were PC Based) and got them for my baseline 2017 Imac 27 inch.

Now in all fairness, i knew these monitors would not be 5k nor was i expecting them to match the resolution, but the colors were way off and even on full brightness they couldent match the brightness of my Imac on half brightness.

I spent the better part of two days trying to dial the settings in, and in every way shape or form my Imac Monitor was vastly superior in both terms of color, brightness and resolution.

Switching any media or apps from the Imac to the monitors (i purchased two of them) was an instant visual downgrade in every level, not just brightness or resolution.

Now in total fairness, the monitors themselves arent bad at all and in hindsight, comparing 230 dollar monitors to a 1800 dollar Imac with a 5k Monitor isnt really a fair comparison and is something i myself overlooked. Its just that my Imac Monitor is such a great monitor, almost anything next to it is going to be a noticable downgrade unless i spend a thousand dollars plus for aditional monitors (i needed two of them)

Do i recommend this monitor? yes, IF YOUR PC gaming on a budget then sure. But for mac...i would spend more for better monitors (with built in USB C Support which this does not have)
So far it works pretty well. Good quality image.

Good monitor, great resolution and clarity
Only gripe would be the audio driver wanting to take over as primary output when drivers are installed or updated. Even after disabling.

I use this monitor for my office workers. We have purchased 4 of these monitors and works well.  I only wish it could adjust the height. Some of my employees have to put a book under it so they don't have to crouch to see the screen.

Just received this monitor and considering my last monitor for 7 years was a 17 in. xerox monitor, I absolutely love this monitor. HUGE upgrade. The packaging was good....doesn't contain an HDMI cord although this isn't much of a problem. The only thing I wish was that it would have come in a more discrete box (which is why I am deleting a star). The box is the box you would get from a store with the address attached to it. I don't like advertising to the neighborhood what I got and considering the fact that it sat on my porch for hours, this could have been taken by an opportunist.

If this is a gift for someone in your household, be aware of the fact that they will know what it is if they happen to see the box first.

This is a great LED monitor that I picked up for $130. The colors are crisp, display inputs are HDMI and VGA and comes included with a VGA cable and a DVI to HDMI cable. This leaves connectivity options. In addition, there is an AUDIO OUT mini jack something that isn't very common. It is more common for AUDIO IN and built-in speakers. While this seems convenient, the speakers are always poor quality in my experience so it is great to have the option to have them output to the source you desire whether it be headphones or speakers.

The touch sensitive controls are easy to use, however, I'm a fan of tactile feedback. This is a minor complaint. The major one I have is the stand that it uses. Most monitors give a direct flat view, but this one makes it tilt back slightly. This is problematic in pretty much all situations unless you sit in a viewing angle slightly above the monitor.

All-in-all if you can pick it up for around the price that I did I believe that it is worth it especially if you are upgrading from an old CRT because that's what I did. I picked it up as a present for my dad who had an aging Viewsonic 17-inch CRT that was flickering more and more every day and on its way out. The screen real-estate, resolution, and eye stress reliever is enough to justify the price.

Note: out of the box it does require adjustments just like most monitors because they are normally default on max brightness settings which can potentially shorten the life expectancy of the monitor as well as be harder on the eyes because of how bright it is.

Because there are many. This monitor is very low quality. About a week after starting to use it, I found that the video was constantly going in and out. I eventually discovered that the design of the connectors resulted in a very loose connection and had to get a cable with a thick end and add a bit of padding. This was after both weren't working. 1 month after having the device for a year, the power button just stopped working. And this was discovered while debugging long pauses in video updates that I also believe to be the monitor. But it's very difficult to debug while dead. So I won't know until I find a replacement.

My advice, do a google search for common problems, there are many and they are really basic. That said, if you've spent a lot on these devices, and are out of warrant like I am, there are some fixes if your comfortable with disassembling the device.
Perfect for the guest room
Thank you

Read thousands of reviews on this monitor and it’s competition. Originally purchased the 34” of this model but was not happy with size and impact of the monitor. The 38” is perfect fit for replacing a dual monitor setup. Resolution is great, speakers aren’t great but that’s not why you’re buying this monitor. Love being able to connect usb-C MacBook Pro and older generation MacBook Pro via HDMI and have split screen capacity and the monitor act as a hub. Easy menu to navigate. Solid large base, easy setup. Monitor does wiggle slightly when pushing menu buttons. I bought this for professional office use, watching the occasional movie and multitasking. I do not game on this regularly but didn’t have any significant issue when testing out Fortnite. People always complain about light bleed, and of course there will be some in a pitch black environment, especially when watching dark scenes (go with OLED if all you care about is perfect blacks), but the light bleed isn’t an issue and even with my OCD and tech perfection I forget that it’s even there (not even noticeable most of the time).This monitor has great adjustments and viewing angles and does rotate left and right. I read in a few reviews about the difficulty plugging in cables because of the down facing inputs, and while that is a valid complaint and yes it can be difficult, but not a deal breaker in any way, and does make for neat cable management and ability to mount flush to wall of so desired. Overall have had this monitor for a few months with intermittent use and am loving it. Old monitor was Apple Cinema Display 27”. This 38” is perfect size bc is gives you the ultra wide without feeling stretched as it is taller (compared to the 34”, which believe me makes a huge difference). Only thing I wish was the Dell would play nice with Apple and realize a huge portion of the world uses Macs and catering to that half would benefit their bottom line (example being that certain features can’t be controlled like volume/brightness from 3rd party keyboards when connecting over usb-c, can get around that with additional usb connections but I digress). Bottom line: solid monitor that makes sitting/standing at your desk doing work enjoyable and more productive, would definitely recommend.
Bought 2 for a home office set up to plug in my laptop with a thunderbolt3 to dual display port adapter. Works wonderfully.
The monitor is okay. However, it died after one year no matter I was on the display port or HDMI.

I love this monitor. I use it for Lightroom work and the picture quality is great. My RTX 3080 just tears it in games, even with framerate locked at 60. It is not a good experience. If you are not going to do any gaming whatsoever, then get this monitor. You won't be disappointed.

I think this would be great to use with something small like a phone. I wanted to use it with my laptop and it was a bit too small.
For $400 would highly recommend this to any PC gamers.

Love this monitor! This is my 3rd HP monitor. They are all still in use. The color, picture and product quality are all outstanding!

THe 27" is a fantastic size for multi-tasking without having to go to two monitors. Watching videos or movies on this makes for enjoyable viewing too.

This is a very slick and sexy monitor with good picture quality.

Great Monitor at great price (I got it on sale).  I bought the UP 25 because I had good experience with a couple of prior but smaller LED monitors.  I rally like the larger size. I didn't know what to expect for the price but what a great monitor. It is very light with good battery and a great screen. I use the monitor with a new MacBook M2 which has a fantastic screen. This monitor looks great next to the MacBook.

Great deal for a monitor as nice as this one. Beautiful image quality. Plus, I bought this monitor to use with a HP Pavilion 522n desktop PC that didn't play sound because it wouldn't recognize the speakers at all after we re-installed Windows XP on it (re-installed Windows XP with boot CD that did NOT come with the PC; couldn't find some unknown drivers after re-install), but now I have sound on that PC again because this monitor also plays sound through it!

I use this monitor for work but it galls me that this is the third one I have ordered and it still has dead pixels. Excellent monitor for the price!

This 21.5 inch size is perfect for my work, excel etc., color is great, I like that the on/off, adjust buttons on front, rather than top or side. I had previously purchased  24 inch monitors, but they  were too "in your face" for work. I like to be able to look out a window occasionally during work.  I requested the two monitor boxes  be placed inside an Amazon box. they were delivered packed very well, undamaged. Received within 3 days, Great price, very happy with monitors, delivery, and service!

This thing is gorgeous.  The colors are amazing right out of the box as each panel is calibrated at the factory. This kind of praise is too often just normal marketing jargon. Mine (placed in a fairly dark corner of a large room) required almost no alterations to look it's best. While I was excited to buy a 27 inch monitor to replace the 21 inch that I had before, my favorite feature might be that despite its large size, it does not dominate my desk.  This is undoubtedly due to the tiny bezel(5/16 of an inch) on the sides and top of the monitor. The bottom bezel, while thicker, is just 3/4 of an inch thick. Color accuracy is a strong point as well. Muy lindo el monitor de alta calidad para todo especialmente para juegos

The Sony 27” INZONE M9 4K is a very good monitor, especially at this price point. The colors were very good and I had no dead pixels. I used it on my laptop, which displays at 2K, and on my gaming computer, which can display at 4K. It had no problem handling all of my games. There are also plenty of settings and adjustments that can be made on this monitor. The only reason I did not give this 5 stars is because of the stand. Nearly every monitor I have ever owned, including my ViewSonic that I am currently using, has a foot that is flat and makes an L shape behind the monitor. This allows me to put my surge protector an other things under it.  This Sony monitor has an awkward stand that makes a much bigger foot print, including the leg that comes straight out the front. I would have to redo my desk in order to use this monitor. The odd stand also makes the monitor shake when I type at my desk. Overall, while the monitor is great, the stand makes me question Sony’s design choice. I suggest you look at this in person before buying and make sure it works for your desk.
Quit working in just over a year. Not covered under the warranty since it was over a year and four months.

I bought this portable monitor for my husband and he loves it! It works perfectly with his Mac and makes his work productivity much better. The screen is very easy to use and he has had no complaints.
Another very good viewsonic monitor.  I got just what I expected and for the price I am very happy with it.

So far I'm very happy with this purchase.  The picture quality and connectivity are excellent.  As with most computer monitors, you will need to add your own quality speakers to receive better sound and volume.  That being said, the speakers are not bad.

I currently have two odyssey g7s. One I bought new and this one I bought renewed. Besides saving money you would never be able to tell the difference between the two. This monitor can be used vertically and horizontally as well very easily. Just as good if not better than any BenQ monitors out there.
Broke after 8 months, it now refuses to turn on. Makes a chirping sound like the caps in the power input circuitry are blown. Took a gamble buying this cheap Acer product, and it didn't pay off.

I am so happy I purchased this.  I travel extensively for work and have to complete multi level Excel workbooks that require multiple screens to complete. It comes in a bag with extra reinforcement so I have been able to pack it safely in my luggage during travel.
Nothing to rave about and not a thing to dislike.  Solid monitor for everyday utility use.

Wow. Just insane how much more vibrant and beautiful my game play is on this monitor. I have never used viotek and try to stay away from underdog products but the price point and reviews did not disappoint here. I am beyond impressed with this monitor and will probably stay with this brand when upgrading.
Product was easy to use right out of the box, instructions are clear, and the image quality is vivid.

One of the best purchases I have made. My concern (unwarranted) was that it might not be compatible with Linux OS. However, Ubuntu 16.04 automatically detected 2560x1080 (21:9) and seamlessly functions without any issues. But I am sure there are other solutions available which can be found on Ubuntu community/forums.

I have tried two of these screens and both were garbage. I have a MacBook Pro and needed a primary screen because mine broke. I've had this monitor for a few months now. I was looking for something not too big and inexpensive. The quality is extremely high. As a gamer and designer I find that the screen does not hurt my eyes after looking at it for a while and even had an eye saver setting that adds a yellow tint just like the glasses without you having to wear them. Overall it is amazing for under 100 bucks. The only thing I wish I'd that the stand was a tad bit thinner but that's just me being picky.

I've had this thing now for a few months and when things are consistent it is quite enjoyable. I am someone who packs out my backpack with a full days worth of stuff for productivity, entertainment using either Samsung Dex or connedcting to my Steam Deck. However, and the reason for me finally writing this review, the reliability of this monitor hasn't been worth it. Throught the Mini HDMI the connection is stable and I haven't had an issue when using that. Problem is, when using the USB-C connection, this thing often cuts out on multiple devices or won't work at all. For a minimal setup, using the USB-C is quite necessary as it provides both power to the screen and the extended display image. This is important when I want to use things such as Samsng Dex for a minimal office setup or with my Steam Deck when I am on the go and want a bigger screen to game on. At the moment, the USB-C connection isn't working at all and I am not going to lie... it's been problematic since the day that I got this thing.

Although I am aware this is a productivity monitor first, I was in the market for a 4K60 Monitor that could game well and would not break the bank. I am used to gaming on console so 120FPS support was not something I required as I am satisfied with 60FPS. I did endless amounts of research on various brands in this pricepoint to figure out what would be the best monitor in terms of screen quality and response time. This specific model did not have many reviews in terms of gaming performance, however I did know that LG makes great gaming monitors, this one has a game mode, and I was already looking at the LG 4K model priced below this one. When this model went on sale, I took a risk and purchased it over other highly praised monitors in this price point not knowing if it would be good for gaming, and I can answer that YES it is!

Starting with screen quality, brightness is great, contrast is beyond expectation, white, gray, and black uniformity are all top notch, with little to no IPS glow. Colors are accurate and the resolution keeps things sharp. No complaints.

For gaming, when you put this monitor directly side by side with a high refresh gaming monitor, it is extremely noticeable that this will not be as fast of a monitor as something gaming dedicated in terms of response time, however it is much more responsive than a normal 60FPS TV or other productivity based screens. I also have a 15 inch macbook retina display, and the response time on this monitor is atleast twice as good. Unless you are an ultra competitive high refresh gaming enthusiast, you will not notice any kind of input lag or poor pixel response in this monitor. It is just as good or better than playing console on a high end TV, and in gaming scenarios, I personally can not tell the difference between the response time on this monitor and my LG gaming monitor. It is only when using UFOTest that the difference becomes very apparent, and this mainly comes down to the difference in FPS. If I ran my dedicated LG gaming monitor at 60FPS, the response would be about the same.

In short, if you are used to gaming on console with a TV and are looking for a responsive 60FPS display to prioritize image quality and play mostly singly player games with high settings, get this one and you will not have any regrets. If you are looking for the fastest thing in gaming and play mostly multiplayer competitive games you shouldn't even be reading this review. If you CAN afford it, I would highly recommend a 4K120 gaming monitor for the best of both worlds. In this price point with realistic expectations in mind, this is a fantastic monitor!

While the 144Hz support is technically there Acer gives you the smallest possible window with which to achieve it. Upon building a brand new system with an EVGA 1070 FTW I wanted to make use of 144Hz technology and at a reasonable price these monitors seemed like a smart buy. I was wrong. Most newer graphics cards these days come with 1 DVI-D Dual Link port and 1 HDMI 2.0 port. The rest are typically display ports. Since setting up my monitors I have spent literally hours trying to figure out how to make my dual monitor system work with only 1 DVI-D Dual Link port on my GPU. What I've found is that unless I spend about $100 on an active converter its impossible. The HDMI 2.0 port on my GPU does also support 144Hz according to my research but the HDMI port on the monitor is an old version which doesn't support this, which is sad really. The other alternative would be the very common Display port but these monitors don't even have them. All in all, this was a mistake of a purchase and for only $10 less than other monitors it still feels like a rip-off. This information presented is so vague and misleading that even with some research before the purchase you could easily make the mistake I did.

That all being said, I still gave the monitor a 3 star because the picture (actually running at the 144Hz) is very nice and smooth and the build quality seems solid for the short time I've had them.

At the end of the day though buy an alternative monitor with an actual display port in the back. They cost almost exactly the same price and are 10 times more convenient plus you get height adjustability in some cases.

Only buy this monitor if you are running a single display setup or if you have specifically bought a GPU with 2 DVI-D dual link ports. Otherwise its a waste of money. And realistically if you intend to reuse the monitor(s) in future builds spend the measly $10 more on a monitor with a better port selection and some height adjustment.

This display is the king of compromises. LG advertises the beneficial features (144hz, 1ms, IPS) but leaves out that this IPS is dim, it’s unresponsive at 144hz... ghostly and input lag... also, I thought 1080p would be ok... but it is a little blurry. Especially when trying to work. I also was surprised that this IPS has terrible viewing angles... isn’t that the whole advantage of IPS?!

I was previously considering the VA panel Acer Nitro... but was afraid of the viewing angles... after experiencing LG IPS I reconsidered... I realized the Acer Nitro @ 3440x1440 provided a better image, brighter @ 400 nits vs a mere 250 nits on the LG. And I could just run @ 2560x1080 whenever I want for the extra frames in games.

It turns out 3440x1440 is INCREDIBLE for viewing your OS, working on video or photo editing, or just browsing the web... And the viewing angles are identical to the LG, just as bad. Basically you gotta view it head on, not great for two people working on one display... but it’s curved... so who’s doing that...?

For gaming, the Acer Nitro is far superior. even @ 2560x1080 the gaming experience was better. Why?! HDR. It Acers display at $50 more gives you HDR400 which I thought would be boring... but it’s actually the highlight, no pun intended. Colors were popping like I had never seen on a display. The idea is that 400 nits peak brightness # means that when there is an explosion, or a bright light, the display channels all 400 nits to that localized zone of the screen. Resulting in colors popping and allowing the game to make the sun as bright as the sun, the neon lights glow like they should, and the world feel more real.

The LG display felt like I stretched out my 1080p widescreen monitor I’ve had for 10+ years... The Acer Nitro felt like the biggest upgrade I’ve seen to my gaming visuals since 2009 when I bought my first 1080p widescreen monitor... think about that for a second. Just go for the HDR400, there is no drawback and no regrets.

They mostly don't bother me, but, for others people's sake, here are the flaws/gotchas that I can see:

- as of this posting, the diagrams about where ports are for the 15.6" unit are misleading. This might be a photo of a different model. Also, the back of the unit looks different than the photo posted, but that is less problematic. Ports are relatively naer the back center of the unit.

- ports are not grab-and-go accessible, especially an annoyance if you keep the case on (I think I will because I might take it with me on the road). This might discourage the dumb habit some people have of packing it with the wires plugged in, so it might not be bad design. Also, you can take the case off and use a stand. - it'd be nice if at least one headphone jack were on the front of the unit. Headphones aren't even part of my usage plans, but that could change.

Obviously the size could be too big for some people's idea of "portable." Carrying it around is like lugging around a very large laptop. Not a bad thing for me, since I was looking for a good middle ground between small and good for Nintendo Switch.

*** UPDATE 12/23/21 ***
This update is to correct the top negative review titled "It's not IPS and it's not 75hz". I don't know where this person gets their information from, but the claim that you "cannot have both 1080 lines of resolution and 75hz at the same time" is completely false and untrue. And I'm a 20+ year I.T. tech. I've just attached a picture showing this monitor running at 75Hz in Linux. I've also had it working just fine at 75Hz in Windows as well. Now while you can't visually tell much (probably none) difference between 60Hz and 75Hz like you can going from 60Hz to 120Hz, 75Hz is still better than 60Hz. And this monitor has been working fine at 75Hz since the first day I've owned it. If that reviewer's statement were true, we wouldn't be able to run monitors at 100Hz, 120Hz, 144Hz, 240Hz, etc. The ONLY way this monitor would NOT run at 75Hz is if one's video card or video card drivers do not support 75Hz. Mine does and that's why I posted the picture of the monitor working at 75Hz. That reviewer's card or drivers likely did not support 75Hz, so the option wouldn't be there.

It sucks that Amazon took away the ability to comment on someone's review. Otherwise this would have been corrected long ago, and probably wouldn't be the top negative review that everyone sees first. It really bugs me when someone leaves a negative review with such completely inaccurate or false information, and then it goes unchecked and others say the review is helpful.

Having said that, I've had zero issues with this monitor. It's crisp, clear, colorful. And has been running flawless. Still 5/5. Definitely worth the money.

The Monitor arrived at home in time for the week-end.
It had a DVI cable and a VGA cable and I had no problems mounting it to its base.
After I plugged it in I realized that the monitor needs calibration right off the box (which is not uncommon with widescreens). After calibration the results are the following: NO ghosting, NO noticeable bleeding on any side, No dead pixels, great contrast ratio, perfect picture: WOW!! The issue? VGA snow effect from the VGA cable at 1680*1050. I had to use the DVI cable to avoid the issue, and i tried everything (including two other sets of cables and 3 different PCs).
Which would not be at all an issue if you are (unlike me) not using a KVM switch to use multiple computers.
I cannot afford a DVI switch, and this monitor won't work adequately in analog VGA at its native resolution. Maybe I got a lemon.
Let me say this: for the price, its still a steal, expecially if you are a gamer. The only down in that case is that the monitor comes with a warranty of 12 months, which I read around is honored with difficulty from Westinghouse. So, not a Samsung or a HannsG, still an enjoyable product if you use DVI. VGA users, BEWARE. Sorry.

I bought the 32" WQHD 240hz to replace a 25" 1440p 60hz IPS I used for both work and play. While I didn't gain resolution, I did gain physical size and refresh rate. I currently pair it up with a 1080p 144hz gaming laptop. For work, I'm able to put Outlook on the right and web browser on the left while I use the laptop monitor for my coding IDE. That might seem odd to some but I spend more time in Outlook and Brave than I do coding anymore in my position. Anyway, like I said the resolution didn't change from my old monitor, but the physical size difference is what made this possible. I couldn't read the text as well on the 25" 1440p monitor and if I scaled the display it lost too much working real estate. Now I have no issues at all so this was a huge improvement.

The gaming experience has been second to none. My laptop is 144hz at 1080p as mentioned so I'm used to higher framerate and have already gone through that revelation (once you know you know), but on this monitor it is so much better! The larger screen allows me to see so much more. I play a huge variety of games from FPS to RPG to Casual games like Bloons TD6 so I wasn't buying this to "compete"...those days are behind me but I still want to be competitive when I do have time to game. The bottleneck now is my laptop as it can only push 1440p above 60fps at low settings in Warzone and usually overheats my CPU. Anyway, this isn't a laptop review. The point is that I was able to lower the resolution to 1080p 144hz to mirror my built in laptop display and I was shocked to see just how clear it still was. When I had done that on my old display, the 1080p and lower was virtually unplayable due to how blurry it was but on this screen, it still looks really good.

My one complaint is that when the screen goes to sleep, I can't wake it without unplugging the HDMI cable and plugging it back in (to the laptop). I didn't have this issue on any other monitor I've used with this laptop so it's definitely a screen thing. I'm going to install the firmware update and see if that fixes it. I'll be building a new dedicated tower PC when chips are more readily available again and I'll switch to DP 240hz then and maybe it is something to do with the HDMI port itself. I also switched cables to a brand new one and it made no difference. Not a big deal really since it's easy to unplug and plug back in from my laptop on the desk but noting it nonetheless. I would definitely say this should not at all be a deal breaker unless you have this issue and you don't have access to the plug (not on the monitor but what you will plug into it).

A: No. I'm pretty upset about this too.

I've had this monitor for well over two years now, and i'm still loving it as much as i did when i first got it. I've had very few problems with it, but i did/do have some.

First problem was the 'jittering' or shaking that occurs at certain resolutions and certain refresh rates. I'm not sure what causes this, dirty power lines, EMF interference from other electronic devices, or what, but using Powerstrip and setting it to a different refresh rate (like 83 instead of 80 or 85) will fix this. I believe my problem was EMF or something from other devices, because now i don't have the problem... but when i did, powerstrip fixed it.

Second problem is convergence... there just isn't enough options, and you can't get it to be 100% perfectly converged, but you can come close enough, and just expanding the screen so it fills up all the edges takes care of it just fine.

Last problem is the casing or bezel or whatever you want to call the front of the monitor, but it's quite fragile. I've nicked it twice now from bumping things into the monitor (coffee mug as i walked by, and lifting a computer and accidentally striking the bezel). They're very small nicks, but still.

Other than those problems... this monitor has been absolutely perfect. I paid over $3k USD for this over two years ago, and although the price has dramatically dropped over the years, this is still one of best (if not the best) monitors around. Everybody who has seen a game on this at 1920x1200 or 1920x1440 are absolutely amazed at it, including me after these years.

Oh, i guess i should mention a couple of problems... but these aren't Sony fault. One, not all games are available at 1920x1200 and higher... which means sometimes i end up playing at 1600x1200. The only problem with this, is that you'll end up having black bars on either side of the screen.

Second, is that when you're playing at such extreme resolutions, your computer - especially your video card - has to be very uptodate, especially for the newer games. This means i'm literally buying a new highend video card and processor every year.

So if you can afford this monitor, and you keep your system updated often, then treat yourself to this monitor. I see people spending $2000+ on a system, then $200 on a 19" low end monitor, and that just boggles my mind. To me, the monitor should be THE most important aspect of a computer. Buy a good monitor, and it'll not only be a treat and pleasure, but it'll last you YEARS, and live through several computer upgrades/purchases.

Things I liked:
-Excellent price
-Beautiful color after dropping the green a bit. I've heard viewing angles aren't as good as IPS, but it's still much better than the typical TN laptop screen I was using.
-Extremely good contrast ratio - the best in Dell's S-series as this uses AMVA instead of IPS like the others.
-I was worried about ghosting because of the overdrive, but the only thing I've been able to find is the rare dark cursor-trail when moving over some grey backgrounds. I probably wouldn't have noticed this for a while if I hadn't specifically been looking.
-Very, very glossy and reflective compared to your typical monitor. I love it; others may prefer matte screens. You probably won't want to use this next to a window, or where you don't have control over the room lighting.
-Glass seems durable and easy to clean

A few cons and notes:
-No DVI input - just VGA and HDMI
-Only up/down tilt is adjustable. Turning it to face more left or right means picking the base up and turning it, which can be a bit of a hassle for a monitor of this size and weight.
-If you're using an AMD GPU, you'll want to switch your pixel format to "RGB 4:4:4 Pixel Format PC Standard (Full RGB)" to get the best contrast ratio (slightly better than default). If you're using an Nvidia GPU through HDMI, contrast ratios will reportedly be horrible unless you set the digital color format to "YCbCr444". (Credit to PC Monitor's review for this information).

Updates:
-In the comments, Mike D has pointed out an alternative to using YCbCr444.

This is a review of the flat screen Gigabyte G27Q 27" 144Hz 1440P Gaming Monitor, 2560 x 1440 IPS Display

For PC gaming this is a great monitor. However if you're hoping to use it with an XBox such as the XBox One X or the XBox Series X where 1440P is now supported with up to 120Hz refresh rate, know that this particular monitor will not work with the XBox One X at 120Hz. It will work at 1440P but only at 60Hz. I tested this with an AudioQuest Pearl 48 cable (HDMI 2.1 cable that works on 8K TVs) so this was definitely not a cable issue. The XBox One X will never show 120Hz as an option, even if you reset the console (I'm talking about the "Reset" option that wipes the OS entirely clean and takes quite a while - not a reboot reset). I tried all kinds of things such as disabling every display option other than 1440P etc. in order to see if that would make the 120Hz option available. I also disabled AdaptiveSync on the display itself and the XBox will still not display 120Hz as an option. I have read that others have been able to successfully enable 1440P with 120Hz on the sister curved monitor, the G29QC 27".

One other strange thing I discovered is that the while this monitor claims to support HDR400 (which is basically HDR10 with different light intensity) it doesn't seem to work with the HDR10 signal sent from the XBox. It does seem to work if the HDR signal is sent from a PC over DisplayPort. So I suspect that the HDR400 support of this monitor may not work over HDMI but may be for DisplayPort only. I also tested this with the monitor's latest firmware revision.

The volume control is impossible 4 to 8 buttons presses to get the the volume menu. Adjusting the volume and is not intuitive or quick. Using the OSD requires patience to use, it is slow to respond to button presses.
The backlight has some light leakage due to poor assembly quality, This is mostly only noticeable in dark situations. This is what I would expect from a low end LCD display.
Overall it is just ok.

After a week of searching and contemplation, I settled on the Gigabyte M27Q as my next gaming monitor, and I have not been disappointed in the slightest so far. Out of the box, the colors look great, but they can be made even better with a bit of tuning. I love the configuration Hardware Unboxed provides in their review video. I switched to this monitor from a 1080p VA panel, which I hated due to copious amounts of unbearable ghosting and low pixel density. I switched to this monitor, and I cannot find any smearing, ghosting, or flickering at any of the modes I've tried, and I have done a lot of tinkering.
As expected, G-Sync works great, without any sort of tearing or flickering. While you should NOT buy this monitor for HDR, the HDR gaming experience was a pleasant surprise. The monitor auto-switched to HDR mode when I first opened Mass Effect Legendary Edition, and I decided to leave it on to test it out. The claims of colors becoming washed out aren't exaggerated, and the lack of any local dimming hurts a lot, but the high peak-brightness of this monitor is quite good, so I did not find the experience to be "bad" by any stretch of the imagination. One of the things that I was worried about when I bought this monitor was IPS Glow/Backlight Bleed, and upon first turning on the monitor, I noticed a hint of it in the top right corner, but unless I'm staring directly at it and the screen is displaying complete black, I don't notice it at all, and I consider myself to be quite finicky with this sort of thing. Another feature of this monitor that I don't see mentioned by anybody is the 10-bit color support. While you do have to turn the refresh rate down to 120hz to get it, and it's NOT true 10-bit, (it's 8-bit+FRC,) I find the experience to be completely worth it, as I do little extremely competitive gaming, and my RTX 2070 really can't hit above 100 fps in games at high settings 1440p anyways. It's also worth mentioning that NO gaming monitors in this price bracket have TRUE 10-bit color, so it's not much of a downside that it is 8-bit+FRC. Another thing you'll hear people mention about this monitor as a negative is the "BGR" subpixel layout compared to the traditional "RGB" layout. After setting up ClearType in windows, I can see absolutely no difference between text on this monitor and text on any other display I use daily. In fact, to my eye, which again, I consider to be astute, text is actually sharper than I expected. Overall, I think that this monitor is unbeatable at the price, and I'd recommend it to just about anybody.

Ends up an ok picture but feels like its broken. Also getting TONS of blue screen errors now at weird times (never high impact gaming) but yes during movies, and once while writing this review. All kinds of memory type driver allocation errors. I want to love this monitor, but do not. Find a better one that works as advertised unless you are SURE you can configure it despite major obstacles.

It is super-bright and washed-out with the default settings and after loading the Windows driver it will revert to those defaults.  Look under Gaming > Shadow Control and knock that setting down a few ticks.  All better.  All the other advertised features seem to be working as advertised, after recovering from the retina burn.

A pretty nice screen overall. I'm using it and will continue to use it as a daily runner tertiary display.

The product title says it's non-glare but there's no way. The whole front face is flush fit glossy and reflective. Looking at dark images you'll also see a ghostly visage of yourself. The screen protector was pre-fit from the factory, which may explain it a bit.

The colors of the display are pretty good but not suited for color critical work. The out of the box settings have the contrast a tick too high and all the highlights are getting blown out too fast. Reducing contrast by one fixed it. The black levels are weak and get worse as the brightness levels go up. The colors are undistorted but may require some play with saturation. It looks good with adjusted settings. With a color calibrator this would look even better. It might just be me but it seems that plugging it directly to a PC without an extra power source changed the colorscape a tiny amount. Connecting over (Mini)HDMI it supports 10 bits per channel.

The touch screen is as one would expect. It's a 10-point capacitive and is quite responsive. The screen has a protector sheet already applied and has a hint of rough texture which makes it easy to use. Without the extra sheet it is probably very slippery which some may prefer. I also haven't left any finger prints on it yet but I don't seem to leave fingerprints even on my phone.

The speakers are meek. Speech is easy to make out at max volume with no distortion, other than being tiny and tinny speakers. There's a 3.5mm port which implies this has a dac/amp onboard but I haven't tested.

The VESA mount is super handy. It's a 75x75 mount pattern with about 6mm of depth. I had some M4*12mm screws ready to go. On an arm this is super handy to use - just tilt it back a little and touch away.
Its perfect for desktop, gaming and streaming.

The height adjustment is done well enough. With just a firm push, you can move it up or down. It's not a super high quality movement, but it works without tools or fanfare.

The colors and vibrancy are great. I do design work so having brilliant, true coloration is necessary.

I have been using 27" Asus monitors for many years now for photo editing and preparing artwork for printing. After calibrating the color for this monitor, I have found that it is at least as good as the Asus 4K monitors, but with one feature that I find sort of amazing to me.  This is an ISP monitor.  With a other LED monitors, it can be tricky to make sure you looking at the monitor from right in front of the screen.  If you are standing looking over my shoulder, or kneeling looking up at the LED monitor, the brightness will be slightly different.  This monitor does not behave in that way.  The color and brightness of the image remains the same regardless of your angle of view.  Since I am often showing he work to a client or business associate, this is a huge advantage.

Admittedly, it was an impulse buy. However, when I saw it next to several others, the difference in color and clarity was evident even at a distance.

When I got it home and plugged in... Wow! What a difference it makes. Even very subtle colors are brilliantly displayed now.

Best of all? It was on sale!

While this definitely is not for everyone, if you are light sensitive, this is a life saver. It’s not especially well documented, so plan to spend some time listening to YouTube videos to figure out the configurations that will work best for you. At a minimum, turning off color filters like Flux or Night Shift, reducing motion/animation and switching either to grayscale or a high contrast setting in your OS helps. Many useful display setting options to play with for this monitor are under Accessibility. Expect to experiment with settings. I also ended up buying the Belkin HDMI to mini display port and a USB hub to get this to work with my Mac laptop. Didn’t need anything extra to make it work with my work Windows laptop, and with the USB hub it’s pretty quick to switch between the two. It comes with hardware needed to attach it to a standard monitor arm plate and the included screw on prop arm works way better than I expected. I chose this model for the front light (and SOL didn’t stock the light-only version at the time), but I ended up using the touch screen quite a bit in Windows to find elements of webpages that weren’t visible in grayscale. Though the cords for it are bulky, and I had to make it a case, I actually carry this around a fair bit and turn my laptop brightness to zero, using the laptop as conveniently compact keyboard and touch pad. It’s made a huge difference - I can now do my data analyst job without making my headaches worse. I’d eventually like to have more than one for the extra screen real estate but it turns out I’m willing to do a whole lot of window moving and scrolling to avoid backlit screens. Bottom line: SOL has a great return policy - if you think you have screen related headaches, give it a shot!

Side note: I use this to watch talking head videos and tech classes on video. As long as you don’t mind hitting the C button occasionally (and I don’t, I find clearing the ghosting on demand super satisfying), and changing up the settings to favor speed, it works just fine to catch facial expressions and follow screen-caps and power point style presentations.

The VIOTEK HA238 is an okay monitor at a reasonable price. Its colors are great, and it is very stylish, but it has its drawbacks.

PROS: It's a great budget monitor at a great price. These colors are very vivid, and are beautiful right out of the box. No adjustments necessary!
Photography and art alike look beautiful. It's also great to watch movies on! I love the sleek, border-less look of the monitor. It is very contemporary and minimalist, which I can appreciate. I can look at this screen for hours without any eye trouble, and even all day (As I often do). Honestly, I have used this monitor for a long time, and I don't have too many regrets.

CONS: As someone who has used this as their primary monitor for about a year, however, there are some things I would like to point out. I'm a digital artist and gamer, my experience has been a little less-than-satisfactory in those aspects. The monitor has a 14ms delay, which you won't really notice unless you're playing a game at 60+ FPS. Rhythm games such as osu! will become harder (Especially in mania and taiko) as it might look like multiple beats appear at once, when they really don't. This can be attributed to the slow refreshing. It is not a true 60 fps monitor. If I had known this initially, I likely would have invested in a different monitor. On an older version of the product page, VIOTEK referred to this as, 'An enhanced cinematic experience'. Which, to me, just sounds like instead of addressing and resolving an issue, or just ignoring it entirely (As this is a budget monitor), they instead tried to reword it into sales pitch, as if saying, "It's a feature!". Nice. Thankfully, it's not much of an issue when watching films. However, you might notice a very subtle frame drop with presentations that require 60 frames. It's not too noticeable, and you gradually adjust to it. If you are someone who games all day, though, consider getting a 1-4 ms delay monitor (Those are about average, which is why 14 ms is VERY bad for fast reaction gaming).
As a digital artist, however, I have to be careful when utilizing colors. As I had stated earlier, the colors on this monitor are beautiful, breath-taking in their saturation. But you have to be aware that this monitor will display colors differently than a traditional monitor in order to achieve this vividness. Therefore I have to use my laptop screen to test the colors. However, they do look quite beautiful when I drag them back to this monitor. I guess I can't complain too much.
Even though this monitor is pretty sturdy, I need to address the fact you can't tilt this monitor up or down, just left-to-right. This was never an issue for me as it's at a perfect eye level from where I sit. However, for people with tall chairs or short chairs, I wouldn't recommend this. Also, when you lift the monitor up, the bottom stand (Which is in 2 pieces) disconnects unless you hold it in place AND the rest of the monitor at the same time. A bit annoying if you're cleaning your desk, as the monitor is very front-heavy without its bottom stand, leaving you holding a thin metal stick. Sure, a sturdy one, but it's still just a stick.
The last gripe I have is something very easy to fix. When you plug this monitor in for the first time, it is blindingly bright. Maybe that's just due to my sensitive eyes, but turning it down a ways seems to fix the issue. However, I wasn't expected to go blind from turning a monitor on for the first time. Like I said, though, very easy permanent fix. Just an odd factory setting.

The PG32UQX is the best monitor in the world currently and is absolutely worth it. The HDR on it is amazing due to the brightness peaks it can reach combined with all of the dimming zones. Unlike OLED, which is dim garbage in comparison, there is just no contest. 4k, 144hz, true 10-bit panel, 1152 MiniLED zones, 1600 peak nits and DisplayHDR 1400 certified, and a G-Sync Ultimate hardware module.

Watching 4k video in HDR on a display of this caliber is such an extraordinary experience that it isn't something that can be described in text. Gaming is also amazing for the same reasons in addition to the G-Sync ultimate hardware module. The color space coverage is also enthusiast-tier (I've never seen higher PCI-D3 and REC.2020 coverage).

Build quality is ultra-premium and it's full of nice firmware features.

I’ve been using this monitor for over 1.5 yrs as my second screen. I’ve been traveling constantly since I received it with no issues. The sleeve it comes in works fine to travel with, you just can’t put anything super heavy on top of the screen, but that’s just common sense. I probably use it about 15 to 20 hours a week and it still works just like it did when I first received it. This is the same portable monitor my former accounting office would use and I used the same one there for 3 years without issue. I did have a coworker go through about 3 or 4 of these in the same time period though, but he wasn’t careful.

Bottom line, this is a good portable monitor and if you’re good at taking care of your belongings, then you shouldn’t have issues. If you break stuff all of the time, then this isn’t the right monitor for you because you will break it.

This monitor’s price tag should be cut in half. The specs should be nice— but it’s a nightmare to work with.

The monitor stand can be awkward on your desk. While it does allow a 100 x 100 Vesa mount, it looks like it may not fit many monitor arms.

The local dimming looks pretty terrible. There’s some unfortunate issues with the monitor going black snd “scanning for signal”. This is fine when you shutdown/restart, but it will do this when you move you mouse away from a game (even with HDR off). No multi-tasking allowed here.

Overall very disappointing for the price tag.

Once calibrated to your particular aesthetic standards, the monitor looks incredible. Games feel so much more immersive with this ultrawide, curved monitor. Images look crisp, clear, and vivid, so much so that during gaming sessions I will spend time just observing the scenery and taking in the environment. There is even a setting to calibrate the darkness of the monitor specifically for shadow quality, which makes horror games more frightening and the overall density and quality of shadows much more realistic. Everything from images to games and movies have more precise and scrupulous detail than my previous ASUS 27" monitor. The stand, however, sucks. I don't care about the absence of swivel ability like others do, but the stand has two legs that protrude too far forward past the monitor. This makes positioning a keyboard and mouse very difficult, such that my keyboard has to be set awkwardly over one of the legs and my mouse hits the other leg occasionally when playing a game. It's more of an annoyance than anything else and since the monitor itself is fantastic, I can put up with it. LG, if you're reading this, please develop a better, more space-saving stand with your next iteration of monitors. Thank you.

First for those who claim this monitor is difficult to set up in portrait mode I don't understand?. It took me less than 5 minutes to  unbox  the monitor, assemble it without tools and install it . I use it in portrait mode and the display just swung around no problem and is fully adjustable. I am fully impressed with the mechanical quality of the mount and the ease of installation. Picture quality is quite good for a 109.00 monitor and easily competes with monitors twice the price. If your looking for a 24" class monitor with a well made fully adjustable mount at a bargain price look no further.  A truly awesome deal and is much better quality than you should expect at this price

I am extremely happy with this monitor. It's replacing a 27" Apple Thunderbolt Display. The colors and brightness are at least as good as the Apple display, and the 4K resolution improvement is very noticeable. Web pages and text look so crisp now, I really don't think I can go back to the Apple Thunderbolt display now.

I LOVE that there is a single cable from the screen to my MacBook Pro, with no clunky adaptors. This charges the laptop, drives the display, connects the keyboard, and mounts by external backup hard drive. I wouldn't care about this if I just left my laptop plugged in all day, but I frequently need to take my laptop away from my desk, and it's now a pleasure to do this instead of dealing with a mess of dongles and hubs.

If I could make any improvement, it would be to add more ports on the back - there are two USB-A ports for keyboards / mice / phones / etc., I wish it had three or more. Also, there is a single Thunderbolt-3 USB-C connector that runs from the laptop to the display - it would be awesome if there was a second one so that I could daisy-chain other displays or connect USB-C peripherals.
I’m blown away with the quality of this monitor considering the cost. I highly recommend it.

I bought this monitor mainly for art purposes. I've tested editing photos, drawing, and working in programs such as Autodesk Maya, Unreal Engine 4, Zbrush, Photoshop, Substance Painter, etc on this monitor. I can confidently say it's a great investment for artists and creatives all around.

I purchased this monitor through Tiger Direct for $599 last year and after finding a discolored pixel and backlight bleeding problems in the bottom right corner I sent it back for a replacement. The replacement monitor also had a discolored pixel in a different location as well as the backlight bleed problem. Luckily after keeping this one the discolored pixel has been very hard to notice and after a few months of usage the backlight bleed completely went away in the corner, perhaps these monitors have some sort of "Break in period" I never purchased this monitor for it's 3D capabilities yet I tried it out and it was pretty neat, but nothing I would use on a regular basis. This display is used primarily for 2D gaming.

The monitor excels at gaming and at 120hz it is breathtaking. the colors are vibrant, the motion is fluid, and I don't notice any ghosting whatsoever. I would have liked to have a standard base with height adjustability. The current design forces the monitor to be slightly close to you if you have it against a wall, also I have some minor concerns with durability as well as the monitor leaning a slight bit to one side, it's not noticeable however. On a side note, although this monitor is very fast with a quick response time, I noticed that my friend's alienware 120hz monitor was quicker when testing movement with the mouse, again, very minor.

It's not the cheapest monitor by a longshot however after using it I couldn't settle for anything less. It's big, gorgeous, and fantastic for gaming. I've logged many hours on this baby and the monitor has yet to have any issues. I've taken it to a few LAN parties where I had to take into account its size and odd shape (it's doable, just requires planning). I'll admit that I've fallen in love with this monitor and I'll hopefully be using it to game for years to come. I want to give this 5 stars yet the few issues I've had have forced me to pull just one.

Pros-Nice Large Display
Beautiful Colors
Fluid motion
Fantasic for 2D 120hz gaming
Wow's all of your friends

Cons-Not the most durable design (Not terribly portable either)
No height adjustment
Quality control (Discolored Pixel)
BlackLight bleed in lower right corner (Went away after several months of regular use)
Price may be a deal breaker for some
Not the fastest response time yet still VERY good

I think this is the only inexpensive 24" 4K monitor, so I thank LG for continuing to make it, but there are some annoying things.
1. The base: the c-shaped base takes up way more desk space than it needs to. I have a 24" Viewsonic monitor that takes up much less space. You also can't put things on the base because it isn't flat, so you might lose some desk space if you replace a monitor with this LG.
2. The on-screen display: every time the monitor wakes up from being asleep, the LG on screen display wants to show itself and tell me that the beeping is set to normal and the accommodations are off, and this display stays on the screen for 10-15 seconds so I have to wait to start using the PC.
3. The power light: after I shut down my PC, the LG monitor power light wants to flash every couple of seconds--very annoying if the monitor is in your bedroom and you're trying to sleep. I put a piece of black electric tape over it.

LG does not use this finish on very many larger monitors.  In fact, they do not use it on any of the 43 inch monitors.  If you like the clarity of a glossy screen but don't want to have the mirror effect, this one is great because even though it is glossy, they add a very slight anti-glare coating to finish it off.  The result is a screen that has exactly the best of both worlds.  You get none of the haze of the anti-glare and none of the mirror of the gloss.  The only way you see a mirror is when you turn it off and the screen goes black.  You get a great screen here.  The only way it will be too much mirror is if you live in a greenhouse (maybe) or work in a disco (maybe).  People say the base is week, which it is relatively, but if you just set it up and leave it alone, it's OK.  I really wish it had tilt.  You really should be able to tilt any ANY monitor, especially the larger ones.  So instead of tilt, I just placed a few books underneath to lift the monitor about 5 inches off my desk.  (Still needs tilt.)  (I received nothing for this review.)

This monitor is absolutely gorgeous. The colors are vibrant, the black levels are impressive, and it has many features. When I was unfolding it from the box, the hinge was quite stiff. I was actually a bit concerned that I would break the monitor right in half. Instead of holding the base and pulling the screen to unfold it, try holding the screen and pulling the base. It's less scarier and it works out easier.

Overall, I really enjoy this monitor and I would highly recommend it!

First of all great price comparing to similar spec monitors. I was looking first to spend $1K but pull the trigger on this one and so far I'm happy I did. I am weekend gamer and 100Hz it's plenty for me comparing to what I've use to have. Games looks superb, at least for my eyes :) Quality build is great and monitor feels solid! No dead pixels on arrival always good! Wall mounting bracket included it's a plus as well.
Two things I don't like about this monitor (first one is the biggest issue for me):
1. PC is connected through DP port and my NVR cameras through HDMI port, when you turn off PC video source is switching automatically into HDMI... and leaves monitor on (unless you setup "turn off time" or you push off button.)
So when you turn on PC you have to manually switch into DP.... that's weird?! Why monitor can't maintain last source and let the user decide when to switch to different one?
2. Location of monitor control buttons - don't like it at all!... underneath front bezel, little hard to remember which one is which unless company would make some handy little remote control for that, or if they place at least these buttons on side bezel.

For the price and size, this makes an excellent gaming monitor (though not THE best). PS3 looks excellent over DVI (I use an HDMI cable with HDMI adapter for DVI).

Though this monitor is a TN Panel, it is still fairly bright and displays colors very well. Just be sure to adjust your settings, factory settings lack at displaying a good picture.

I use the VGA port for my PC, and while it looks good, DVI looks slightly better (but again, DVI is for ps3).

With PS3, 1080P downscaled on this monitor looks perfect and is better than 720P upscaled. With the 1440x900 resolution of this monitor, stretched out 720p looks very blocky, but 1080P downscaled looks very detailed and sharp. Granted, its not full HD, but its definitely good enough :]

Also with PS3, contrast and color on this monitor is inferior to my Sony KDL26M4000, but I prefer the superior response time of the Dell (5ms vs. 8ms makes a BIG difference for me). The Dell also has a tilt function and is more portable.

Cons of this monitor:

-No HDMI port (have to use an HDMI/DVI adapter)

-No Component/S-Video/Composite ports (but they are really not needed)

-No built-in speakers (I use external ones)

-No usb (I would'nt use it anyway)

-Viewing Angle (Tilt eliminates this problem for me)

For the price (well under 200 bucks), this is a great, cheap alternative
to buying an expensive hdtv if you do a lot of gaming.

I've been waiting for a 4k @ 60Hz monitor for some time now. Previously I was using the Seiki 39" 4K using my Macbook Pro.

I'm running the Seiki Pro 40" 4K@60Hz using my Macbook going from mini DP to DP on the Seiki.

My gaming PC with consists of:

i7, Asus GTX 780ti, 16 GB, 1 TB SSD, 236 SSD, 4 TB HD, going from DP to DP on the Seiki Pro.

I must say I am liking this monitor so far, but let me tell you what I don't like first:

CONS:

1. The price - $999 right now for this monitor is a bit high considering you can get the Phillips for $799. Seiki has always been aggressive on price so I am bit disappointed the price is not a bit lower.
3. For a $999 monitor the stand is NOT height adjustable. This is simply unacceptable if you ask me for this price point.
4. It makes no sense to me why this monitor DID NOT ship with HDMI 2.0. Seiki says they will ship later in the year with HDMI 2.0 so be aware of that. For me I'm using this strictly has a monitor and using DisplayPort and not HDMI.
5. Maybe I'm nitpicking here as I've had some of the newer Dell/LG monitors recently, but I don't like the controls on the Seiki OSD. I find the controls easier to use on the Dell and I know on the newer LG ultrawide monitors there is a little joystick to control the OSD. Again, a minor point but I thought I would bring it up.
6. Again, a minor annoyance but why is the Color Temperature not defaulted to 6500k? I would change this when you get the monitor unless you like other Color Temperatures.
7. Another minor annoyance is that if I go to reboot my Macbook which is connected to DP1 and my gaming PC already is on and connected to DP2, the monitor automatically switches over to the active connection. I then have to go back into the OSD and go back to DP1 to go back to the Macbook Pro. Again, just something to be aware of.

PRO:

1. It's 40" 4K @ 60Hz. I like it, but in all fairness I haven't tried the Phillips 40" 4K monitor that is also available here on Amazon, but that takes months to ship. If you are a hard core gamer 60Hz probably won't be good enough for you.
2. Black levels I find to be pretty good. In the TV world I only buy full array Sony XBR's so I do know good black levels from bad. In this case, the Seiki Pro is not at that level which is no surprise, but the blacks I do find to be good. Do you find some flashlighting sometimes going on with the panel? Yes, but overall the black levels are good.
3. No real glare. Again, it is not a totally matte screen, but it is not shiny either. My wife noticed it as well as soon as I set the Seiki Pro up.
4. Probably the biggest PRO here is that 40" 4k @ 60hz looks great. I have played Titanfall, H1Z1 and it looks great.
7. There are PIP controls, I just haven't tried it yet as I have had this monitor for a day.
8. I do find text to display well on using both the Macbook and my gaming PC.

Although I am a technical guy, this is not a technical review. I'll let others run this monitor through the torture tests, but to me it looks good and is one of the few 40" 4K@60hz monitors at this SIZE out there. However, I do like this Seiki Pro monitor very much and as I continue to test it, I will definitely keep it.

As I continue to test it I will add more comments and happy to answer any questions anyone may have

I purchased this monitor with the sole intention of using it for console gaming and casual computer usage. I am not a PC gamer nor do I do any heavy photo/video editing. I transitioned from my 55" Samsung LED TV to this monitor and it has made a huge difference in my gaming experience. I connected my XB1 to the monitor and my TV via my Yamaha receiver and had the output display on both screens at the same time. There was significant lag noticed on the TV vs the BenQ monitor was responding instantly. During game play, I can really see a major difference in how fast the game responds and believe it or not, my KD and SPM has gone up quite a bit over the past couple weeks of using this monitor. For giggles, I attempted to play on my TV the other night and it felt absolutely horrible and sluggish. I can not praise this monitor enough. If you are a console gamer this monitor is for you. Consoles are limited to 60FPS so a 144hz monitor is overkill and wasting money as the console will not be able to output for than 60FPS (at this time). As far as color/picture quality goes, its pretty decent. Sure, some IPS monitors will have richer colors but pretty colors will not help when it comes to gaming. I spent a small amount of time adjusting the color/contrast and was able to tune a picture that I am very satisfied with. I also noticed a major difference in the black level adjustment on the FPS setting on the monitor and I am able to easily pick out enemies in dark settings where as on my TV it would have been pure darkness. 27" screen is the perfect size for my gaming setup and I sit about 2 feet back of the monitor, 24" might have been ok but I am definitely glad I bought the 27".

Summary:
If you are a PC gamer and have a rig that can pump out 144FPS then look for a higher up model. If you're looking a for a general computer monitor, look for something else as well as you can get a nice IPS monitor for general PC usage for much cheaper. If you are predominately a console gamer, this is the monitor for you as it was designed specifically for console gaming. I game around 20-30hrs a week and wish I purchased this a long time ago.

I really wanted this one to work. I tried and tried, but to no avail. I purchased this monitor to act as a nice, portable solution for travel. The idea of a single USB cable providing power and video seemed like the perfect portable solution, but the monitor was never able to work.

To preface, I am very well-versed at computers and use and have used numerous DisplayLink products (video through USB) currently and in the past.

I was not able to get this monitor working with my Win 7 laptop. Furthermore, I even went as far as calling ASUS support for assistance and they were not able to help me either. After being tossed around to several different departments because each didn't think this USB monitor fell within their department, someone finally tried to help me. They suggested several troubleshooting steps, including pressing buttons that were not on the device and trying to use a Y-cable that wasn't included with the device. I had to instruct the representative to view the product page for this product on the ASUS website so she could see that this product does not come with a Y-cable and doesn't have the buttons she wanted me to press. In the end, she gave up and told me to just return it to the manufacturer with no mention of being willing to replace it under warranty or to escalate my call to a higher level of tech support.

While I am very disappointed in the product and the level of support I received, I will give an honest assessment of the product's features:

PROS:
+ Small form factor and light size
+ Comes with a carrying case (which can double as a stand, but not a great stand)
+ Single USB 3.0 cable connection

CONS:
- May be very difficult or impossible to get it working.
- Tech support is very poor overall - uninformed and not motivated to help.
- May need to purchase an additional Y-cable in some circumstances, according to support desk.

In the end, I returned this product and instead purchased a GeChic On-Lap 1502I 15.6" Full HD IPS Panel 10-point Projected Capacitive Portable Touchscreen Monitor (http://www.amazon.com/Projected-Capacitive-Touchscreen-Compatible-thickness/dp/B00DS18ZTG), which is a great monitor.

I'm currently taking online classes to get a degree and for three + years, I did all of that on only a laptop. I wanted something more dependable and reliable than a laptop and was thinking about an All-In-One. After cringing at the prices of those, I found a really, really, good deal on a desktop computer that has Windows 8 and since Windows 8 was pretty much made for touchscreens, I wanted a touchscreen monitor. I walked into a retail store and they were looking for $400.00 for one of almost equal to maybe a little greater quality than the one I found on here, but a different brand. Instead of cringing, I ran out of that store and bought the one I found here and I absolutely love it. It's easy and simple to use, and even easier to hook up.
The only downside is the sound quality, but I'm okay with that because pretty soon, I'll be moving in with a roommate.

Disclaimer: I was provided this unit free to review. The opinions are my own and are not affected by this fact.

Update 2023-04-06:

I’ve been using this for a while now as a 3rd monitor for my work setup, and it’s been performing very well. It’s crisp, clear and works great over a single USB-C cable from my MBP.

The only issue I have found to date is that when my MBP goes to sleep and shuts off the display, the Svaanto monitor goes into this weird state where it shuts off and then comes right back on with the bright white logo screen. And then back and forth, off and on again. Since this on a desk in my bedroom, this is extremely annoying. I haven’t found a way to enable power saving on the monitor or to turn it off, so I just unplug the cable from the MBP for now. I tried changing Eco to something other than Standard, and when it goes to one of the modes, it just power-cycles the monitor and reset that setting. Same for the DCR setting.

Initial Impressions:
The packaging was well done, with the screen protected by sufficient foam on the front and rear and the entire thing in a thick, solid box. I had a little damage to one corner of the box, but it didn’t touch anything inside.
Unpacking the monitor, and it as light and thin as I was expecting. This is a far cry from the original style of these external monitors that were used by consultants and traveling programmers.

Powering on:
While the unit comes with plenty of cables (2 USB-C and 1 HDMI to mini-HMDI) and a USB-C power brick, I was able to fire it up right away just by plugging it into my iPad Air (4th gen) and 2021 MacBook Pro (M1Pro) via a USB-C cable. Both provided sufficient power for the monitor to come to life immediately. I have no clue what it will do to the iPad battery life, but it’s nice to know that you can use it as an external monitor if you want/need an external display.

Display Impressions:
The display, when connected to the MBP is crisp, clear and plenty bright enough. It ships with the brightness turned all the way down, but turning it to 50 makes it look great. I hit the option to Scale the display and set it to the native resolution of 2560x1440 (you have to check the box to show all resolutions, for some reason it doesn’t come up with the native one by default). And it defaults to 130Hz, but I also tried 120 Hz. The image is gorgeous and it even support HDR on the Mac. I see no signs of dead or hot pixels on the display, which is nice to see. To adjust the monitor settings, you have to press in on the little flip switch on the lower right side. It ships with Brightness very low (20) but Contrast and Black Level both at 50. It has the full spectrum of other adjustment options. One note, Setting HDR on the monitor doesn’t seem to work with a Mac, and setting HDR on the Mac Display Settings causes the display to be very washed out. Also note, if you set the monitor to HDR Auto - you will need an external power supply connected (the manual states this) or else the monitor will try to pull too much power over USB-C causing the MBP to cycle the power on the port and you won’t be able to disable it until you connect it to external power.

Folio/Case:
This is the one are that is a little disappointing. The cover that comes with it is very cheap and doesn’t leave me very confident that it will hold the screen in the selected position (one of two). I’m not even sure if there are 3rd party options for a cover or case out there, so this may be the achilles heel on an otherwise solid piece of gear.

Sound: I did not review the sound, but the monitor has speaker grilles visible on both sides. I can’t imagine that would be anything to write home about, and most users will likely use headphones or a headset when using this.

Overall Impressions:
For the price, I think this is a very good monitor for the road warrior. It’s not full 4k, but most gamers (which this is designed for) don’t tend to game in 4k because the GPU power required would be pretty massive. But for a 2k monitor that is light, powered by USB-C and look good, this is a solid buy.

Other notes: There are 2 USB-C inputs, a mini-HDMI input and a headphone output on the monitor.
