Arrived packaged nicely, however about a week in I have a stuck pixel in the middle of the display that is noticeable on dark colors. I know stick pixels can be common but only a couple days after getting the monitor made me a bit upset.

So, I'll be reviewing this as my first real, HD monitor in about 6 years.

What did I use between that time?

A laptop, a CRT, and an HDTV.

Laptop was fine.
CRT was blurry and bulky.
HDTV was alright, but had very noticeable jagged edges and slight blurriness.

I've been doing myself a disservice for years in HD PC gaming. About 3 years ago, I decided I wanted a big screen, one that I could play both my PC and PS3 on without having to switch cables/monitors/setups. I also didn't want to be broke, and being a teenager at the time, I had no job and was still in high school. My solution? Buy a rather cheap 32" TV.

I was thrilled at the time. It was pretty damn big, had some decent color, and my games were huge!

However, it wasn't as advertised. It claimed 1080p, but it was a 1366X768 resolution AT MOST, and it was rather grainy and jagged. Text was a bit blurry, games didn't look their best, but it was what I had, and up until last week, I was perfectly satisfied. But I was seriously missing out.

I've had a steady job for awhile, and have been upgrading my PC because of it. New case, new 780ti, new fans, it's all been great. However, something was missing. My games ran better for sure, but didn't look any better. I knew why. My screen sucked. HARD.

So browsing the internet, I found this. It was decently priced, and while slightly smaller than my HDTV, said full 1080p with great response times and amazing colors. Hours later, the transaction was complete, and I eagerly awaited it's arrival.

Setting up was simple. I'll warn you now, I got this "used-good condition". It claimed it had a few cosmetic issues, but worked fine. When I got it, I saw no issues at all, not even cosmetic, and it was perfectly wrapped despite a pretty rough looking box. Nice job Amazon!

Anyway, the screen looks great. It's rather thin, looks sleek, the screen barely reflects at all, and upon turning it on my desktop immediately looks gorgeous. After some tweaking, colors popped out appropriately, edges were sharp and clean, and everything looked so much clearer.

I tested a few games with some decent AA settings, and games like Far Cry 2 looked like brand new games! I could see every detail, even in the distance. Barely any jagged edges at all (so long as you have the anti-aliasing on and/or have a high resolution set).

I really wish I could compare this to other real monitors, but the only other one I had was a small 19" HD Dell monitor as a secondary. Now I use my HDTV as my secondary!

I can at least say, I don't regret my purchase at all. I do wish I still had the larger screen space of my TV, but I'll settle with the compromise for much better clarity overall.

First, don't swallow the HP spec that says this display has an 8-bit front end. It's a 6-bit LG-made e-IPS panel, the same panel used in the hallowed Dell U2211 and several other competitors, including one I also own: the newer LG IPS226v. The monitor stand is a heavyweight (weighs more than the monitor) and works well, once you spend some time fiddling with it. I still haven't figured out how to unmount the stand, but neither have I gone thru all the documentation (Documentation? This owner reads documentation? What's documentation?). The box is huge for a 21.5", more like a box for a deluxe microwave, but half the space is for the stand which itself is ready-assembled; just slip the monitor over the grooves in back, and voila. It's a headache having all the rear connections facing down, but it does make for a very thin monitor. The LG panel inside is 6-bit, but its a proven performer. My gray ramps show so little dithering or banding that staring at them occasionally during hours of use still reveals no defects or artifacts. Screen uniformity is even; my desktop for video work is an RGB32 dark gray, nearly black. I don't see any edge bleed, and as I say my desktop color is really dark, which helps when color-correcting video. It's a sharp image, but was a little noisy at first with my analog graphics card: I ran the VGA adjustment utility that comes with the CD, and any complaints I might have had simply vanished 5 seconds later. Neat. Out of the box, color was OK, brightness too high. After a few calibration runs with an Xrite/Display 2, the image meets D65 and sRGB standards to a T. My colorimeter tells me the darks below IRE 20 display a slight falloff in Red, something like a piddling 2% down, and blue is about 1% high down there. The rest of the RGB grayscale is a straight line. I wanted a gamma of 1.8, and that's what I got after calibration. I also tried HP's Display Assistant; not the greatest calibration software, mainly because doing that sort of thing by eye is no match for a colorimeter. But it's better than nothing, and it's more precise  than other such features I've seen. Overall the image looks sharp and almost film-like, not as grainy as my LG IPS226v. A 20-step gray ramp displays with only a barely (and I mean barely) hint of warmth in the panels, but all of them uniform, and all of them distinct from darkest to brightest. The color out of the box was OK but too cyan, with very slightly yellowed brights (you had to work to see it). I lowered brightness to 66, and lowered Green and Blue by about 15 to 20%, not that much of an adjustment from the defaults. Adjusting RGB precisely was tricky, though, because the RGB controls are set at 255 max by default, which means you can lower the colors but you can't raise them. Odd. But lowering Blue and Green worked OK. The panel is on its first day now; I expect to recalibrate after a week of burn-in. The CCFL backlight is a blessing; the LG I compare it to has great color, not quite as film-like and with visibly less low-level detail than the HP. The LG's edgelights had poor uniformity and visible clouding in two corners, the sort of problems that plague all LED's. This HP has better clarity and no screen problems that I see yet. After editing a few old videos I can see some minor but visible color errors in the darks that were obscured on the LG. The LG could get a more accurate calibration, but I'm talking about a 1% difference that can be measured but not seen by eye. From the HP I get an sRGB CIE chart that looks as close to ideal as any I've seen in this price range. Both monitors, having the same LG-made panel, have slight haloing issues that this panel is (in)famous for, but the backlight difference makes it far less obvious on the HP, and in actual use the problem effectively doesn't exist. A 100 white (RGB255) full screen white panel shows a subtle cyan clouding at each side,, so vague it seems to disaappear in actual use with graphics and spreadsheets; brightness was very even, as I measured less than 10% variation, and thankfully there was no bright hot-spot in the middle. This HP is for my 2nd video computer. With this level of performance in a non-pro monitor, I'll be using that 2nd PC more often. I'm not a big fan of HP's displays for video work, but this one looks like a keeper. I hear HP plans to revise this model by replacing the backlight with LED. What a shame. I'm glad I picked this up before yet another manufacturer ruins a good panel with yet another useless fad. Caution: I use it to color-correct video, but not for extended movie viewing: at full-screen there's frequent motion stutter. Somewhat surprising, since other monitors using this same LG panel don't have that problem.
It has incredible picture: clear, vivid and dynamic. It has every type of audio and video output people need. It is light weight for its size.

I've had this for a week.

Pros:
Construction feels great. If you use the stand, the feet are metal and the stand is covered in a black shroud that is plastic but appears metal. The frame is metal (assuming aluminum). No glue spots on the edge of the screen.
Vesa mountable if you don't use the stand. The stand is highly adjustable and way better than most in the box stands I've used.
Bezels are almost not there. The sides have about 1 1/2 mm bezel and top has 1mm. About a 1cm bezel along the bottom, but expected as that's where the power switch is.
I dont have any dead or unresponsive pixels after running a pixel test. The picture is fantastic. Great colors out of the box but could use a slight more tweaking.

This is my first curved monitor, but I enjoy the curve quite a bit. It's adds a bit of immersive feeling to the games.
I sit. 3" from the screen and I can't see pixels or lines. Looks great from far away as well.

Cons
Typical VA panel cons. Black smearing and slight in low light scenarios. Bad viewing angels (a bit wider angles than I've experienced with other VA monitors). High pace/ fps games, stick with IPS or TN.
RGB. Rgb isn't a con for me, but the placement is. You won't see it at all no matter where you place the monitor, which defeats the entire purpose.

Other things to know
I can run AAA games at max settings with this monitor. I use a 2060s, and it can get taxing on my card at times. If you plan to run AAA games, at a minimum I'd have a 5600xt/2060s or to be safe I'd have a 2070s/5700xt.

My PC runs three monitors from two 1 GB video cards. I had planned to make the LG my forth monitor (two monitors on each card) by connecting its HDMI port to my PC's HDMI port. All four monitors booted, but after about 15 minutes the LG would enter a power savings mode and never recover. This did not happen when the LG and PC are connected via DVI ports.

I tried two remedies but neither worked:

* Bought an adapter to convert my PC's HDMI port to a DVI-D port and connected the LG to it, and
* Adjusted Window's power settings to never enter a power-saving mode.

The LG web site does not address this problem; but I noticed similar complaints elsewhere on the web. Also, the instruction manual notes possible "incompatibilities" if you use the HDMI port to connect the LG to a PC. You should not buy this monitor if you plan to connect it to a PC using their HDMI ports.

OK, first of all, I'm using this for both gaming and regular PC use. I have very light-sensitive eyes, so I'm really happy with the Dell Display Manager software which can be set to automatically detect what you're doing and adjust the monitor's brightness, saturation, gamma, and contrast for you, so you aren't fiddling with monitor controls all the time. This means when I'm surfing the web, it's darker (because I can't handle the bright white web pages), and then when I switch to a video or game, it gets brighter and the contrast is adjusted automatically to give the best quality image. This is a fantastic feature.

Let's talk about the built-in monitor controls for a moment, though. Dell was smart enough to make the monitor buttons create an on-screen display showing you which buttons to press to get the desire result. No more squinting at tiny letters or symbols, or fidgeting with buttons trying to figure out which one does what. Adjusting the Dell monitor is a breeze!

This doesn't have a DisplayPort on it, which I consider a non-issue. I do have DisplayPort support on my video card, but honestly I think HDMI is just fine for a 1080p image , which is all this monitor can handle. There's no 4K resolution support for this model, but that's fine. Gaming in 4K is nice but setting to 1080p means a higher frame rate and less work for my video card, so I'm fine with HD. Besides, Windows does weird things with fonts and window sizing in 4K, and this is just easier to deal with for everyday Windows computing.

Of course, if you're still rocking the old school VGA connection you're still covered here. Dell hasn't abandoned it yet. However, you should be aware that I tried an HDMI 2.0 cable on this and it balked at detecting my PC. I had to switch to an older HDMI cable to get any results.

This monitor does not have built in speakers, so you'll need to consider that when setting up. However, it is a really nice IPS panel that has a super-thin bezel. It's all screen, with the tiniest black border, and that's nice. It maximizes desktop real estate while still giving you a very large true 27" diagonal picture.

Colors, contrast, sharpness, and white levels are all excellent. No complaints whatsoever regarding image quality. There's no ghosting or lag, either, so gaming works quite well.

Bottom line: This is an excellent 1080p IPS monitor with an intuitive software companion app and full support for HDMI and VGA. Five stars.

I use this when traveling to boost my productivity by having a second monitor. Although it comes with a protective sleeve, there is not a lot of protection for the screen. I usually travel with a piece of hard cardboard cut to fit the screen and slide it in my notebook bag. I found that it works better to have it plugged in to the PC before you power-up the PC, otherwise it might go through the flashing image cycle trying to sync. It rotates the image when you change the orientation. The image is clear enough to read a PDF manual at normal resolution.

This is a very good product 100% recommended.
The color and the style is very cool.
The product came in exellent state.

Needed an extra montior since I started working from home. My work computer had two monitors, and especially given how small my little laptop is, this was an absolute necessity. Installing drivers to get it to work was a huge pain since it comes with a disc but this laptop has no disc drive of any kind, but obviously that's not their fault. Ultimately I was able to visit the manufacturer website, find the drivers, and get the monitor working. I wish I could adjust the aspect ratio on it, but my laptop at least doesn't allow me too. However, that's not a huge deal since I can manually scale screens up and down anyway. What I have beenost impressed with by far is the durability of this thing! As you can see in my picture, I set up my home "office" outside on my balcony, and twice now I have managed to knock this thing off the desk and have it land face-down on the hard surface below. I seriously expected zero picture or a cracked screen or something both times, but it has hung in there like a champ! If you need a durable external monitor for your laptop, this is 100% the monitor for you!
P.S. The desk, keyboard/mouse tray installed on said desk, and fan in the picture are also all Amazon finds, in case anyone was wondering ;)

I have owned plenty of monitors through the years and BenQ in past has impressed me. Needless to say when it came time to move to Gsync I waited for this monitor to come out. Compared to other Gsync monitors that i have previewed BenQ's XL2420G really stands out on its own. It has a solid feel to it and does not feel cheap. Gaming on this thing is a joy though it should be noted that many games still do not work the best with Gsync or higher framerates. (I am looking at you Skyrim and Diablo 3.) While it is only 24" large and standard 1920x1080 i find that is perfect for myself. I have little to no desire to move to larger or "4k" monitors.

The setup should be noted when you plug it in via display port (needed to use Gsync) the monitor was reporting that it had no cable plugged in. I needed to go into the monitor options and switch it to the Gsync channel at which time it fired right up. That may cause some minor confusion.

I think that most games in the future will be supporting Gsync and the only ones that are not doing well also do not work well with 144hz monitors. In those cases telling the monitor to run slower with Nvidia control panel has sorted out all of that. While I am tempted to remove one star because of the price of the monitor, it is about the same price as the rest of its Gsync hyped counterparts.

Other reviews and the q&a cover the technical aspects pretty well. The base is pretty stable, and having 2 hdmi ports you can switch between is nice. The monitor's controls are accessed by pressing a small joystick under the bottom of the monitor. It can feel a little clunky at times compared to having a button for each setting, but honestly all monitors feel pretty clunky when choosing settings.

Only thing I dislike about this monitor is the lack of speakers. Yes there's an audio jack and yes usually you use a headset while gaming, but if I'm just working/surfing the web and wanna listen to music I'd rather it play through the monitor.
Great quality picture, I use it for the security system.

Great monitor! I absolutely love the IPS panel. The price is amazing for 144hz, and an IPS panel. The graphics that this can run are amazing! Of course, this is only 1080p, but you can still see amazing graphics. 10/10.

It's a great monitor but has no audio. You'll need a "Dell Genuine AX510 Entry Flat Panel Stereo Sound Bar 1908FP" if you want to hear anything from your computer. That or your computer into an external audio source like your stereo to hear anything.

The curved nature of this monitor is perfect. I've used ultrawides in the past but I found the screen a bit too narrow vertically. This screen requires less head movement from side-side and more vertical screen-space. The corners are curved in closer and it perfectly compensates for the further distance these areas would be at if the screen was not curved.

I can see some people having issues with this monitor's curve or lack of adjust-ability. I do wish the monitor was more adjustable and I did have to put some bumpers on the bottom of the back to get the angle of the monitor to be more downward.

All in all though I think this style monitor is super underrated, though in the future I might try a smaller flat 4K and set it closer to my head which is probably what most people do. Although being able to set the monitor back further let's me have more room in the front of my desk for stuff.

Ordered it from Amazon March 16, 2014 and received it the 18th.  It is Revision A3 and works very well, as advertised.  Why four and not 5 stars?  If you are a professional who needs accurate color, it needs to be calibrated.  The color and luminescence are way off, and while it is true that calibration is a must with any monitor, older calibrators will not work with this model because of the type of panel it is.  So be prepared to add about $225 for an X-Rite EODIS3 i1Display Pro so you can do the necessary calibration.  At least you will be doing a hardware rather than a software calibration, which is better.  Dell provides the software to use this, and only this, calibrator for the hardware calibration.  And before I forget, make sure you use the DVI-D cable that comes with the monitor.  Watch out for cables that are not -D, they won't work, and neither will HDMI, at least it did not work for me.  I am a  professional photographer and the monitor is excellent.

Note to buyers: This monitor only has a single Display Port output. No HDMI or DVI-D etc, so plan accordingly. Also if you plan to use the USB 3.0 hub feature on the monitor you may want a longer cable. It ships with a very short 4 foot cable.

I chose this over the ROG Swift as I'm only running a single GTX 770. I didn't want the frame-rate drop of a higher resolution and wasn't wanting to have to buy two new video cards. I'm guessing in a year or so 4k will be the name of the game and the prices will be dropping like flies as the technology becomes mainstream. For now I'd rate this monitor as an excellent value.

I couldn't be happier with the result.

The monitor is very well made with a sturdy and well designed base and stand. Everything feels nice and solid and you can position and tilt it any way you want to. No screws or tools are needed as everything snaps together easily for assembly.

Hookup was simple, no special cable needed for G-Sync beyond the Display Port cable that comes in the box which hooked right into my EVGA GTX 770. The drivers automatically detected and set G-Sync to be the global setting. The only thing I had to do was set the monitor's refresh rate to something higher than 60hz.

I did experience some foreground label hiccups when using 144hz in Diablo3. Switching to 120hz fixed the issue, but be aware that some games may not be optimized or work well for the 144hz refresh rate.

You can really crank up the brightness of the monitor by turning off ULMB. Which seems rather necessary as the default brightness with ULMB is a touch dimmer than an average monitor. You can also rotate the screen to work in portrait mode if you desire. Nice for viewing web-pages but probably not something many will do often as this is really a gaming unit.

After seeing this in action, without a doubt a high 120hz+ monitor with g-sync is the single largest upgrade you can make to your gaming rig today. Most graphics cards are limited by the monitor they run through and usually are capable of outputting far more than 60fps. Getting one of these monitors removes the bottleneck and lets you see the performance you've been paying for all this time.

Screen tears are absolutely gone, with or without the ULMB turned on. This monitor is fluid and just screams speed-wise. Frame-rate drops are hard to detect due to the lack of screen tearing. Everything just looks smooth and flawless. Just an amazing upgrade from the 60hz panel i was using previously.

Gaming detail is great due to the large monitor size, colors look rich and vibrant, it's an eye candy experience for sure. The only thing to complain about is that viewing angles affect the brightness of the image. This isn't really a concern for me though as I don't make a habit of jumping up and down while playing games. Once again, this is just par for the course when you want a super-fast TN panel. Contrast feels great in games and the size of the monitor makes the experience feel very immersive.

The settings interface is quite different than other monitors but fairly easy to use once you get how it works. Once I got used to it I find it faster to quickly edit common settings than I have on other monitors. This monitor also snaps to attention quickly when you wake it up. I've had other monitors that have a bit of a slow warm up time. This one just pops on and is ready to go.

No blown pixels on this unit and I haven't seen any reports of that in other reviews over at Newegg. The quality controls seems to be excellent.

This is definitely a keeper of a monitor while waiting for the 4k market to arrive.
I liked how clear the picture was on this monitor. I got it to act like a 2nd monitor for my laptop for when I travel since it makes life really easier. Would definitely recommend it.

I bought this monitor to be used with a Raspberry Pi computer and it is perfect.  It looks gorgeous and video looks great on it.  The built in speakers for this monitor are great for my setup as I don't have to worry about rigging up some kind of speakers for such a small computer.  Really happy with this purchase.  Setting up the stand took me a few minutes as there were no directions on how to do so.  I wasn't sure how to put everything together until I started taking parts of the stand apart.  Once I figured that out, though, everything went together very well.

First let's start off by describing how I will am using the monitor: I use it as a TV when connecting devices such as a roku, fire tv stick, or a kodi powered chromebox. Additionally I use it as an external monitor for my macbook air 13" early 2014 model.

Connectivity: My normal monoprice minidisplayport to hdmi cable could only support a 1080p image instead of the native 1440p. I bought a new cable "6ft Premium DisplayPort 1.2 Male to Mini DisplayPort Male Cable" which connects my notebook to the fullsized displayport of the monitor. I initially had concerns that the air couldn't output native 1440 content, but I tested the output and it works well without any stuttering. Honestly 99% of content is in 1080p anyway, but it's nice to know that you can view higher definition content.

As a monitor, this is a very nice display. I'd give it a 9 out of 10. It's definitely not a $2,000 display, but then again it's not priced to be. The image quality is better than the screen I was replacing it with (Samsung 23" LED monitor). Images are crisp, clear, vivid, etc. I can't really tell a difference between it and the build-in display of my macbook air (other than the resolution).

In addition to the image quality, the hardware is very nice and sturdy. The monitor feels heavy and the stand feels VERY nice. It definitely looks and feels like a high end item. I'd give the hardware a solid 9.5 out of 10. It's as close to perfect as I can imagine.

Now, let's get to the "speakers" of this monitor. They are maybe a 4.5 out of 10. This was one of the main reasons why I bought this monitor over a similarly priced monitor. It had built-in speakers which would allow me to have a nice all-in-one solution when using my computer monitor as a TV. Previously I used a Bose Soundwave + Samsung LED monitor for this setup. Previously this monitor had "beats" branding and now it has "bang and olufsen". Do you actually get "bang and olufsen" speakers? NO. The audio is "tuned" by whatever the brand you have pasted on the front of the screen. These speakers are nowhere near the bose speakers I have. They are ok for watching tv or some music, but they don't have any bass to them. I believe a pair of $50 logitech computer speakers will produce better sound than these speakers. Don't buy this screen if audio is important to you.

Considering the cost of the monitor was mostly for the screen and not for the speakers, I only took one star off of the possible five in this review. If they could've included better speakers and raised the price by $50 or so, I would've been very happy. They would've had a killer product that no other manufacturer could really compete with in this category.

To complete the experience of this monitor, I'd like to review something that you may be using on a daily basis: The remote. It simply sucks. I'd give it a 2 out of 10. It feels weird, the buttons are mushy, and the front is one piece of molded matte black rubber. This was definitely an afterthought. I just got this monitor, and thought it was fantastic, except that it didn't tilt. But with a little perseverance, I figured out this was NOT TRUE... It does tilt... Also, I was worried about how "red" the rose black frame was... But, it turns out it is actually black, and when you hit it with direct light, it gets a red "sheen" to it. So...

Final Analysis: This is a very sweet, very large monitor that has great color and sharpness. I could give it 4 stars because of the touch controls, as they are a little finicky to use... But the fact that the rose black frame didn't clash with anything in the room, and that this fact made my wife happy, easily made up for that last star. (Also the fact there weren't any burnt/dead/stuck pixels...) In the end, I would definitely recommend this monitor to others.

Tips on tilting: I have read some articles with people frustrated the stand doesn't tilt. It actually does. How you tilt it is the trick. Apparently the weight of the screen "locks" it in place. But if you lift it enough to take the pressure off the stand, you can then push the bottom back and forth. It does still require a little pressure, it doesn't swing "free".
It was nice and slim. Picture was not as bright and glossy as I had hoped.
There were mixed reviews for this monitor, but I hooked it up and it just worked! Accuracy for the touch screen is also very good. I would buy this again, no problem.

The monitor itself is good.  The screen protector material is designed for a smartphone. As soon as you get any part of it close to the surface it jumps to cling with static and which forces you to start over.  I would buy a 3rd party product and trash the included screen protector as that is about all it's worth, trash.

So far, I can say that 4K with G-Sync is an amazing experience. My GTX 980 can handle Battlefield in 4K with AA/MSAA turned up quite a bit - in the high 50s (57-59ish FPS), and it doesn't stutter or tear one bit. Personally, I feel that the jump between 1080p and 4K is astonishing; I feel if I had been coming from a 2K screen, I might have been less impressed, but for my particular situation I am thoroughly enjoying this product right now. It's quite amazing how G-Sync and 4K work so well together; it's one thing to read about it on paper (or web sites to be specific), but another to experience it first hand. It's smooth all the time, as long as the frames are being pushed above 30. Even when it's about 20-something frames per second, the image is pretty crisp and clear.

I'd recommend this product to anyone, as long as they are willing to shell out the big bucks for it.

This laptop uses a 65W charger, which is what the monitor is spec'd to handle. Moreover, even when running on a separate charger, the monitor fails to provide USB-3.2 support that is native to the built-in hub. I have had to lower it to the USB-2 setting for the connection to become steady. I have tried multiple cables known to work, so it is definitely something with the monitor.  Contacting Philips support has provided so far no solution other than the potential to RMA the unit, but that has not yet been resolved.

I originally purchased a '42 Sony Bravia for the sole purpose of it's low lag time of 17ms. For gaming, it's a solid choice and I don't regret my choice. I mainly play Destiny, exclusively actually, and my Bravia displayed Bungie's world very well.

In Destiny, I have become increasingly interested in improving my Crucible game. I had also recently purchased a custom Battle Beaver Customs Elite Controller with maximal tension on both thumbsticks. My game improved slightly, but one thing was missing: a monitor. I did some research that led me to this purchase.

The only way to really showcase how potent the 1ms response time is would be to adjust to the BenQ and then sit with your old monitor. The difference really is that striking. And besides the obvious increases in play due to no lag, the display offered here is super crisp and clean; there are details seen on this BenQ that I never picked up on before.

To summarize, this monitor is worth every cent.

Two complaints. This monitor can definitely strain the eyes if you play for prolonged periods of time. Each Crucible session lasts me 2+ hours. The first couple of days I exexperienced wicked migraines, but they passed. Definitely play with gaming glasses, like Gunnars. And secondly, the speakers are decent quality, but only decent. Definitely have a pair of headsets to maximize on the whole immersive FPS experience.

So yes, as evidenced in this glowing write up, gaming monitors are beyond a worthy investment. I love this thing!!

The new 23" display with the aluminum bezel is invaluable to professional writers / page compositors for its ability to display full-sized pages (side by side).

This display is sharp (edge to edge), bright and easily driven by G-4 laptops as well as the desktop line. It has industry-standard I/O and (for Apple users) two each firewire 400 and USB ports on the back of the display. These act as a pass-through to the computer driving the display and are very discrete.

The mounting options are superb and the quality, fit and finish are museum-quality. If you need a great (big) display for failing eyesight, video or book compositing or you just want to see HD DVD playback at the highest resolution available - this is your display.

I've always liked Samsung monitors and TVs, so I thought this was a safe purchase.  After 98 days of not-quite-daily use connected to my laptop, the HDMI channel just stopped working.  Crapped out in the middle of my work.  Totally black screen, except for a momentary "HDMI" graphic when it was connected.  No amount of replugging, restarting, or reconfiguring would bring it back.  Tried another laptop; same issue.  It *might" be the cable, but I think that's unlikely.  Since I'm not a gamer, I tried the VGA input through an adapter, which works fine for my purposes.  So caveat emptor and all that; you might consider buying the extra coverage offered by Amazon, or at least save the Samsung box for a year.

Now that I have purchased a power supply for this monitor that has enough wattage, and I can see an image, I wish it were a better image. The blacks are not black. The text is poorly focused. In general the image lacks fidelity.
As a second monitor for a desktop, it is below my expectations. If I were to use it as a second monitor for a laptop it would still disappoint me. The Lenovo brand has lost its shine for me.
There are major hurdles to using this monitor with a Windows 10 based desktop and the typical hardware found in a desktop machine. In addition to marketing this to portable computer users, Lenovo and Amazon might have done just a bit more to market to me, like telling me the specs for the power supply that is required to turn this monitor on. This is a totally new realm for me, a person who does not use a laptop. I own many USB power supplies but not one that is sufficiently powerful enough to run this monitor. Maybe they feel that all their users will know what power would be needed because they already own a power supply of sufficient power to run their laptop? But not even Amazon made a suggestion regarding an appropriate power supply.
And if there were no major hurdles, then why is Lenovo leaving this to be supported by the community?" They should have put together a package of accessories that would make this useful to a Windows 10 desktop user.
When the power supply that I recently ordered arrived, I was able to turn it on immediately which allowed me to update my product rating from one star to two stars.
I hope you enjoy this lightweight portable monitor with the fuzzy image more than I do.
Good luck with the Lenovo community support.

This monitor is breathtaking. It truly is. Games feel so incredibly smooth and buttery and I absolutely love the enhanced experience. Screen tears are a thing of the past. I didn't realize how detrimental those were to my experience but this monitor has really opened my eyes to it. Even in games that my 980Ti can't power at a full 144hz, the gameplay doesn't take a hit when you drop to 80-90fps.

The one issue to bring to light is occasionally on monitor wake or start up, you'll get a weird band across the right side of the display. This is actually the monitor displaying the pixels in the wrong order, and that strip of display exists somewhere else on the screen. At first I had no idea what it was, and panicked thinking that I need to box this back up, disassemble the multi-monitor mount arm that I put it on because now my second monitor will be out of balance and tip over. Turns out it's a weird little glitch that is fixable by simply hitting the power button and then turning the screen back on. It's a minor inconvenience, but still a reason to knock off a star rating. To have a premium product with a glaring issue like that is cause for the loss of a star. Especially for the hour of anxiety I had trying to figure out why my monitor messed up on the second day of having it.

All in all though, I definitely still recommend it for any gamer as the color accuracy, refresh, brightness, and physical appearance are all phenomenal.

This monitor works terribly with my MacBook Pro (Retina, 13-inch, Early 2015). The mouse lag is so extreme that it's not possible to do any kind of design work with it. And there are rendering problems with some application windows; for example, Photoshop's title bar disappears, or my Mail app's inbox goes completely white, until I move the windows and let them re-render. Also, the auto-rotate to vertical orientation doesn't work. I'm going to keep it, however, because I haven't heard of any other portable monitor that has better Mac compatibility. (Why doesn't Apple offer one?) Even though I can't work on it, I can use it to read reference materials while doing the actual work on my laptop monitor. It's very light and slim, which is nice, and was easy to set up. I would give it fewer stars based on my own experience, but I don't want to knock it too hard for those of you with PCs or those who don't need to do the kind of intense mouse/stylus work that I do.

Don't be fooled by the fact that Amazon gives you the option to purchase a monitor arm as a package deal with this monitor. The monitor arm (conveniently an AmazonBasics one) will not be able to be used with the monitor. The arm that Samsung sells with this monitor has two major shortcomings: you can't rotate the monitor whatsoever, and you can't use it as a regular stand if you don't want to clamp it to your desk.

I originally bought two 32' monitors, with the intention of buying a third. I assumed that because Amazon sells a mounting arm with this monitor as a package deal, the monitor arm would be compatible. My plan was to use all three monitors at my corner-positioned desk.

A lot of other Samsung monitors also aren't VESA compliant, but there are adapters that work for them. This is not the case with the frameless monitor. The mounting screws are staggered in such a way that non of the existing adapters would work. Once an adapter is created, this will be a different story, but at the time of writing this (March 31, 2020), there is not a single available adapter. This monitor is so incompatible with other devices and accessories, it almost feels like it was designed by Apple.

And it’s been great! I can easily move from room to room as the monitor only needs one cable when it’s fully charged. Overall, I’m happy with the purchase and I’m actually using this monitor a lot more than my smaller 4K one.

This product is amazing. Going from a 144hz VA curved screen to this was a lot more than I expected.

Refresh:
144hz to 240hz isn't as intense as going from 30hz to 60hz, or 60 to 144, but the difference is still there. At 240hz, I can read text while scrolling or otherwise moving. And everything is just a little more clear and smooth. One thing I want to mention is the amazing response time. While acer put in a .5ms extreme mode for marketing, the normal overdrive works best, as the extreme produces clear inverse ghosting. However, the normal overdrive is more than enough to nearly remove ghosting and other artifacts.

Color:
VA to IPS was a nice upgrade. The HDR mode doesn't really seem to do much, but if you have decent color vision, you'll notice more saturated colors.

Resolution:
1440p is a nice upgrade from 1080p, and with everything else, made images a lot sharper and clearer. The screen has a very thin plastic bezel, but doesn't actually go to the edge. It has a 1/4" black edge on the sides and top of the screen where the display stops and the screen cover continues.

Other:
Going from a curved panel, my eyes took a day or two to adjust to the flat panel.
This screen has a pretty bright backlight, so it won't be nice on your eyes if you like to play in the dark. Definitely a daytime/bright panel.
It has a dedicated power button, which I appreciate coming from a monitor with only the menu widget thing.
The renewal packaging was very minimal, which I dont really mind, since I dont really plan on using it for anything except a possible return.

Major con:
This panel, along with other acer monitors, suffers from some defect which causes white bands to flicker across the screen when using the displayport input. I believe this is caused by a poor quality DisplayPort cable. Make sure you have a quality DP 1.4 cable, and update any older video cards to support DP 1.4.

I will update once I get a new cable.

I purchased this after having gone through 4 PG279Q models, and it’s already on its way back. One may wonder why still even bother with Asus at this point? Well, it's pretty much for the same reason everyone else does: the allure of a monitor with a refresh rate of 165 Hz, a resolution of 2560x1440, an IPS-type panel (AU Optronics calls it AHVA or Advanced Hyper-Viewing Angle), and G-Sync (which has since become less of a selling point due to Nvidia's decision to support FreeSync). The monitor I received had backlight bleed similar to the fourth and last PG279Q I purchased. It's usually the worse in the top right corner, followed by the bottom right corner, with a very minimal amount in the bottom left corner and the least amount in the top left corner.

It all has to do with where the bezels place pressure on the screen. One can press on certain points of the bezel and watch the bleed fade resulting in a more even spread of that cool blue/white sheen, known as IPS glow, that we've all come to know and love on IPS-type displays. Upon releasing pressure, the bleed creeps back in making the root of the problem quite evident. For the uninitiated, IPS glow is a compromise one accepts when using an IPS-type display while backlight bleed is a defect resultant from loose manufacturer tolerances related to quality control back at the factory. To make matters worse, the color temperature in the upper left quadrant of the screen was much warmer in comparison to the rest of the screen. There was an orange tint present that irked me to no end when I was in file explorer, word processors, or anything that had a white background. My sample failed to accurately portray light content in addition to dark content.

I have a Dell U2410 that I keep having to take back out because so far, at least for me, nothing has been able to beat it as far as overall quality goes. Quite often I see the argument made that one wouldn't stare at a black screen during regular usage. Although I agree with this statement, I’d also like to point out that it’s nearly impossible to not encounter dark content whilst browsing web pages, watching videos, viewing pictures, or perhaps most relevantly, playing video games. I've tried to cut Asus some slack in that I understand that these panels support a technology that took AU Optronics some time to achieve; however, the problem now lies not only in the bezels but the panel itself. I do hope that LG gets their take on this market right with their LM270WQA panel. I would advise staying away from this monitor because the story is a familiar one.

I wanted to upgrade the 1080p TV I was using as a second monitor (and TV) in my campervan. I'm in a small apartment right now, so I needed a larger monitor than my 17" laptop has for editing photographs, video, and for using a Roku 4k device for streaming media. This monitor also has a USB port, which is great for powering the Roku. Another feature of this monitor that appealed to me is that is natively 12 volt. The included power brick just reduces the 110v power to 12 volt. This means I can power the monitor from my house battery, making it a great 4K monitor solution for RV and vanlife use. Although the monitor does not come with a 12v adapter, one can be had for about $10. Here's the one I purchased and it works great: https://amzn.to/3uXVnL7

The picture quality is very good right out of the box. The stand is easy to install and seems plenty sturdy. Once I move this monitor to my van, I will remove the legs and use the VESA holes to mount to my existing articulating mount. The included picture shows the monitor next to my 17" laptop to give a better perspective on the size. The bezels are very thin on three sides, with the bottom bezel being bigger. Overall, I'm very happy with this purchase. Hopefully it will prove to be a durable and long lasting monitor.

There are some negatives, however none are enough to have kept me from purchasing this monitor.
- It has no speakers... not that I would have expected the speakers to be any good, but it would have enabled the monitor to have volume control. I have to control the volume with my attached JBL speaker to the speaker/headphone out port on the monitor. A remote to adjust volume from the monitor would have been nice.
- There is no dedicated source switch/button. You have to navigate to the correct menu page to change the source input, which is several button presses. A dedicated button would have been nice to quickly change inputs. Again, a remote would be great for this purpose.
- My Roku box must be set to 4k 60hz each time I use it or it will enlarge the video so only a fraction is shown on the screen. This could be a Roku issue, so it may not be the monitor's fault, but regardless, it's sort of a pain.

Otherwise, I'm really happy I got this monitor at a very reasonable price compared to the big names. Also, many of the big names didn't have the USB port to power accessories, or have the ability to operate on a simple 12v connection.

For the record Glossy Screens are Superior to Matte in everyway because Matte is basically a hard coating they put on the beautiful LCD to deflect light hitting the screen the positive side is no reflections but downside is it creates a nasty haze and damages your eyes over time.

In other words this glossy screen is like looking at the road through your car windscreen that has just been washed and cleaned. A matte screen is like you accidentally put Vaseline and tried to clean it off by just turning on the wiper only to make it worse or throwing egg whites on a car windscreen and turning on the wiper only to make it worse. Yes you will be able to see but it will be hazy and cause eye strain over time. As you can see from the pics I put I tilted the monitor to show you the worst it an be, this is hot summer right now around Christmas time for me with blazing sun and glare as you can see once you do not purposely set up your monitor to reflect the outside or a light source you will have no issues at all. The trade off is worth it for a gloss, you only need to adjust your room's lighting, another solution is you can paint your room in black or use air conditioning and put on blinds. Because a dark movie or dark game will cause an issue in daylight with this kind of scorching summer.

This is a gorgeous display. It is bright, clear, and has fantastic resolution and clarity.

It has very nice gold bezel with a texture that doesn't show fingerprints or smudges. It has clean lines and easy-to-access ports along the right side and controls along the top (they are on the back-side but easily reachable from the front).

It has built-in speakers, with good tone, clarity, and volume.

I plugged it into the HDMI output of my Raspberry pi 3 and set the resolution to 1600x1200@60hz. After a reboot, the screen came up in full-resolution. There is a very, very, minimal border around the picture (less than a millimeter) that lets you know the picture is not overflowing the edges - I didn't have to adjust it in any way.

The speakers are loud and clear, and comfortable to listen to at 40-50%, but loud enough to hear in a noisy environment.

I also plugged it into my Odroid-C2 (high-powered rPI clone) with the same result - fantastic picture and sound @1600x1200 resolution.

This screen is large enough and clear enough to use as a desktop replacement with an rPi3 or other high-powered single-board computer.

Alternatively you could use it as a second display for your desktop or labtop for watching videos, reading emails, etc.

It comes with a nice adjustable display stand but the screen is too thick to actually fit down inside the stand - but it did not slip off and seems pretty stable - this is the only issue I have found with it and consider it a minor annoyance.

It comes packaged with a high-quality power adapter, and good 5-ft flat HDMI cable.

Overall I would highly recommend it.

First, let me present some facts because the descriptions provided by Amazon.com and Samsung are just not helpful enough.

1. The actual screen itself is NOT GLOSSY. It is MATTE. It does NOT reflect light.

2. The black plastic frames around the screen is GLOSSY.

3. This monitor has only VGA and HDMI connectors. It does NOT have DVI connector. However, since you can easily convert DVI to a VGA with an inexpensive adaptor, it is not really a problem.

4. You can tilt the screen forward and backward. But the height of the screen is NOT adjustable.

P.S. I'm not going to dig into LCD vs LED. I owned a 25" LCD monitor before buying this one. I can't tell the difference.

This is a pretty good monitor, with good viewing angle, colors and contrast.

However, there is a high pitched whine that doesn't bother me much because I can only barely hear that pitch nowadays, but could be very annoying to kids and young adults (seems to be ~11 khz). The whine becomes louder as the brightness is lowered.

I'm also not convinced it's flicker free. At low brightness slow-mo shows several light/black bars scanning across the screen. In normal 30 fps video the brightness sometimes varies between frames as the scanning and camera ccd vary. So it's flickering faster than should be visible (like hundreds of hz), but I wonder if the same areas of the screen are dark for multiple scans because it certainly *feels* like flicker at low brightness. My other LED backlit monitor has zero flicker even on slow-mo.

I wouldn't recommend this monitor if you using it in a poorly lit area because at full brightness it's very bright.

Install was easy, though it does require the software to run properly. My laptop doesn't have a cd drive, but I'm pretty sure I can download online. I installed it on my desktop and it works great. I don't know how I don't have 10 of these already! I will update if there are signs of deterioration (product failure). Otherwise, my 5 stars stand.

Update 10/11/2016
It may be my laptop (HP Spectre 360), but there is significant lag on the screen when running anything that requires video card (such as a movie). It has gotten to the point where I would rather drag the screen back to my laptop to do anything. The only time I don't mind is if I have a static document, such as a book or word document, which requires no interaction. Otherwise, the lag is TERRIBLE. Knocked off a star (though I wanted to knock off 2) only because this is to be expected from a USB device. It runs a lot better on my custom built PC, but that computer is pretty stout and should not be used as a standard.

Update 12/15/2016
Poor performance, knocked off another star.

Really nice monitor. Factory calibration is excellent and produces very lifelike colors with Photoshop and Lightroom. Be aware that this monitor has a very wide gamut so you have to switch it from default back to SRGB to make most windows programs look normal as most windows features including browsers and backgrounds support only SRGB on the output side. Fortunately, this monitor is very easy to switch back and forth and it is very accurate on SRGB as well. I am running it with an i3770 and a new GTX1080 and the whole system is very responsive. I suspect the results wouldn't be so good if you had an old graphics card or a doggy CPU.
The recommended scaling is 150% and that works well for most windows programs. Lightroom CC does not respond to system scaling, so the scaling has to be set in Lightroom preferences to large (again at 150%) and all is well. It is kind of a shame to waste pixels with scaling, but the result is crystal sharp and text is very smooth.

I waited months and months to pull the trigger and till they were almost unobtainable for these to go on sale ($419; they debuted at over $650) and grabbed two, and I wish I had gone for three at that price after actually finally seeing and using it up close.

This is the enterprise/business model and has the three year warranty, as opposed the the plain 27UD68P or -W models which have only one year.

Though it is 50nits less bright than the consumer/pro version, It is far brighter than I or most people need, and I had to turn brightness down to about 70% as I use it in mostly darkened or low-light environment, but if you have a very brightly lit or sunny office, this will more than do the job.

Excellent color and clarity, excellent pixel density, response and refresh, multiple inputs and a perfect addition to any Mac or PC. On the Mac, you may want a third party utility such as SwitchResX to access additional resolutions and refresh rates beyond those in the System Preferences Displays panel. It supports 90º pivot with Mountain Lion or later.

It took me a bit of fussing in the onscreen controls to fine tune the color profiles for accuracy for print work, but most people will be thrilled with the default settings out of the box for general use.

The antiglare coating is more than acceptable; virtually no perceptible reflection except in very bright lighting.

You can drive up to four of these displays at full 4K/60hz (4th display limited to 30hz via HDMI) resolution on any Mac Pro 2008 - 2012 using an nVidia GTX 960 or better; or you can use a lesser card to drive fewer 4K displays; Mac Pro 2013 will drive six; any Mac or iMac with Thunderbolt or MiniDisplayPort can drive this (adapter might be required) at full 4K/60hz

The included stand on this model, unlike the consumer/pro version is fully adjustable with 90º pivot and pretty okay, but I have mine in a six-screen array using the included VESA 100 mount point.

A top reason I picked this over the highly rated Dell 4K UltraSharp HD models, and over the less expensive 27UD58-B, is the exceptionally narrow bezels on the top and sides; this is critical for multiple displays side to side and top over bottom as in my setup. The HP Envy 4K and the Acer 4K both have even thinner bezels, but did not match feature or display quality in my opinion.

Now the bad:

I wanted to give this display five stars, but I needed to knock a half-star (resulting in four on Amazon) because of the LG propensity to build awful onscreen user interfaces and obnoxious indicators.

The display is a bit slow to fire up after sleep, in part because it uses auto-input detection, which I can live with, but it insists on displaying a popup window indicating connection type, color profile and EnergyStar rating; then it goes black again, before finally displaying Desktop or Login screen. This is not only awkward and annoying to wait several seconds for, but is especially obvious when my other four displays pop on immediately, including one connected via DisplayPort.

I thought it *might* be a behavior of my video card or Mac with multiple displays, but it does the same thing on any native DisplayPort machine of any age. Terrible, needless user experience.

Then, as if that weren't obnoxious enough, when the video card output goes to Sleep, the display doesn't recognize a Sleep command, and instead goes to black, then almost immediately to a full, bright gray screen with a message indicating signal loss and that it will put itself to sleep "in a few moments." A few annoying seconds later, it finally does go to sleep, long after my other displays went black on command. Same behavior on any computer.

This is just sloppy programming, as they are assuming you may want it to auto-switch to a different input source, such as a TV input via HDMi or a second DisplayPort device like a Playstation. There is not, but should be, a setting to lock it to a single input, and have it accept Sleep/Wake commands without telling me about its connection or loss thereof.

This behavior is unacceptable when in or near a bedroom; if the computer wakes briefly for network access, or overnight maintenance ops, most displays will remain dark until told to wake. This LG, however, flashes on with both the connection message noted above, and repeats the 'no signal' routine for several seconds numerous times during the night. Just plain stupid.

Another annoyance is the INSANELY BRIGHT BLUE FLASHING POWER LED when the display is finally asleep. I had to put gaffers tape over the button recess on the bottom of the display to keep the entire room and nearby areas from glowing with near-reading level on/off blue light. It blocks access to the onscreen control toggle, but once I got it set up and fine-tuned for single-use with my Mac Pro, I didn't really care. However, if I need the control for brightness or a change of color space, I have to peel the tape to access and reapply.

There should be a setting to at least dim the damn light, or better, turn it off altogether. It just isn't needed for most computer setups. It's just masturbatory engineering at tis finest.

Now this model and any of the 68 series are hard to find, rarely available and for far more money; they have been superseded by the 27UD88 series, a USB-C/Thunderbolt-3 version which would require expensive DisplayPort adapters to use without native Thunderbolt support. Sad that LG doesn't continue to make this model available instead of the lesser 27UD58-B "gamer edition" with a much fatter bezel and far less bright screen.

If you need a 4K with DisplayPort or HDMI and can find one of these for at or under $500, you won't be disappointed; if you can use USB-C or Thunderbolt connections, look for the 27UD88 series which occasionally go on sale for under $500; there is a consumer and enterprise edition with one and three year warranties, respectively, so try to grab the enterprise version 27MU88(W,-W) [[ASIN:B01LG5RY74 LG Electronics 27MU88-W 27-Inch Screen LED-lit Monitor]] which is otherwise identical, and has gone on sale for under $550.

- Build quality is not great, it feels really cheap in hand and the lower part of the monitor noticeably flexes any time I pick it up, which gives me a some concerns about the long term durability and longevity of the monitor.
- Menu buttons are confusing, the "down"/"up" buttons are flipped ("down" is ABOVE "up")
- The speaker makes a faint humming/buzzing sound when there's no audio playing, fortunately you can go into the monitor menu and mute the speaker to get rid of it, but if you wanted to actually use the speaker then this might be a problem. The sound quality of the speaker is also really poor (as expected for the size).

Other:
- Works with a Nintendo Switch as long as external power is provided (sound quality not great as previously mentioned)
- Does not work with Dell XPS running Ubuntu 18.04 via USB C, but does work with a USB C dock that outputs HDMI. I didn't put this under cons because I am not really sure what to blame for the direct USB C connection not working.

In the end, a combination of the reflective screen and the poor build quality mean that I will be returning this monitor, but not without some inner conflict. It's the cheapest 4K 15.6" portable monitor I found on Amazon at $209 after a coupon they were offering, and others cost between $50 to $150 more.

If you want the cheapest 4K portable monitor, you're using it in a dark room, and you won't be moving it around too much then this monitor might be a good choice. You'll get great colors and sharpness and the cons I listed won't matter much. Otherwise you'll likely be better off spending a bit more to get something a bit more solid that will be more useful in varied lighting conditions.

Another model I've tried: https://smile.amazon.com/gp/product/B0828CXGN7/. The other monitor has significantly better build quality, has a matte screen so it'll work better in different lighting conditions, and has surprisingly good speakers for the size. However, the viewing angles and colors are not quite as good (but still good for professional use).

Overall Review:
I am a college student using this display as a second monitor for my Dell Inspiron 15 7567 Gaming Laptop with a Nvidia GTX 1050 graphics card. It has been great so far. I have it connected to my laptop with an HDMI cable and have not had any issues whatsoever. Pretty much a plug-and-play experience. It's a great size for productivity and gaming on my small desk. I'm no expert in color accuracy or anything, but it looks great to my eye and gets plenty bright for my needs. The anti-glare finish is one of the better ones I've seen and completely eliminates reflections in my environment when looking straight on at it.

Gaming on it has been a great experience. It looks and feels smoother than my laptop display thanks to the 75hz refresh rate. If you have an Nvidia graphics card, don't expect to be able to use FreeSync, as Nvidia cards only support Freesync over DisplayPort, which this monitor doesn't have. I can't speak to how it handles Freesync as I don't have an AMD graphics card to test it with.

Overall, I would highly recommend this monitor for someone on a budget who wants to game or do productive things on it. As I said, I can't speak for the color accuracy or usefulness for photo or video editing, but it's fine for my needs. Just make sure you're not expecting FreeSync to work if you have a graphics card that isn't AMD.

I think the "glossy" in the product's description refers to the bezel because the screen is definitely matte. My last monitor was a Samsung 206BW that failed (a capacitor issue). For the 2-3 years it lasted, I really liked the picture (as well as loving my 52" Samsung LCD) so I decided to give Samsung another chance. I wanted a larger size monitor mainly for photo editing that I do in Photoshop and Lightroom. For two hundred bucks, I could not be happier with this unit. I was almost going to buy an HP 2309 or 2509 monitor because it also has a great looking screen, but after comparing in-store, I wanted a matte screen because I think it's easier to resolve finer detail on a matte screen.

All I can say is that the brightness and colors of this monitor are great. I can see where some people might find the default settings a bit too bright but I prefer a brighter screen. The only fault I found with the default settings were that the colors were a bit flat. Once MagicTune was installed though, I set the gamma to Mode 3 and MagicColor to "intelligent" and am very happy with the picture I am getting. I'm using Windows 7 so I had to download the driver and MagicTune off the internet (the supplied CD-ROM seems to be more for pre-Windows 7 machines). I also had to reboot my laptop before 1920x1080 was an available resolution, but that's not the monitor's fault (not that it's a big deal to reboot anyhow). Some people might also complain of this monitor's lack of HDMI, but I was already using a HDMI-to-DVI cable so no biggie. I have separate speakers so I can't comment on the monitor's speakers. Nor can I comment on gaming performance (i.e. ghosting or lack thereof) or movie performance. I only use this for everyday tasks and photo editing.

After having this monitor for a couple days, I can easily say that I still would have given it 5 stars even if it would have cost upwards of 250. I'm glad I didn't blindly order a glossy screen from some place online. Don't get me wrong, glossy screens can look excellent, but I find matte more accurate for photo editing, and this monitor is not lacking color, contrast, brightness, saturation, etc. I think my only beef with the MagicTune software is the calibration feature. It shows test patterns and allows you to adjust things, but gives no explanation as to what you should be trying to achieve. But with the settings I chose (that I previously mentioned), I can tell that I'm getting a pretty accurate picture based on past experience from the myriad of photos that I've been looking at (and have had printed).

Not sure what else to say. I really love this monitor and am glad I bought it.

GPU: Nvidia GTX 1080 (Gigabyte GForce G1 Gaming), attached via DisplayPort. Stock clocks and fan.

First impression: solid packaging kept this monitor in perfect shape for shipping. Out of the box, the first thing I noticed was how solid the stand was, and the light weight of the monitor. Stand took 10 seconds to set up, and another 5 seconds to *click* the monitor on, which was very intuitive.

After plugging in power and display port, the monitor automatically chose the correct input and displayed Windows. Immediately noticed the crisp colors, sharp edges, and shockingly good contrast for an IPS panel. The colors, to my eye, look very accurate and I haven't changed them at all. I immediately went into the monitor settings and changed into Adaptive Sync mode (hardware vertical sync) which caused the monitor to turn black for a few seconds, then display my screen again. EASY. I also noticed that the monitor was refreshing at 144Hz in the information page for the OSD. This rate is only supported over DisplayPort, HDMI can't handle it.

The first game I fired up was Risk of Rain 2, which my GPU had a hard time keeping at 60 frames with VSync on. With Adaptive Sync, I get a solid 120+ FPS and buttery smooth action. Adaptive Sync really is one of the best features of modern day monitors.

I also tested this with Escape from Tarkov and Hell Let Loose and had the same buttery smooth picture. For FPS games, the contrast really stands out as a key feature for peering into dark rooms, or complexly shadowed environments like forests.

Pros:
* Almost certainly the best monitor at this price for the feature set (2K, 144Hz, Async)
* Amazing feature set for the price, and no goofy RGB, "gamer" stuff, icons, lights or anything like that
* Minimal setup and easy menus
* Excellent contrast
* Excellent response rate allows buttery smooth image without noticeable blur
* Automatic input detection

Cons:
* Menu buttons are on the back of the monitor, so it's hard to feel which button your fingers are placed on
* Ergonomics concerns if you're anal about monitor positioning, or need it to be vertical (you will need to buy a VESA stand)
* Minor light bleed in the corners, to be expected with an IPS panel. It's impossible to find a panel without it.

I bought the adjustable stand version monitor from another site. This is a good monitor if you are not expecting the best.
It does everything well. It is fairly color accurate out of the box. Not sure if it has an Srgb mode, but it does have a custom mode which is somewhat color accurate.
It all depends what you are comparing this monitor with. I have it placed next to an Asus P328Q which is a formidable AMVA panel. The LG is not quite as color accurate. The AMVA has deep blacks, and high contrast. The LG lacks both. Both monitors are 75hz.
I noticed a lot of backlight bleed on the lg especially from the bottom. I guess this is typical of IPS panels? Only one dead pixel. This is forgivable.
I think this lg can be further calibrated since there are ample settings to change RGB etc. I have not found any color profiles for it yet. I need to spend time trying to calibrate this monitor, to imitate the sRGB of the Asus monitor.
The LG has has different picture modes you would typically find on a tv. It has a simulated HDR mode, which simply brightens up the scenes. None of the monitors have good HDR at 300-400 nits anyway, this one is no exception. If you want to see good HDR buy an LCD tv with dimming zones.
It's funny but the AMVA panel (Asus P328Q) does not have HDR, but if you crank the brightness and contrast all the way up, it performs similarly to this monitor that does have HDR.
Windows 10 HDR does not work with this monitor, the colors look all washed out. You need to use it's own simulated HDR feature.
"All white' or "bright white" does not play well with this monitor, if your line of sight is directly in the middle of the monitor the edges look darker. This does not happen on the AMVA panel, Whites look uniform.
One thing that greatly annoys me is that LG have a software called "On screen control" You can change picture modes with this. If I set it on one picture, for example "HDR effect" then click on my game, it reverts back to custom settings. In other words it does not keep the setting you have asked for.
This software also has a menu "my application" presets. It would hypothetically allow you to attribute a picture mode to all the software programs you have on your pc. The problem is that Steam and all my games are missing from the list and I cannot add them. I would obviously have liked to attribute steam and games to FPS mode through the software automatically.
The only way to do this FPS selection, is load the game, and manually use the toggle switch on the monitor to select the mode. This defeats the purpose of having the software in the first place.
Gaming at 75hz is fine. This monitor is much darker than most other gaming monitors I have tried. So darker games are problematic.
I have always gamed at 75hz, and find it smooth. I do not turn on Vsync or Gsync. I just let my 2070 push higher frames. I see or feel no tearing.
I have tried all high end gaming monitors (144-165-240hz), and they are a little smoother sure, but they cost up to $900. Most gaming monitors are not nearly as color accurate as the Asus monitor. I have found a few 27" IPS panels that were quite color accurate, but contrast and black levels are poor.
The dell 32" (Dell S3220DGF) and the Samsung 32" (Odyssey) had much better brightness and contrast than this LG, however this LG is more color accurate for photo editing.
I comparing all these monitors to my reference Asus PG328Q which is considered a professional monitor.
To conclude. You are not going to find a color accurate monitor in 2020 that is perfect for gaming, has high refresh rate, deep blacks, high contrast, perfect Srgb.
With this logic in mind, you may as well buy this monitor since it's affordable, and wait until something else comes out.
I did find a near color accurate gaming monitor, but it was a 27" and I do not want to mix 27" and 32" together on the same desk. The ASUS TUF Gaming VG27AQL1A, was color accurate, had higher refresh rate, and was a tad brighter than this LG.
If you guys have any monitors you have tried that are color accurate, have higher than 75hz refresh rate, have deep blacks, high contrast, please let me know.

Plus:

This review is for the LG 34WQ650-W 34 Inch 21:9 UltraWide Full HD (2560 x 1080) 100Hz IPS Monitor delivered on time and in perfect condition from amazon. Included in the box is everything you need to get up and running, I have it connected to my Windows 11 workstation, no drivers were required or installed for this monitor to work perfectly.

This monitor is FHD, not QHD, and a flat screen, not curved like some of the gaming monitors that are out there... makes this monitor perfect for a home workstation to give you a little more real estate to accomplish tasks. the text is perfect and the monitors color palette was dead on and needed no adjustments by me to get my workspace up to date - literally plug and play! It has included speakers which are by no means loud, but as a home workstation completely acceptable, I did not use the included stand, I have this mounted to a adjustable arm and it has a standard VESA 100x100 mount.

This is a quality IPS screen, which syncs perfectly to my Nvidea G-Sync card at 100hz, and windows recognized the HDR 400 in the Windows 11 settings just as advertised.

Nothing not to like - Highly recommend this monitor to all!
Product works great

Pretty stoked on this purchase. Worth what I paid and is perfect for a regular/medium sized desk. Overall really happy with the purchase BUT beware the monitor doesn’t tilt on the base so sometimes I need to prop it up to make it easier on the eyes.

monitor worked fine for about 2 months then all of a sudden the side buttons started failing. Powers on and then I can get to the menu but after that any button I press exits me out of the screen. Must be faulty wiring. Absolutely nothing I press will fix this issue.

So overall I love the resolution and the color and contrast of the monitor is great.

What bothers me is I had to get the 1st one exchanged because of a bad pixel. The second one seems to have a bit of backlight bleed in the bottom right corner but I may just keep it. I ended up totally skipping the hub and just leave it unplugged with mouse and keyboard directly connected to the PC itself.

Overall I'm happy with my purchase going from a dell u2412M, but the u2412m seems to be a bit more of a polished product.

I'm pretty please with my purchase. I was wanting a cheap monitor to keep at home to use for my side computer repair business.

This monitor has both HDMI and VGA connection, so that's convenient since I deal with both new and older PCs. It comes with a VGA cable. I am always down for having an extra cable around, but I would guess most people would rather have an HDMI cable included.

The base on this thing is really solid. You have to screw on the base, but a pretty neat little screwdriver is included to help you get the job done. While I wouldn't describe the overall picture as remarkable, for $99 it makes for a solid display if you are looking for a budget monitor. The blacks could be darker and other color more vibrant, but that's not really of any significance for me considering how I am using it. Also, I was able to adjust the monitor settings using the side buttons and my picture is much crisper and looks much cleaner than when I first plugged it in after unboxing it. I found the menu button system on the monitor to be better than most and easy to navigate.

Although it has an HDMI connection and although there are audio jacks, this monitor does not have speakers built-in. I'm guessing the audio jacks are so you can plug in some external speakers. Perhaps the sound is carried from the PC via the HDMI and then out to your external speaker that are connected to the audio jack? Just a hunch. I haven't tested it yet. This would make it a little more convenient to plug-in external speakers since you wouldn't have to crawl around on the floor to do so if you have a tower case.

One other reviewer mentioned that the metal making up the bezel around the outside of the screen is not flush all the way around. I noticed this minor issue as well, but for the price I'm not expecting perfection. I did dock it a star on the "material quality" feature for this issue, but I don't feel this should detour you from purchasing this monitor if you need something cheap.

All-in-all, a very solid monitor for the price.

clear picture, amazing size!

Having used several different generations and sizes of cintiqs at work, I've become entrenched in the wacom world, but I've never been enthralled with the price tag. This is why I've held off for so long on buying my own. I read all the encouraging and thorough reviews on the older yiynova, and was excited when I read that a lot of the issues of the v1 had been fixed in the v2. I figured I'd give it a try. Why not, when it costs half as much?

I've owned it for about 3 weeks now, and I haven't been disappointed.

I like how bright the screen is: it feels brighter than the cintiq. I haven't had any issues with the values when switching an illustration between my yiynova and my mac.

I like the angle adjustment much better than the 24hd cintiq. I also like that it's light enough that I can put it in my lap.

I prefer the slippery glass surface of the yiynova to the matte texture of the cintiq: I wear a knit glove with all but the pinky finger cut off to keep my skin from giving friction on the screen.

The color isn't perfect, but it's close enough. I spent about an hour color calibrating between my mac and the yiynova when I first set it up, and I got it very close. The warm colors, especially reds, don't display quite properly on the yiynova, but I've calibrated it close enough that as long as I get my values and color harmonies working properly, the color doesn't bother me when I drag my illustrations from one screen to another.

I would actually prefer that the screen be about 10% more pressure sensitive: but that's because I have a super light hand. I think this would be ideal for people who tend to push really hard when they draw.

I've never used the buttons on the cintiq screen. or the eraser on the pen. So the fact that the Yiynova doesn't have them doesn't bother me. I'm a hot key fiend. I set up an apple trackpad and another apple keyboard with my screen so that my laptop just becomes the second monitor.

The holder for the pen is trash. It's a cheap plastic holder that's supposed to notch into a slot in the back of the screen. The stupid little notch doesn't fit very well, and keeps falling off. So I just keep my pen on my desk, stored flat. It's not ideal, but then again, I never used those little wacom pen holders either. So it doesn't bother me. Whatever.

The screen can get warm at the white non-screen strip at the bottom, but i use it for like 8 hours in a row or longer. It never gets uncomfortably warm.

I like it. I'm perfectly happy with it. It fulfills every need I have as an illustrator, and it cost me half as much as the cintiq would have. The only question now is whether this sucker can stand up to several years of constant use. I'll keep updating if I see any issues.
Monitor constantly loses power and i have to unplug from the back ans then back in again. This not used for gaming but just every day use when operating laptop

I bought this product at Fry's Electronics in Renton Washington

Anyways, I see a lot of negative reviews and I guess your experience may vary from screen to screen..

The screen is a Samsung curved panel (FYI).. anyways.

I'm using a DP cable connected to an RX Vega 64 and I'm getting silky smooth frames at 144hz with freesync on (for those that don't like to figure stuff out and be spoon fed information you need to turn on freesync on the monitor for it to be enabled). I really have no complaints other than the minimal screen bleeding I've seen. There's 2 spots top and bottom where the focal of the curves are that give off a small amount of bleeding. I haven't seen any dead pixels and I've had this monitor or about 2 months now. There are modes you can select: standard, FPS, RTS. But I believe standard is the best image quality you'll get where the other two change the color schemes up too much for my taste. There's no built in speakers but I use a headset anyways so its all good. The price is very good for a 144hz freesync monitor from my stand point.
