Would recommend.
It’s a great monitor but it doesn’t have very good audio. That doesn’t matter for me though because I just use headphones.

The over quality and features are really good. But the issue is to get the colors to look good requires alot of setup. Tons of trial and error i had to do. The screen will look washed out by default. No settings in the OSD will make it look good. Eventhough there are “saturation” settings. Those saturations are for the 3 indiviual colors. So setting them to 100 looks just like it does at 50. I ended up having to adjust the colors and turn down the backlight in my video card settings. Never had to do that for literally any monitor i have ever owned. I have ALWAYS gotten the colors to look good by the OSD. For a $750 piece of tech, that is not good. The auto dimming function is also not great. When it kicks on, the white in letters turns greyish and gets bold making it hard to see. I was on the brink of returning it until i found the setting. Its in the “setup” menu instead of the picture menu. It affects the picture, why isnt that setting with the other picture settings? Why do i need to go digging thru menus to disable a horrible feature?

For a comparison, i have an acer monitor that looked washed out too. But i was able to get that to look good just by the OSD. So those settings will stay even if i reinstall drivers or redo my computer. With this samsung, i will have to put those settings back. And they are both at 10bit full 4:4:4 color and the cheaper one was easier to work with.

I really do like the monitor. I just hate that the thing costs $750 AND i had to go thru a lot of hassle just to get it to look good. For that much money samsung could have atleast included the settings needed to make the monitor look good, instead of being lazy and requiring the tweaking of the color settings in the video card software.

I was stoked when this monitor arrived. I thought I'd done enough research and bought a good product, and now I was going to be rewarded with 27" of new desktop space. But the damn thing didn't work. I received it on a Friday afternoon, and by the time I got it all connected and seen the problem, BenQ tech support was already gone for the weekend. I talked to an Apple support rep instead, and confirmed that the problem was not with my MacBook Pro -- I screen-shared with him, and he could see the whole second desktop. On my end I saw only my laptop monitor and a big, brand-new, totally un-illuminated black screen. On Monday morning I called BenQ tech support and told the guy what was going on. He said, "yeah, it might just be a dead panel." I asked, "So this is a problem happens often enough that you have a name for it?" He said, "No no, it's just, you were unlucky and got the 1% of monitors where this happens." I told him maybe I should have read more Amazon customer reviews, because it looked like it might be a little more than 1%. So I returned it to Amazon. It'll take longer, but I'd rather get my $ back and buy a different brand than roll the dice on another one of the same BenQ product.

I have bought this monitor after reading the excellent reviews. Indeed, It is a nice monitor, overall I am satisfied with the purchase. I will start off by stating the cons :
* Pricey
* Backlight bleed (Inherent with IPS panels)
* Menu is clunky and takes getting use to
* You need to have a large table if planning to use the display with the supplied Stand
* Has only 1 display port and 1 HDMI port
Pros:
* Nvidia G-Sync ( a huge plus if you have a Nvidia graphics card)
* 100Hz display ( you will notice the difference immediately after over clocking the display to 100Hz)
* Beautiful color reproduction
* Fast response time
* The curve display is a gimic but looks cools
* Onboard speakers are adequate if you arent listening to music or playing games for watching youtube/facebook videos they are fine.
* The LED strip at the bottom of the display is a nice touch now I can match the display accent lighting to my computers lighting
* The 4 usb 3.0 ports on the display are much appreciated you can never have enough usb ports
Let me tell you why I chose this display, I knew it was pricey but the 2 most important things to me were G-Sync and 100Hz display because I have an EVGA 1080 FTW graphics card. If you have an AMD card I would suggest you stick to Acer Predator Z35 which is $400 cheaper than this display nvidia is charging a premium for their G-sync technology. The backlight bleed does not bother me, I have been using IPS displays for the last 13 years and not once did I see an IPS monitor without backlight bleed, the backlight bleed one the X34 predator is on an acceptable level...well its much better than the 1st batch of 2015 displays I was lucky enough to get a June 2016 manufactured model.
I primarily use the X34 predator for gaming so I dont mind having one displayport and one HDMI port. I also use external speakers for sound and headphones for competitive online gaming so I dont care about the onboard speakers.
All in all I am very happy with it except the price if you are thinking of getting it ...get it you wont regret it just make sure you get a may-june 2016 manufactured model because all the issues reported with the monitor have been fixed with the latest iterations of this display.

Knock off 3 stars for the poor audio design, which renders the built-in speakers unusable for all practical purposes.

When sound to the display speakers are over HDMI or Display Port, it disables the Mac keyboard volume controls (F10 to F12 keys). So, master volume can only be adjusted by accessing the little toggle on the bottom of the display. Which in my case is not easy since I cannot use the stand, must lift or tilt the display. I have the display sitting without the stand, which is very stable, but the bottom is where the speakers and toggle are. With the stand installed, it sits too high and will not adjust low enough for me.

Porting the sound over Bluetooth does allow the use of the Mac keyboard volume controls (F10 to F12 keys), however, it does not remain connected. And re-connection is an absurdly tedious multi-step process: first, click "connect" in the BT menu on the Mac, then wait for several seconds for LG's on-screen display to ask are you sure? yes/no, to which you must respond by quickly accessing the display toggle within a few seconds. If you don't get it in time, you must start over. If it was a more permanent connection, then this would be a perfectly acceptable way to port the sound to the display speakers. The tedious connection process would be a fairly minor inconvenience if you only had to do it once. But, having to reconnect BT every time you want to play something with sound is completely impractical.

The display's jack port is only "out". So there's no other way to get sound out of the display speakers. I plan to return.

I got this because I was tired of having a big screen at my computer desk behind the couch at my house, and wanted something I could travel with also. This works like a champ on my chromebook, and also works on all of my laptops. It really was plug and play. It is a single wire operation for my chromebook, meaning I don't have to hook it up to power, and it worked with HDMI and usb-c to my one laptop, and my second laptop I hooked up the HDMI and had to hook it into the power block that it came with. The one thing that really surprised me, which honestly I was not expecting, was how thin it is. It even comes with a nice little sleeve. The only thing that concerns me is that it is so thin and I can just throw it in my laptop bag, and it might break. To solve that, I put it in the same case that my Chromebook is in to give it a little bit of extra protection. I don't know what everybody is talking about it being dim, because as you can see, it is just as bright, if not brighter, then my chromebook. The other neat thing I did not even think about until I got it, was my wife and I can watch the same movie on separate screens. We spent the night at a hotel out of town last week, and we were each able to hold a screen to watch a movie. (My eleys are bad, so need it farther away)  I would not have figured this out, but I could not figure out how to cast my screen to the hotel tv. All in all, this was a great find for me, and now I am just a bit more productive on the road with work as I have two screens now. One little tidbit of tech support help would be to make sure you change the resolution to match what your computer or Chromebook is. I got really frustrated and thought I did something wrong because my mouse was moving super slow. Turned out I had the resolution set super high. I just dialed it down to what my Chromebook resolution was, and the mouse worked without any problems at all. Oh, this did come with 2 usb-c cables, HDMI cable, and power brick, which I was not expecting the power brick.  It also came with a USB c adapter.

Ive noticed that there are too many reviews with incorrect/uneducated information. Im going to break this down simply.

Requirements:
In order to get 240 HZ 10 Bit color you need DSC (display port compression) graphics card otherwise you are limited to 8 bit. This also has to be set manually in the NVIDIA / AMD control panels.

GPU capable of even achieving 240HZ and if you plan to game with this depending on the game /settings you may need a very high end GPU to hit high enough in the fps range to utilize as much as the refresh rate advantage as possible.

Proper display port cables/ The ones it comes with from the OEM.

Issues:
Flickering - Completely resolved by the latest firmware update as near as i can tell. Latest firmware update contains a VRR control that you must set in the monitor menu.

Backlight Bleed - its there and very apparent when the screen is completely black. Its not oled and its not FALD. It does have light zones though. This should be a non issue for the actual target reason to buy this monitor.

Stuck Pixels - I got one with stuck pixels. It wasnt just one it was several in different areas. Its why i bought directly from amazon. Shipped it back. Got a different one without the issues.

Panels Broken in shipping - Yes Samsung has an issue with this. 1000R curves will break at a high percentage during shipping. No real way around this due to the curve and carrier handling. If you can buy from a big box store that sells them regularly in store. they usually are shipped on pallets together and arent as mishandled by carriers.

"My mac cannot detect this all the time Bro.. I have to unplug the cable and plug it back in" This at the current time appears to be true. though for some reason apple cannot ever seem to get this simple thing that windows has been doing since the 90s right. Its a problem for lots of monitors on their latest build of Big Sur and its a problem on their new apple silicone(m1) with their own cpu/gpu. I observed it on my own mac mini with the m1 PROC.

No ultimate gysnc module - Yep it doesnt have it. Look up the BOM for it on the internet. then ask yourself would you pay maybe double for this monitor to have that module? Ive noticed no gsync issues with monitor and i personally cannot tell any justifiable difference between this and gysnc ultimate on a x27 predator i owned.

Samsung service is terrible - In my individual opinion they are. Every comment online i have read about interacting with them is terrible. Its why i bought from amazon and made sure all was working.

Slight vertical lines( non dead/ on broken pixel rows) - Yes this VA panel may have them. They show up in certain colors and brightness's in certain spots of the monitor. These are extremely subtle and i doubt most people will notice unless they really are looking or super uptight about quality.

Rumored issues:

Ghosting - Your high if you think so. Look at the Youtuve videos of the UFO test. This thing performs and has low response times. gtg 1ms

HDR - Its HDR 600 and no FALD. They dont advertise it as FALD. HDR is noticeable and makes a difference in some games. Its not going to wow you and this isnt an issue with this monitor. HDR works at pretty much the level your paying for in my opinion.

So why the five star review?

1. Curve is 1000R. For me life changing eye comfort.
2. 240 HZ 10 Bit color. Smoothest gameplay at 1440P i Have ever seen. If you can run at 240 FPS the game your playing its beautifully smooth. (There isnt a huge difference between 144hz and 240 that i can see but its still awesome)
3. 240 HZ productivity. window movement and any motion that can take advantage is beautifully smooth.
4. RGB lighting is nice
5. Color is nice
6. HDR while not the FALD 1000 NIT wow factor its i think pretty good for what you pay for.
7. Overall design... Very nice. stands out.
8. Power cable length. I have a slightly higher desk and lots of my cables don't reach. this one did.
9. Dual Display
10. Settings to adjust for gaming/response/performance/etc.
11. Monitor button. Very nice to switch between two computers.
12 Bezel... its not thing and technically its a bezel inside of a bezel which is common to give the appearance of a smaller bezel.
13. Mine included a vesa mount plate.
14. Super USB charging

Is this monitor perfect? no its not. Is this monitor catering to something uniquely awesome 1000R 240hz 10 Bit color? Yes it is.

I love this monitor. you can nitpick all the imperfections and obsess over them. When using this monitor I don't even want to ever touch another x27 predator or a asus pg27uq(regarded as the best monitor ever made by many). Reality has become this is my go to monitor.

TLDR: If the price is good, and the resolution is good buy it. It's a fantastic piece of HD glass in a box. You'll need speakers, but monitor speakers usually suck.

As much as I wanted to love this monitor based off the specs, I just can't live with the major flaw in the sampleI received.

About 1 cm from the bezel on every side of the screen there is a noticeably darker area where the contrast is much lower than the rest of the screen. I tested with multiple sources and connections, all showed the same results. Its as if this 35 in monitor has a 34 inch backlight. The much less expensive Acer ED7 34 inch curved monitor I am testing against does not display this problem.

Additionally the height adjustment of the included stand is very limited in range and does not swivel, which the ED7 does. The bottom bezel is much thicker than the sides or top, taking a bit away from the near bezel-free look.

One of the main reasons I purchased this monitor is the HDR feature. My HDR10 4K Samsung TV is quite stunning with HDR source material so being an video/audio enthusiast I wanted this feature for a computer monitor I spend so much time with. I knew going on that this monitor does not have a high contrast ratio needed for HDR nor does it have local dimming zones needed to truly create the effect. Nevertheless the visual impact of HDR material is noticeable and impressive on this monitor.

However the black box 1 cm from the edge of the bezel surrounding this screen kills it for me. Just too noticeable and jarring. As a sanity check I had my wife sit and look at the monitor with me and she also noticed it immediately as well, and she's not normally one to notice these sort of things that I nitpick at in TVs/monitors.

Will be returning this to Amazon.

If anyone has a suggestion for a freesync 35 inch UWQHD curved monitor with at least 100 Hz refresh rate and HDR compatibility please let me know!

I'm glad I chose this one, and it achieves everything I wanted in a display.

It's a little problematic though, because using Windows at 144Hz makes my 60Hz display feel laggy. People who use my computer then go back to their 60Hz displays at home always tell me they didn't realize how much smoother mine was until they used their 60Hz one. Going to a higher frame rate is really one of those "you don't notice until it's gone" things.

There's also a stated 1 year dead pixel warranty. This monitor I'm using has no dead pixels, which is good to start. Hopefully that lasts, and if any show up, hopefully the warranty isn't bad. This is a great monitor for the price! It compares favorably, picture wise, to my $500 ViewSonic 27" 4K monitor. I ordered it as a second monitor and the contrast and sharpness is a good match. Of course, I use them in 2048x1152 mode instead of 4k mode, but I cannot read 4k mode anyway! It streams videos just as well with no noticeable lag.
Pros
- 2 HDMI and 1 DP inputs
- sturdy, swiveling base that conserves desktop space and is flat on top so you can set your phone cradle, etc. on it
- easily adjustable height from 2" to 7"
- standard VESA 100 mount and stand. You can use any VESA 100 stand/mount with it and use the included stand with any other VESA 100 monitor. (The mount point on the monitor is recessed so a mount with a large plate may have to be installed outside of the recess with longer screws.)
- the bezel is about 2mm which protects the edge of the front "glass" but looks good. I prefer this since I have had edge-to-edge monitors fall and bump something and crack the front plate.
Cons
- strange, hard to reach, joystick control on the back of the monitor to change settings. I'm not sure why they did this but I have too much stuff on my desktop to try to reach behind my monitor to change settings. It would be impossible to reach in an enclosure.
- the monitor defaulted to turning off after an inactivity period, so it would turn off while I was watching a movie on my other monitor and I would have to reach behind it and press the menu button to turn it back on. I found this "feature" in the menu and disabled it, so this is now a non-issue.

I did not test gaming with the monitor but saw no noticeable blurring in videos so think it would do well. I think it is a good value for a Samsung 27" monitor!

...with the Quicksilver/Mirror Mac especially flavored Apple logo. The Cube is the Mac I use it with and I wanted to report a bizarre flaw coming from something Apple did to cut costs (while advertising they did the opposite) combined with user error resulting from trusting Apple.

The flaw Apple engineered into these displays is that Apple sat there and made the ADC connector's USB signal run off of the built in USB port number 1 while advertising (except in the finest of print that I couldn't find out until it was too late) that the Studio Display would not obstruct any of the user facing USB ports.

I admit I read the advertising materials more than the fine print, but to see the Cube suddenly lose all USB port power to avoid the USB controller blowing out and having to unplug the computer and plug it back in and then not be able to use all of the desired hardware (I wanted to use a Zip drive, and a DeskJet 970Cse with it) because of the fact that the monitor and speakers take up one whole USB bus's power by themselves leaving only one port available for everything else (which if you don't hook a keyboard and mouse up, you're hosed) is not going to cut the mustard.

I still love the display, and I still love the Cube, but the fact that you get what you pay for as regards the free advertising materials leaves me wanting to find something that "just works" like the Cube, but that allows me to fully use the computer without spending money on a second vintage USB hub that completely destroys the reason why an individual would even choose the stylish Cube over a not so stylish minitower.

I know I'm working with vintage software and vintage hardware, but every time I want to buy into the Apple Cool-Aid (vintage, modern, or retro) it seems like I have some reason to be disappointed, but especially with anything that has to run X, which since my 17-inch Studio Display can't run in Mac OS 9 below 9.2.2, then I can't sit there and install 9.0.4 like what the Cube came with and Mac OS X might as well be an error of SOD (i.e. Windows with an Apple logo on it) so I use as old a version as I possibly can and it doesn't get used very often.)

I just wanted to alert other potential 17-inch Apple Studio Display buyers to what they can face with this monitor even in a "preferred" application, rather than trying to make it work with a beige G3 (what a nightmare that would be.)

Bought a pair of these to put on a dual mount VESA stand at my workstation. Several models of Dell monitors require an accessory VESA mount adapter which adds about $30 to the cost of each monitor, so I spent a lot of time culling the herd down to this monitor which has a 100mm VESA pad build in. As packed in the box, the monitor's VESA pad is exposed and basically ready to use. it includes four screws, already screwed in. These screws might look like they're holding the monitor together, but just unscrew them and use them for the mount, or use whatever screws might come with your VESA stand. The included desktop stand comes in two pieces, to be snapped together and then attached to the monitor, plus a cover for the VESA pad. Obviously mine are still in the boxes, unused.

Ironically, the instructions that come with the monitor say nothing about VESA mounting.

This is my first review, and I am writing it because I am very impressed. I also never buy refurbished, but at more than half off the price, it was totally worth it.

I recently upgraded my MacBook Pro to the 2018 with quad core. I was using an old 1080p Samsung HDMI monitor. It was not a great monitor, the text was not smooth, the display was okay, but it was not pleasant on my eyes for any long period. I remember when Apple began selling the LG monitors, even at the introductory discounted prices it was too expensive. So I had that Samsung sitting on my desk and periodically used it for when I needed 2 screens to work with. Other than that, I primarily used the built in display. It was always so much easier on the eyes.

The last time I used the Samsung, my eyes were so fatigued, I decided I was done with it. I went to the Apple Store not long after and looked at the 4K and 5K LG Ultrafine. The 5K was way too large and way expensive for my needs, but the 4K was the perfect size. The quality was beautiful! It felt like I was looking at a larger version of the incredible screen on my MacBook Pro. Unfortunately, it was still way out of my price range. I searched a number of sites for different prices, and nobody discounted off the $699 MSRP. Refurb prices were typically over $400, just outside of what I was looking to spend. So I saved it in my cart hoping the price would come down. At one point, I saw it for $279 and was ready to pull the trigger. I was just about ready to check out, but I wanted to look at the reviews again. Something had to be wrong with it at that price, and I wanted to see feedback on refurbished versions. When I made up my mind to finish the checkout, they were gone from that seller and the price was back up to $400. I kept it in my cart and checked prices daily. After a month, I saw them come back up at the $279. I immediately checked out, and 2 days later a large box was at my doorstep.

I was expecting a generic blank box with styrofoam and wrapping. I opened the huge Amazon box and was pleasantly surprised to see the original packaging. There was a white label that said refurbished, and that would be the only hint that it was not brand new. I opened that box and everything was carefully covered with protective sheets so nothing would be disturbed or damaged during shipping. I unwrapped everything and looked over the entire unit, there was absolutely no signs of usage anywhere on it. It came with all the cables and plate, also carefully packaged and wrapped in its own box. I placed the monitor on the desk, plugged it in, and everything worked as it should. The last test was opening my photos app and testing the display with multiple color screens I use for all devices I get with color displays. There are no dead or stuck pixels, everything works precisely as it should.

The USB-C Hub is the other perk to this monitor. I plug my MacBook Pro into this monitor, and it charges it. I plugged in a USB-C SSD Hard Drive that I use for Time Machine Backups, and it just works. No need to store it away anymore, I am keeping that drive plugged in. There are 2 additional USB-C plugs available if I need them.

I also like that you can adjust the height on this monitor. I like to stand sometimes when typing, it's nice that I can adjust it or swivel it up or down no matter what position I am working.

Being that this was made for Apple computing, the OS works fluidly with this display. No buttons on the display needed, I use the software to adjust brightness and volume. And thats the last part of this review, the sound quality is great. I watched a couple of 4K movies from my MacBook on this monitor, and it sounded great! The volume is nice and clear, no static or buzz on certain sounds.

I am 100% happy with this monitor. If you own a MacBook or MacBook Pro with USB-C, and can get it for the right price, you really can't go wrong with buying this refurbished. I am looking forward to years of enjoyment out of this.

The User Guide claims that you can enter and save ten custom settings, but offers no details on how to do that.  [One of my systems didn't match; I was able to connect an older monitor (which automatically connected correctly) and see what settings it chose.]  I tried to contact HP Support via their web page (to try to find documentation on how to make custom settings).  [The URL in the manual was bogus/outdated.]  The HP support page insisted on a serial number and product ID, but rejected the product ID (which I got off the sticker on the back of the two E190i monitors that I bought).  The HP Support page wouldn't let me go any further without an acceptable PID.  It also recommended logging in.  I happened to have an account, so I did log in.  It made no difference.  So, depending on what you try to connect this to, you might be golden or you might find that you have a shiny new doorstop.

There have been countless reviews here about how great this monitor is, and that is true. So rather than write yet another similar review, this one is specifically for the MacBook Pro users who may be searching for an external monitor.

For a MacBook Pro user, it's important to know the "Display Port-to-Thunderbolt Port" cable included with this monitor is all you need. I attached this cable to my MacBook Pro and instantly it worked perfectly in the clamshell mode. No adjustments are need regarding the display arrangement in Preferences, as is the case with some external monitors. Also, the MacBook Pro (with Yosemite installed) recognized even the model number of the Dell monitor and automatically adjusted from its Retina display resolution to the full 1920 x 1080 resolution of this monitor.

Speaking of resolution, many Mac users will want to know that fonts on this display at 1920 x 1080 can be much easier to read than the Apple's own 27" Thunderbolt Display. I recently purchased a 27" iMac and lived with the small font sizes at its 2560 x 1440 resolution for a few months before buying a MacBook Pro and this monitor. It's not enough to just go down one notch in resolution on the 27" iMac as it then looks horrible when out of its native resolution. This is something most users find out only after they've purchased it.

For audio options with this monitor and your MacBook Pro, again OS X automatically recognizes the exact model number of this Dell (U2414H) and provides it as an option it the OS X audio preferences. Then all you need to do is plug in your favorite speakers into the speaker jack on the rear of the monitor and you have great sound, freeing up the headphone jack on your MacBook Pro.

If anyone is skeptical about picture clarity with this monitor...find something else to worry about :-) The display quality is every bit as good as the Apple Thunderbolt Display and for me, a plus was the matte finish of this display so I don't have reflections like I had on the 27" iMac. I normally don't care for matte finish displays, but for some reason, this one doesn't bother me...in fact I like it a lot.

Another great feature of this monitor is the easily accessed settings for anything you could dream of adjusting (unlike the Thunderbolt Display). I was able to adjust my brightness and contrast separately to suit my office environment. Bottom line is if you're a MacBook Pro user and want a great monitor you can use by itself, or daisy chain, this should be one of your fist choices.

I just received this monitor a week ago. It was easy to install, the screen brightness has been sufficient, great product for the money and am glad I purchased it. It also came with screen protectors, cleaning cloth and a carrying case.

I was wary of this monitor a bit after reading several reviews talking about the heavy anti-glare coating on the screen, rendering the monitor useless as the coating was so distracting to the user. My experience has been that, yes, there is a SLIGHT coating on the screen that I can BARELY see when my face is about 4 inches from the monitor itself AND I am working in a dark room. The reality of my situation is that I work during the day, in a brightly lit room that has several windows. When I am working in these conditions, the anti-glare coating does what it is supposed to do, and is not visible to me. I keep the monitor at 100% brightness and level 100 contrast as I work mostly in Excel, read PDFs, and browse the web. I feel like this monitor was made for an office environment, and it executes its purposes flawlessly. Additionally, at the time of this review, this monitor is going for $205 and free shipping with Prime. Cannot beat that.

PROS:
- Viewing angles. Thanks to the IPS display
- Super bright. Excellent for working during the day in a well lit room
- Configurations. This monitor can rotate, twist, and raise/lower like a champ
- Size. For me - 23" @ 1920X1080 keeps text size just right, but pixel density good enough for amazing photo and video clarity.
- Colors. Comes out of the box almost perfectly color calibrated, Very impressed with this from an office monitor.
- Menu. Super easy, making adjustments (brightness, contrast) take less than 3 seconds due to the well designed menu interface.

CONS:
- Bulky bezel. While not major or that noticeable, this monitors bezels are thick and chunky, which is not super aesthetically pleasing.

Overall I am very satisfied with this monitor, and it will serve its purpose well for the next several years.

Purchased both the U3011 and the U3014, the U3014 had a super sales price at the time.  I received the U3011 first so I had a good amount of time to spend with it.  The U3011's older technology was a bit of a turn off but after using it for a while I got used to it really fast.  The U3011 is a beautiful monitor with great color (once it warms up).  4K content looks absolutely fantastic, as does 1080p movies and high resolution photos.  Played Bio Shock Infinite at 2560x1600 and the colors were breath taking.  Short comings are the delay when the CCFL tubes warm up, the slightest lag in very fast moving games if not using display port.  If you're use to glossy screens forget it, this looks a little dull in comparison, the higher quality screen makes up for it though.  No UBS 3.0 and none of the latest tech you'll find in the U3014.  Now for the U3014.  That display was a horrible disaster!  had the U3011 and the U3014 side by side and the U3014 stuttered when running Valve's Lost Cost fly over and it didn't have the U3011's color saturation, both were set on the Game setting.  The U3014's settings constantly shifted by itself. Before the weekend was over the U3014 developed horizontal lines.  The U3014 was brighter and cooler, had USB 3.0 and a host of fancy feature that some may like.  I personally would not touch it with a ten foot pole, what a piece of junk.  It might have been from an earlier production run with immature firmware, some have not experienced what I did with the U3014 and some have experienced worse.  Needless to say, I decided to keep the U3011. I haven't tried to register it with Dell yet but I've heard mixed reviews about that.  All in all, the U3011 has been a joy to own and I say if you can afford it you'll love it as well.  Cheers!

This monitor has worked well for me as a stand-alone touch panel, but it has one major flaw: touch input does not work correctly in a dual-monitor setup. Since the touch panel is a separate USB device on touch-enabled monitors, it is normal for a multi-monitor user to have to go through a setup process in the Windows control panel to pair them correctly. For whatever reason, that doesn't work for this monitor. All touches act on the primary panel only, no matter which you actually touch. I called Acer technical support, and they confirmed (in veiled, careful words) that this monitor indeed does not support touch in a dual-monitor setup. They offered no impending driver or firmware fix.

I would have liked to give this product only two stars because it is useless in the application for which I bought it, but to be fair, it functions perfectly well in a single-monitor setup. The built-in speakers are awful, but the display is bright, the reclining stand is sturdy and the touch panel is responsive. Just steer clear of this one if you ever might want to put two side-by-side.

I've had this for a week so can't comment on long term use. But I'll provide my initial impressions and my experience of setting up and using the Dell UltraSharp UP2414Q for the first time. I have used it on a 2012 Macbook Pro 15" and a top spec 2014 Macbook Pro 15" with additional graphics card. Here's my Dell UltraSharp UP2414Q 4K Monitor Review.

In both cases, it was seamless to connect and use it. The supplied displayport cable was all I needed. In both cases, it was 100% plug and play. The monitor automatically went to optimal "retina-style" display. I was pleasantly surprised that the older machine worked because I had been led to believe that only late-2013 MBPs worked. But it was fine, running at 30Hz refresh. On the 2014 MBP it was the exact same experience except once I set the monitor to displayport 1.2 it switched to 60Hz.

Colours were ok, but I did calibration and have it very closely matched to the laptop as I find its colours very accurate and pleasing to view.

My experience so far is solely for business use, graphic design and web browsing. Adobe Creative Suite and Lightroom, Word and all the i-apps. I was looking for a "match" to my Macbook Pro's monitor, something larger and screen-extending, but not too large. I found my previous 27" too much, and this 24" suits me just fine. Lots of real estate to work with but not overwhelming.

Text is crisp. Colours are consistent around the screen. Brightness and contrast can be well controlled across a wide range.

For whatever it's worth, the physical design of the Dell monitor complements the Apple computer very well. Similar black rounded bezel and brushed aluminum stand. The monitor itself is very light. The stand is solid and heavy like it should be. I've had good experiences with two previous Dell higher end monitors over the past seven years. Hope this continues the trend.

To conclude, if you're wondering if 4K displays have "arrived" as a suitable "retina" extension of your Macbook Pro, I'd say they have. Shop around. Found mine for $500. That's an incredible value. Even at $800 it's an excellent monitor purchase. (Long-term reliability review withheld until much further notice.) For now, great.

I've had this monitor for nearly a year and it hasn't had any defects. The controls are a godsend, and if I had my way, they'd be mandatory on every TV and computer monitor. Okay, I kid, but seriously, the controls are a revelation. The colors are pretty vivid, and I never find myself wanting a brighter backlight. For the price, it's an excellent choice for gaming. The wide aspect ratio gives you a huge advantage I'm first person shooters, though the 60Hz refresh rate may leave you wanting a little more. Fantasy titles and RPGs really pop on this display too. Be warned though - you might want to upgrade your graphics card if you expect to be able to play on the same settings if you're upgrading from a lower resolution.
For work, this could possibly replace a dual monitor setup, depending on your needs. If you need just a little extra horizontal space, it's perfect. But if you do a lot of graphics work or computer programming, you might find yourself a little cramped. The matte finish is an attractive feature if you spend a lot of time staring at your screen, due to the reduces glare. The colors don't seem faded on it either, which is nice. The viewing angles are solid, and overall, it's a great upgrade if you're still using a lower resolution. Admittedly, it looks a bit expensive on paper, and maybe you do have to pay a premium for it being a niche product, but displays are a personal item so what works for one person might not for another. Try it first if you can. If not and you're still considering it, I highly recommend it.

It took me a day and a half to put this monitor together. Thank Goodness for the yellow button in my orders that says Get Product Support. I also emailed the sender, MasterTronics because my desktop didn't come with a cd player and I needed the urls for downloading a few of the things I needed that were on the cd. They also provided me with the User Manual. I not only chatted with tech support but they called me too. My Windows 10 did not require all of the cords and plugs they sent. My computer did not have a VGA input (look it up; in fact look up all the inputs which I did to see what they looked like). I did happen to have an HDMI input on my computer; I also have  a DisplayPort input and a DVI-I port. HP does not supply an HDMI input cable. I got mine at Fry's for $4 and that seems to be adequate because I had the DisplayPort inputs attached and on page 8 of the manual you'll see the monitor chooses which inputs to choose. Except every time it chose the DisplayPort, my mouse wouldn't work. So I took it out. The conflicting data is between the Manual and the large paper covering the monitor called Quick Start Guide. The Manual tells you to plug in all the inputs. The Guide has you choose (it skips DisplayPort to HDMI to VGA from #3 to #4). You must plug in the cord for #4 called a USB type A to USB type C (the type we all know). USB type C plugs into a regular USB port, not a blue one (blue ones are 3.0 ports). If you have a VGA input then get the sound cable which is NOT included (buy it at Fry's with your HDMI cable). I learned it is called a male-to-male cable but I don't know which color port to plug it into. I guess you should chat or call tech support. Oh, and on that note, two things: 1) If you contact HP, they'll want your serial #. I found mine under the metal base "SN:" 2) you can test your video on Skype. The video is pretty good.If you check the answered questions, the warranty is 3 years. My warranty books were all in Spanish. Well, good luck!

I have been waiting awhile to write a review on this monitor, solely to do it justice. Well, I'm at 325+ back light hours and I think I am ready. I will create sub categories so you will have the ability to read my opinion on certain parts of this monitor or as a whole.

PHYSICAL APPEARANCE:
I'm not going to lie, one of the biggest reasons why I purchased this monitor was it's appearance. I wanted something that looked nice while it was off. Previously, I have owned Apple products and monitors and this one fits right in; it even compliments my Apple keyboards. Overall, I would give the appearance a solid 5/5. It looks and feels sturdy, you will be proud to have this monster sitting on your desk.

BUILD QUALITY:
This thing is heavy, I'm talking 20+ pounds. The stand is solid aluminium and has some heft to it. Sure, it has it's cheaper plastic bits but overall it is a nice build.

BEATS AUDIO SPEAKERS:
There's not much to say here... It's Beats Audio; their headphones are not to impress and these monitor speakers are not quite impressive either. Yeah, yeah... They're better than most any other monitor speakers, nevertheless, still terrible. Do not buy this monitor for an amazing in depth audio experience.

DISPLAY - STANDARD USE:
Browsing the web, iTunes (Windows Media Player?), watching movies and whatever else is a joy on this monitor. Don't get me wrong, the out of the box presets are horrific but once you mess around with the contrast and colors a bit it is absolutely stunning. Finding 1440p content at the time of writing this review is a little hard, although, when you do it is quite worthwhile. Screen savers and full 1440p wallpapers make you want to lick the screen at times (especially my water melon wallpaper). The large landscape you achieve with the not only 32 inch screen but also 1440p resolution means you can have two full sized Chrome (Internet Explorer?) windows open at all times (or Steam + Chrome / iTunes + Something else?) The only minor thing I would say about this monitor is that reading/viewng text may not come as easily as other monitors. If you're used to Apple Retina displays this will definitely look grainier and harder to read in comparison; I am not saying it is illegible by any means, but in caparison it does leave you wanting smoother text (especially over extended sessions).

DISPLAY - GAMING:
This monitor, despite it's 7ms refresh rate, really shines when it comes to games. The colors are rich and the picture is stunning. The screen is so large you feel very immersed in your game. I highly recommend it for gaming. You will need a rather powerful graphics card to run a worthwhile title on high graphics settings, despite what others may say. I am an avid gamer and I use this monitor mostly for gaming. Lets just say an AMD 290/290x will get by okay. The GTX 970 hardly even manages to meet 60fps in most AAA titles. If you want a flawless gaming (and you're okay with turning some settings down) experience go with nothing less than a GTX 970 (for NVIDIA) or R9 290/290X (for AMD). If you do not have (or do not want to invest in) a newer graphics card I would consider a 1080p monitor.

THE REMOTE:
It's okay. You often have to click things twice for it to register, it doesn't work from more than 5 feet away (so don't expect to lay down and watch Netflix ladies), and unless you memorize the buttons it's useless in the dark. The remote is definitely not a selling point for this monitor.

I may add more to this review down the road, but for now I'm keeping it rather short and simple. Feel free to ask any questions.

I'm a bit of a windows power user. I write code for a living and I have three monitors for my desktop at home, four monitors for my desktop at work. I also have a very nice laptop but I'm used to having monitors to spread things out on and increase my productivity. My triple monitor desktop is setup in the basement, I don't always want to work in the basement and I also do a fair amount of travel and work on the go.

Naturally I eventually found and started shopping for portable monitors. As much as I enjoy having multiple monitors, I do not enjoy complicated setup or tons of cords. Recently I had been mildly sick and I worked from home. I did not want to sit in the basement so I drug two 24 inch monitors up to the kitchen table. Plugged in their power adapters, got USB-C to HDMI adapters plugged into my laptop, and managed to do some work but the huge 24 inch monitors were too large for the little table we have and it wasn't great, the setup and teardown was a pain, etc. Ok, so now I REALLY wanted a couple thin, light, portable monitors...

When I found this one I was skeptical. I had actually tried to order a different, more expensive one but they had something goofed up with their listing so I ended up ordering this cheaper one instead. Let me tell you what, they are not advertising this product strong and clear enough. I ordered the first one completely prepared to return it, my list of requirements was high and the product listing was not clear enough to know if it would meet my needs. Well now that I have one, I can confidently tell you its a good one!

They have several listed so this review is for the 13.3 inch variant. Its perfect. Checked every box I needed.
1) One-cord power and video works great on my Lenovo Yoga 920. I pulled it out of the box, plugged in the ONE USB-C to USB-C cord, and instantly working. Love it.
2) One-cord power and video works with my PHONE. Now that is unexpected and awesome. I have a Razer phone 2, pretty darn powerful phone but I did not expect it to run this monitor without plugging it into power. Going to be nice for showing people pictures and videos when I don't want to fire up the laptop.
3) Power delivery back to the laptop WORKS! This was the biggie for me and the one I was most nervous about because its not clear in the listing. I can plug the charging cord for my laptop (which is also USB-C) into the monitor and the monitor will in turn back-feed the laptop with power. This is so great, I only have two USB-C ports on my laptop so if I use two monitors I have to be able to charge the laptop via power delivery (PD) with the laptop charger hooked up to the monitor. This works perfectly and I am so pleased.
4) The OTG ports work on my laptop and on my phone. This is great because ports are so limited on my laptop. I can plug a mouse and keyboard into the monitor with the adapters they give you. Very handy when you laptop only has two USB-C ports and one USB-A port!
5) I greatly appreciate that, unlike some competition, they have ALL of the ports on ONE side of the monitor. This will allow me to do portrait mode and avoid some cable mess.
6) They kindly send a bunch of cables and adapters in the box. You get an HDMI cord, a mini-HDMI to HDMI cord, a mini-to-full HDMI adapter, two OTG adapters, a power cable, a USB-C to USB-C cable, a 2.4 amp USB wall plug, so many goodies! Granted, I don't really need any of them as I'm doing the one-cord solution but I may at times hook up older stuff or use the OTG and it was nice of them to include everything you could possibly need. Huge value in this for the price.
7) The case is kinda plastic-looking up close but its nice enough and feels durable. It will definitely protect the screen while its in my backpack and it works good as a stand.
8) The screen and picture quality look great to me. I need 1920x1080 for spreading out code and spreadsheets and other text data. I am not doing photo or video editing or gaming with it BUT it looks very nice and its easy to change brightness using the buttons on the side. It has many other settings but realistically the brightness is the only thing I will ever adjust, they made that the first menu item which is perfect.
9) Metal (aluminum) body should be nice and study. I don't bang my backpack around but I do carry it daily so durability is important to me.

Yeah I am glad to say they really nailed it with this one. It was a steal deal when I bought the first one. The price went up about seven bucks after I ordered but now that I've seen how good it is I have placed another order for a second one right away. When you can whip it out of a bag and plug in one cord and be off and running... I have a feeling its going to be a long time before I sit at my basement computer again! And I will be able to work more effectively on the road as well.

I wish the product listing was better so I could have been more confident with the first order and bought both at the same time when they were a bit cheaper, but even at the current price these are a great deal for what you are getting. Highly recommend this model. Just remember they have like four different ones and I am only talking about the 13.3 inch USB-C non-touchscreen model, the other versions they sell are probably good too but 13.3 is the size for me, matches my laptop screen so stuff is the same size and scale as I move things between monitors.

Get one (or two!) for yourself and enjoy your freedom of workspace location and increased productivity!

--------------------------------

One-Week Update:

The inexpensive nature of these monitors began showing pretty quickly but overall I am still pleased with them. I did buy a second one, on one of them the buttons on the side are even and perfect, for some reason on the other one half the buttons are recessed so far into the body of the monitor that you need a pen or something to press them, kinda weird but the buttons still work. Both of the monitors have this weird minor issue in that the screen itself seems to be slightly "loose" in the frame. Sometimes you pull them out of the bag and fire them up, part of the bottom right corner of the screen (the clock on windows computers) is cut off because the screen is slipping down into the metal frame just slightly.If you turn it upside down and give it a tap, it goes back into place...

Last bit is there is something weird about how power works with them when using two, depending on how you have them hooked up. I can do the one-wire hookup to both of them using the only two USB-C ports on my laptop, cleverly the monitors will use a low-brightness setting when their only power is from the one-wire connection. If you plug in the USB-C charger for the laptop into one of the monitor's power delivery ports then that monitor becomes twice as bright and starts charging the laptop. OK thats great but I want the other monitor to be bright too, so they match, so if you try to use the USB-A to USB-C cord they include to power up the brightness on the second monitor it goes into some crazy blackout loop, it doesn't work regardless of whether you are using a USB-A port off the laptop or a standalone USB charger in the wall. The only way to have both monitors powered and bright at the same time is with each of them having a full 20-volt USB-C laptop charger connected to each of them. That isn't the end of the world, I had hoped to only carry one laptop charger but I guess for a long session with full brightness you kinda need to carry two.

Minor blemishes on an otherwise stellar product for the money, but I did notice the price has ran up since I got my two. I still like them a lot and they are both working well, used them for a full day of working from home in the kitchen and other than needing two laptop chargers for even brightness it worked great. I think I could side-step the brightness thing if I used the HDMI input with a USB-C to HDMI adapter out of my laptop instead but I haven't tried that yet.

I ordered some right-angle USB-C adapters to avoid the hard turns on the cables, the monitor farthest from the laptop's ports has just enough cable with what they send you to reach but its pulling a bit hard on the port, the right-angle adapter should solve that. USB-OTG still working good for external mouse, keyboard, hooking up memory card readers, etc.
Do not buy, this monitor has constantly buzzed out of the box and only gotten worse regardless of the settings.  HP does not stand behind their products either.

A piece of the stand assembly was missing, and I had to go through a few hours of support with LG to get the right part ordered, at my own expense nonetheless. But this is likely the fault of the seller, not of Amazon itself. I'm sure that if you buy new directly from Amazon, you won't have these problems.

Now on to the monitor itself. I love this thing. It was very surprising when I first unboxed it, because while I knew it was an ultrawide, I didn't know exactly what to expect. I was blown away by the size and width of this monitor.

When you get it hooked up and turned on, the experience only gets better. From my unprofessional opinion, colors on this display are great. They seem very accurate and natural. Viewing angles also are great; it has to be viewed at extreme angles to even start to notice a difference in color and quality, so there is absolutely no problem viewing it from the tame angles you would be at at your desk. The display has a matte finish that makes reflections on the screen a non issue.

Controlling and changing the settings of this monitor is also very pleasant. LG has put a single button on the bottom that also acts as a joystick of sorts, and it is pretty easy and natural to navigate with it. The menus are well laid out and easy to read. I believe LG also has software on their website that allows you to change the settings onscreen with your mouse, but I have not used it so I am not completely sure.

One thing to note is that this monitor does not have inbuilt speakers, so you will have to use another peripheral for your sound needs.

In conclusion, this is a great monitor for a great price, and I would highly recommend it to anyone looking to venture into the ultrawide market.

[[VIDEOID:ba202c10d3adcf46692a597f44c09be2]] I love ASUS products, unfortunately this monitor was the single most disappointing purchase I made in a long time.

Pros

Cons
1. Screen goes black for 1-4 seconds and comes back. Happens everyday, sometime very frequently. Specially annoying when playing games, as you can imagine. There's a semi-workaround I've found. When the issue starts, reset Windows video drivers by pressing the keys windows + shift + ctrl + B.

4. Shadows, specially with windows with dark/black top border. Using windows in dark mode makes this ugly. You can see shadows bleeding to the right side of the top border while moving windows. Best workaround I've found was to give up dark mode and have a bright colors theme.

The TL;DR is simple: I have found the SW321C to be a phenomenal, no-compromises, nearly flawless monitor. Great stand, great user interface (I love the puck), outstanding image quality, even backlight, no backlight bleed. It's superb for work and leisure. There's IPS glow, but that's unavoidable.

I'm a programmer who spends most of his time on his computer -- reading/writing text, watching video, and playing games. Previously I was using 2 Dell U2518D monitors, which, even after calibration with an iDisplay Pro Plus, had the following issues:
* Crushed blacks
* Poor contrast
* Uneven colors
* Uneven backlight
* Backlight bleed
* Glare, despite the matte surface

After deciding I didn't want to put up with those issues any longer, I did a week or two of research on what the best no-compromises monitors were in late 2020. That led me to the SW321C, a professional monitor rather than a gaming/consumer monitor, because consumer models -- no matter how fancy they claimed to be or how slick the marketing -- all seemed to fall short and require me to resign myself to compromises in at least one area (and I didn't want FALD).

The BenQ SW321C is expensive (though not as expensive as the EIZO and Dell screens that it's often compared to), but I consider the money well spent. The image quality is outstanding. Colors are stunning. Images and text are markedly sharper and crisper than they were on my 25" 1440p monitors (though this would be true of any 4k 32" screen). The backlight is the evenest I've ever seen. There's no backlight bleed that I can detect. I love the shade hood and never take it off. The matte coating is far and away the best I've seen/used.

For the movies and tv shows I've watched on it (Blade Runner, Ex Machina, The Witch, The Expanse, The Man in the High Castle, The Midnight Gospel), I couldn't be happier with the quality of the picture. The HDR may be "fake," but it still massively improves the viewing experience, especially in regard to handling of darkness/shadows.

Last year I bought and returned the BenQ SW271. I loved it, but the backlight was uneven, there was backlight bleed, and 27" was too small for 4K". (32" is, for my eyes (late 30s), the perfect size for a 4k screen.) If I was going to spend $1,500, I wasn't willing to put up with those issues.

The SW321 isn't as bright as the SW271, which is a shame, but it also fixes every issue I had with the SW271.
==
Update: the blacks aren't as dark as I'd like them to be. (EDIT: correction, for $2k you can NOT get a very decent oled tv with perfect blacks. You will experience burn in.) The monitor also seems to produce a lot of heat. I'm not sure I regret the purchase. It still ticks all the boxes for me, and I consider the money well spent, but if I could do it again, and my living quarters allowed it, (EDIT and recantation: I would not opt for an OLED)

Update #2: Scratch that. I still wish the blacks were darker, but this is as good as it gets on an IPS panel. OLED TVs aren't meant to be used as PC monitors, and if you use one that way, there will inevitably be burn in. I'm still quite happy with the purchase, despite the one, single, occasional stuck sub-pixel.

I've seen a few reviews here that fault the hardware calibration, but for me it has been superb -- certainly better, to my untrained eye, than the i1 Display Pro Plus's software calibration. That being said, I encountered a couple of bugs/problems when using the Palette Master software: it didn't detect my calibrator until I set colors to 10 bit, full output, and I had to plug it into a USB port on my desktop, not one of the SW321C's USB ports.

Update #3: The stuck pixel started to occur more frequently, so I contacted BenQ customer service and asked them to service the screen. The customer-support staff, who were phenomenal -- prompt, professional, understanding, knowledgeable, and incredibly to work with -- helped me, despite a couple of mix ups (which weren't their fault), resolve the matter to my satisfaction.

Update #4: The top review is... awful. What on earth sort of person brags(?) about bossing around delivery people? The top item in this person's list of pros is "high Quality Display Monitor," but the review also states that the screen was defective, that s/he has seen better image quality on $200 monitors, and that the colors look "horrible." Garbage. Neither of us is an expert. Ignore amazon reviews. Read reviews written by professional photographers, for whom this monitor is intended.

Dec 2022 update: my sw321c is one of my favorite possessions. Even my partner who isn't finicky about screen quality loves it. Watching movies or shows on it is always a treat. I can't praise it highly enough. And I can't believe I omitted from my review that its cardboard box is the nicest, most thoughtful I've encountered for anything I've purchased. It's huge but well worth keeping, as it protects the monitor perfectly and stores and organizes everything incredibly cleanly and effectively. No loose cables. No textured carton compartments. Everything has its isolated section, or its own box (which perfectly fits into only one place). My only complaint with the monitor concerns the shade ood, which has started to fall apart easily. I doubt it's meant to be handled, put on, or taken off as much as I have though. I cover the screen with several layers of cloth when I'm not using it, e.g. at night, because I want to keep it pristine, and they don't fit over the hood.

When my friend recommended Pixio to me I was a bit hesitant because of their price tag. However, I bit the bullet and bought the monitor when it was on sale.

The picture is crisp and the settings allow for a rather dynamic set of changes. You can also save different profiles into the monitor itself so you can easily have a few different "picture modes" along side the ones that come standard.

The on-screen FPS display is a bit too big for my liking, but it's still cool they include the feature.

The crosshair is super useful, since it can not only be re-positioned, but there's a few variations on the style too. No more putting a piece of tape on the screen and drawing a dot here, and no need to buy some silly laser-dot string to put on your monitor either.

There's also a timer built into the screen but I haven't actually made use of that just yet.

All in all, it's a great monitor and well worth the money, sale or not. But do pay attention to which model you're buying. The 248 and 248S are almost identical, but the 248S is a bit smaller in height (like 2-4cm or so) and has a higher refresh rate (144hz vs this one at 120).

I travel for business 50% of the time and I have been using a 2017 13" MacBook Pro for business traveling. Navigating from a single screen when you have multiple files opened is very difficult. The latest Apple OS update allows you to use an iPad as a second screen so I was thinking about getting a 13" iPad Pro just for that. The things that held me back to get an iPad Pro were the price and the potential that my carry-on may get rejected when going through airport security due to carrying too many devices which contain big batteries. So, for those reasons, this is a perfect solution as the monitor does not contain a battery and the price is reasonable in comparison to an iPad Pro. Also, it is very light weight about 2 lbs which makes it easy to carry around in a backpack. The star reductions are due to the monitor quality. The Type C connection is finicky. Maybe the 13" MacBook Pro is not powerful enough to power the monitor via the Type C connection, it requires another charger for the monitor which I am okay with. The problem is the signal. I have no problem with the HDMI port as I have tested the monitor with a MS Surface Computer and a Surface Pro using a Mini Display to HDMI adapter. I can't pick up a signal from the MacBook Pro without some troubleshooting. It is not my MacBook Pro problem as I have the same issue with the 2016 13" MacBook Pro. The cables that came with the devices are not the greatest quality but they do the job. The biggest disappointment is the monitor itself. For some reason, there is a small white patch on the screen when it is on standby mode. However, the white patch goes away when it is power down. The white patch does not appear when it is in use so I am not sure what the problem is.

I will return this monitor in exchange for another one that without a white patch. Aside for some inconvenience when using it with the MacBook Pro, this monitor makes a great companion for business travel. I picked this up to replace an older 1080p TN monitor with something a bit larger. After doing some reading, it seems like 1440p is the best resolution for a 27" monitor, so I went with this one. I mostly use it for gaming, so the 75hz + FreeSync was a big plus for me. Don't believe the hype about 144Hz monitors, your mid-range GPU is never going to be able to churn out more than 75 FPS @ 1440p anyways.

For starters, the monitor looks fantastic. The color reproduction is amazing, just be sure to use the "Custom" picture setting mode to remove the color temperature adjustments. Most of the preset picture modes either distort color too much or apply too much of a sharpen filter.
So far I haven't had any issues with FreeSync mode. Just be sure to update your display's refresh rate to 75Hz in Windows 10 and enable "variable refresh rate" under the graphics settings. It looks fantastic while gaming with no signs of ghosting or input lag.

Unfortunately, the monitor doesn't play nice with Windows 10's HDR mode. Once you enable HDR mode in Windows, you'll lose the ability to modify the "Custom" picture mode settings, leaving all of your non-HDR applications with an ugly warm tint. You can still use the other predefined picture modes, but the lack of fine tuning will be a hindrance. After fighting with it for a few days, I decided it just wasn't worth it and just turned HDR off because it wasn't worth the headache and it didn't seem to actually add much.

Lastly, I can't give this a full 5/5 rating because the monitor does have a few flaws. Firstly, the stand feels pretty flimsy. Don't get me wrong, it holds the monitor up, but a monitor this size deserves more than pencil-thin legs. Thankfully, the VESA mount on this monitor is easy to use, so you don't have to use the stand if you don't want to. Secondly, the monitor's on-screen display could use some attention. For example, most of the default shortcuts involve volume adjustments... but this monitor doesn't have speakers. In addition to the HDR issues mentioned earlier, it seems like the monitor tracks separate settings for regular, FreeSync, and HDR modes separately. If you ever adjust your contrast/brightness, you'll need to manually apply those changes to FreeSync and HDR modes as well.

But hey, at $230 price point, I'm willing to overlook it.

I haven't looked into the 170hz, but I believe you have to have the monitor at 1080p in order for it to work (which in my opinion is a useless tradeoff from the 1440p). ALSO be wary that this doesn't have any VGA or DVI ports on it, but in my case they would have been useless anyways. Also it doesn't use the traditional power cord that you would per-say plug into your power supply, it uses an A/C power converter with this little cord that looks like a laptop charger, though for now it seems to work just fine. All in all I believe it's worth it if you're not paying over $500 dollars for, since the price seems to keep fluctuating I would wait for it to be between $400-$500 since I payed around $483 in total for this. [[VIDEOID:mo23C49PLYG2NYX]]UPDATE: I wanted to add a little short video which shows what I'm seeing in my comparison of the U2412M to the Benq TN panel monitor I had before I bought the Dell. As the video shows, the Dell looks so much better as to be in a whole different class - which it IS, being an IPS panel versus the TN panel in the Benq monitor. I was plenty happy with the Benq; but as the video shows, it was only because I did not know what I was missing.

ORIGINAL REVIEW: Well, after a couple of weeks of reading EVERY review I could find with The Google Machine about this monitor, I decided to pull the trigger. I originally ordered from NewEgg for $299 but then I saw that Amazon Warehouse had a "used - very good" available for $233, so I cancelled the other order and placed with Amazon Warehouse. Monitor arrived on time and boxed well.

My main use will be as an external monitor for the Retina MacBook Pro I'm buying next month - but for now, this monitor is serving as the second monitor for my 24" 2009 iMac, running at an identical 1920x1200. This is the resolution I have used for almost 7 years, and I prefer it, and 16:10, to a 16:9 format. I'm connected with the DisplayPort connection in the Dell, and all is well.

This one shows to be Rev A00, manufactured Oct 2013. I don't see any of the "yellow tint" problem which is warned about in one earlier review, and in fact, is mentioned in a lot of places on the InterWebs too - it's a REAL thing. I might have gotten lucky with mine - time will tell.

The anti-glare coating, to me, appears to be a non-issue. I'm coming from using +20" iMacs since 2007, so I am used to a Glossy Screen, and I was really worried that the AG coating would be a problem. But so far, Hour One of Day One, I'm not really even noticing it.

I have not adjusted the colors at ALL out of the box, and it looks great. I will play around some with the color settings more later, especially when this becomes my MacBook Pro monitor. But as of today, I don't feel any adjustments are required.

I will update as things (if things) change, and for now, I say if you are hedging a little on what monitor to buy, pull the trigger on this one and you won't be sorry !!

I'll start by saying I knew exactly what I was buying when I purchased this monitor on thanksgiving day 2019. I wanted a monitor that had phenomenal colors, improved my productivity, and had decent gaming specs. IPS monitors are all the rage but I just can't deal with "IPS Glow".

This monitor doesn't disappoint. I used my color calibration puck (Spyder 4 pro) and this monitor looks even better after calibration. at 100% sRGB, the colors are great and the deep contrast ratio helps this monitor look even better.

I have a 1080ti graphics card so I needed to make a custom resolution with a refresh rate of "98" to get rid of screen flicker. As long as you do this, enable gsync, and your game runs over 40fps you will not have screen flicker. However running Assassins creed Odyssey at ultra settings made the fps dip below 40 which resulted in screen flicker....Adjusting the graphics settings slightly improved performance which in turn eliminated screen flicker.

My second monitor is a Dell S2716DGR which is 1440p at 144hz TN panel. Playing the latest call of duty game on the BenQ, I can instantly tell a difference between these monitors for fast paced games. The BenQ feels slow and a bit blurry. Even with AMA on premium, there is ever so slight ghosting.. I only notice this in Call of Duty, so I will continue to play that on my Dell monitor. other single player games such as Odyssey are quite fantastic on this display.

HDR on the BenQ is nice even if it's not "True" HDR. It makes games look a bit more realistic and youtube HDR video's especially look amazing. I have yet to test a 4k HDR bluray.

I seem to have received a very good VA panel with no noticeable defects of any kind. I can somewhat see the dark side edges people are talking about, but I can't tell if I'm actually seeing it, or if I'm imagining it. Either way, it's not an issue for me.

In conclusion, if you are a competitive FPS gamer....This isn't the monitor for you...Sorry.. Everyone else will be extremely satisfied.

Its the most expensive display I've ever purchased and its possible I would have been satisfied with something cheaper but I have no regrets. If I do have future problems I'll update this.

Honestly I was super hesitant to buy this monitor as I have been looking for a proper ips ultrawide since the introduction of the x34. Every subsequent product to come out since then has been faulty in some area enough to warrant avoiding it. The LG 34gk950(f or g) model I was extremely hopeful for. It was sad to see there were so many issues with quality control.

Moving on to the aw3420dw, I was skeptic because of Alienware being notorious for overpriced mediocrity. Fortunately a bit of research goes a long way. This monitor uses the same nano-ips panel that the LG 950 has, but dell has a much higher QC and much better customer service.

I have had ZERO issues with this monitor and I couldn't be happier to have waited. The picture is colorful, bright, and fast....the specs don't lie. Many would say that the aesthetic is Overkill but honestly it's great, and when paying the price you do...it feels more premium.

What I don't like:
-the software to control the ambient lighting is buggy, and honestly the ambient lighting is pretty dim and useless. With how much it is pitched, it is underwhelming. Don't buy this monitor if you are looking for rgb overload.
-uninstalling the software isnt possible unless you know how to block auto installing programs, and the software isn't necessary for the monitor to properly function.
-could use more display control options on board. Not a huge deal as most of it you control through gsync panel, but it would be a nice update.
-the white finish is a fingerprint grabber. Place it, clean the prints, and DONT TOUCH IT. lol.

What I do like:
-This thing performs I run this on ultra settings with everything on a 2080ti. Solid 120fps 2ms all of the time and it looks great.
-multiple presets built in. If you aren't a huge fan of calibration, it does have a ton of precalibrated setting for various uses.
-STURDY. This monitor along with the stand is super solid. It really feels like a quality product.
-no panel problems as of yet. No dead pixels, etc.

All in all...I would definitely buy this again.
If you're on the fence....go for it. Really. I waited years for a monitor like this and I'm glad I finally found this one.

Pros:
- Great IPS picture quality. Much better quality for gaming than a similar IPS Acer panel I bought 6 years ago.
- Good variety of input options
- 75 Hz, 1 ms response time is good for gaming (my desktop can't run 144 Hz, so 75 Hz is plenty smooth for me).

Con:
- Matte anti-glare coating obscures text. Wasn't an issue during games or movies, but somewhat noticeable during work.
- Excessive backlight bleed

The backlight bleed was ultimately so excessive that I had to exchange the panel. The upper left corner of the screen was quite bright, and was very distracting during dark scenes in movies or games. While my older Acer still has some backlight bleed, it's nothing near as bothersome as this monitor. If I was using this monitor solely for work, the issues wouldn't bother me enough to have returned it.

TL;DR Go for it if you are willing to play the panel lottery and hope you get one without backlight bleed. Otherwise, there are probably other options at this price with better QC.

Excellent monitor - in use as the main monitor in a digital music studio set up. No complaints thus far. I see none of the artifacts and other negative observations on other reviews. I will attempt to use it for photo editing later in the year, and will update this review at that time. The stand's positioning flexibility far exceeded my expectations. I was concerned that this monitor had been around for 4 years and might be technologically obsolete, but it has turned out to be an awesome value.

I have my Apple Mac Mini connected via DisplayPort, and a 2nd connection via HDMI to an HDMI Matrix splitter so I can watch cable, Apple TV, or blu-ray content. The various color shadings available make this an awesome monitor to watch movies on, and I was not distracted by slow refreshes in action scenes. It Is a good idea to walk through all the settings in the menu, as you will find this monitor to be a step above both your TV and your typical PC monitor, in terms of flexibility. Check VividPixel settings for pixel smoothing for example. You can make the display as sharp or smooth as you need dependent on what you are doing with it. I like choices, don't you? There is even a calibration for gaming, where you can calibrate how the monitor tracks for 1st person shooters, I suspect.

I am using the monitor with Apple Logic Pro X, which offers up a TON of data across multiple windows. The additional screen real estate from running at a higher resolution is a gift. I now no longer need two screens. I have an older G5 Mac and a Windows PC that I will be connecting and will update this review later with feedback on how that goes.

All in all, nothing negative to report, all positive on this front.

The only thing I would complain about is that the resolution isn't high enough to give crisp retina quality AND high resolution. The text on my MBP is so crisp, it's hard to view anything else in non-retina resolution. True retina on this monitor would make it a 1920x1080 monitor (resolution divided by 2 in both directions). I have viewed it in true retina, and it's fantastic, but I need the resolution so I am using it in 2560x1440. It's still very sharp but not Retina clear. 5K native resolution would be perfect, but that isn't ready for prime time yet.

The monitor is also very light and does not generate any measurable heat. My old professional NEC generated so much heat on my face, I had to give it away. The built-in USB 3.0 hub is so nice. Not having an external 3.0 hub makes for a clean desk.

btw, it's not an Ultrasharp model. It's pretty evident the build quality and features are not up to the high end models, but the display panel is awesome. I am using the panel for software development and photo editing. If you're looking for a super premium display, this isn't it. If you're looking for a great display for a really good price, you can't beat this monitor at this time.

I own an Mimo 7-inch Monitor and was looking to upgrade. The Mimo Magic Monster is their entry level 10-inch Monitor. Touch functionality was considered a bonus since I wanted to use it primarily as a second screen. I wasn't too concerned whether it was a resistive touchscreen like this model, or capacitive touchscreen like their other more expensive models.

PROS:
- I was able to power and operate the monitor off a single USB port from my HP Laptop, including the touch screen
- Looks better built than the 7-inch and the adjustable dock/base is heavy and sturdy. Would not look out of place in a boardroom.
- Bright screen (I had to dim it slightly) and 1024x600 is extremely usable and suitable for a 10" display.
- Resistive touchscreen is surprisingly responsive and supports multi-touch.
- Windows automatically recognises that a touchscreen has been added (Hint: run Tablet Setup to configure the touch functionality, or cursor will default to the primary non-touch monitor). DisplayLink drivers are solid.

CONS;
- Expensive. This costs more than some Tablets and larger USB driven monitors.
- Minor gripe, but I didn't care for the 'Magic Touch' label on the top left of the monitor. The 'MIMO' brand placement was OK.

[Update: I just realised I was shipped the Capacitive model UM-1050, which explans why the screen is so responsive. Nice!]
