This does not include the nano IPS coating of the 27GL850-B.

Onto my own experience, the monitor itself comes in a nondescript box noting its name and with the LG ultragear logo on it. Included is a large wall wart as its stand and a decently long DP cable.
After getting it set up, I was surprised to see that the panel I got was of great quality having both no dead pixels as well as some outstanding panel uniformity for an IPS of its class. The backlight bleed was negligible and the blacks themselves were very good. (at least for my unit)

This is where my positives end, as to get the best picture out of this requires a large amount of tinkering within the OSD, as the pre-calibrated profiles it came with were horrendous. Mostly crushing the blacks and leaving out detail in dark areas. The panel comes calibrated relatively dim in all profiles. I could mainly chalk this down to a possible bias, as the old TN panel I had previously been using for the past 5 years may have not been the most color accurate. Do not expect a perfect white either, as no matter how I calibrate it, the backlight always exhibits either a very slighly greenish or bluish tint depending on profile. Personally, this does not annoy me, but I would look elsewhere if you think that it may be an issue. (this could also be down to the panel I got in particular aswell)
Once calibrated though, I would say I am very much satisfied with the picture quality.

One item of note though, I would not recommend that you use the included stand as IPS glow is very apparent at the fixed angle that the stand is at. A VESA compatible mount is a requirement in my opinion as the stand it comes with although built well and aesthetically pleasing, has literally no available adjustments.

Finally, with motion clarity, this is another thing that this monitor is very good at. At 144hz and with the 'fast' OD option, there is no noticeable motion blur from what I could see. Very impressive for an IPS panel.

Do note though, the advertised 1ms response time only applies for the 'fastest' OD setting, which causes a great deal of overshoot on the pixels making the already good lack of blur into a mess of visual oatmeal.
The 'fast' OD setting already is responsive enough for me not to notice a difference. Just keep in mind the advertised 1ms response time is more likely to be around the normal 4ms for IPS panels, at least if you want decent motion clarity.

Both G sync and HDR work as expected on this panel, but don't expect the HDR to be particularly good, as this is still only an edglit panel.
G-sync seemingly does work from the advertised VRR amount from its 48hz to 144hz. There are no complaints to be had on that front, just make sure you use the DP connector.

This monitor, being my first foray into 144hz as well as IPS technology came as both a shock and a pleasant surprise. I add this with the caveat that I would not recommend buying this with its full $400 price tag. If you can get it on sale, you are unlikely to regret it, but if not, just go for the LG 27GL83A-B, to save $20 and get a better stand as these are basically the exact same monitor bar the stand.
This thing is smooth. 144hz is the only way to go if your machine can handle it.

Offering Plug and Play convenience you can use these with a higher amperage USB Hub to provide multi monitor display capabilities for existing system. Generally windows 7/8 will detect the device and download the required driver.

Some may experience a compatibility issue with the active system display driver since these operate as a slave device when a Primary Display is already active. First, uninstalling, then reinstalling all relevant display drivers using Windows Programs and Features, Control Panel command will correct any issue that may occur.

Lack of reliable USB 3.0 support inhibits the potential for this display monitor due to the bandwidth restrictions imposed by USB 2.0 primarily affecting refresh rates. However because this Display Link type monitors' color gamut is truncated somewhat, and also because the resolution is lower than full HD, when used appropriately the overall picture quality is similar to value priced laptop displays and can provide a simpler solution to other multi-monitor setups.

Overtaxing the USB ports can be observed when the display image will effectively begin to roll and distort, not unlike older CRT based television sets. Also expect some picture degradation when used in portrait mode due to the restricted viewing angle when compared to a landscape orientation.

These considerations combined with limiting USB port functionality for other devices, because of the power requirement necessary to drive this type of display, requires some pre planning, and research. The additional considerations described earlier, should be reviewed prior to integrating these with any system. When integrated appropriately a workable tri-monitor setup is possible to supplement available primary display monitor desktop space for general computing tasks or possibly to augment tablets display capabilities. (Experimental Usage which might or might not work depending on the make, model, brand.)

Here are the pros and cons of this monitor as I see them:

Pros
* 2K QHD resolution (2560x1440)
* 8 bit, 100% RGB coverage, essentially the entire color spectrum meaning pictures look great.
* 100% Rec. 709 coverage
* The above allows it to be Technicolor certified
* Monitor stand doubles as a built in docking station to use with a laptop.
- USB C connection (allows you to connect your laptop to the dock, monitor and peripherals with a single cable)
- USB Power (with included laptop style power brick)
- Headphone jack
- 4 USB ports
* Display port connection
* Stable and fully adjustable stand
* Can orient in portrait mode (though how many upper end monitors can't?)
* Can daisy chain monitors with multi-stream transport technology
* Small bezel - the screen essentially goes edge to edge though the it only displays to 3/8" from edge.

Cons:
* Minor edge darkening at bottom of screen
* Quick setup instructions are far from intuitive

Summary:
This is a very bright very nice monitor that includes a lot of functionality and some thoughtful design features. If it weren't for the very small bit of lower edge darkening this would easily get 5 stars. 4.5 stars.

This thing works as advertised and has vastly exceeded my expectations. It does one thing only and it does it quite well: it turns your car's wired CarPlay radio into a wireless CarPlay radio. That's it, and there are very few catches (see below). It's incredibly convenient no longer having to fish my phone out of my pocket to use CarPlay. I just get in my car and this thing automatically connects without fail.

I've had the device for a couple of months now (I'm using it on a 2020 VW GTI with an iPhone Xs and an iPhone 12), and I have very few complaints. The thing is rock-solid. Below, I'm only going list my nitpicks with the device. If anything is not listed, I could not find fault with it (I haven't tried anything involving video).
1. The instructions were a bit sparse, but I eventually muddled through the installation. Once installed, it's absolutely trouble-free.
2. My biggest complaint by far is that it can take a while to connect to your phone once you get in your car. I haven't timed it, but I'm guessing up to 30 seconds. Once extremely rarely, it may even take longer, but the device will keep trying until it establishes the connection.
3. This device doesn't automatically resume playing any audio that was previously playing once the connection is established. You have to navigate to the app (Music, Podcasts, etc) and hit the "play" button.
4. This may be my imagination, but I'm guessing that the audio prompts during navigation come about a second or so later than with wired. It not enough to be a big deal, but I want as much advance notice as possible.
5. Only once in over two months of almost daily driving, CarPlay stopped responding. Simply unplugging the device from the car's USB port and plugging it in again fixed the issue. This may not even have been an issue with the device.
6. I've upgraded my phone from a Xs to a 12 Mini. I muddled through adding the new phone without problem, but the old phone is still shown in the list of paired phones when the device starts--I can't figure out how to remove it. It doesn't really affect anything, but it's annoying seeing my old phone in the list.
7. As with all wireless CarPlay devices, expect your phone's battery to drain a bit faster when connected. This is because your phone has to basically has to create a wireless hotspot with your radio. There's probably no way around this.

Why haven't you bought this thing yet?

TO THE MANUFACTURER: This device is a winner. Do what you have to do to make sure everyone knows about it. If you could make an improved device which improves the issues I mentioned above, you could pretty much name your price and I'd happily pay it.
Being HDMI monitor not great quality pictures.

I ordered a pair of these for our law office a while back. No complaints. The image looks good and the price is right. We later opted for a dual monitor set up and ordered a second pair of these monitors. Two surprises. First, the original monitor had a pair of outputs, the standard VGA as well as a DVI, the newer version had removed the DVI output. For an instant we thought this would pose a problem. You see, our basic computers have only one VGA input, and one HDMI input. To connect two monitors we'd purchased two DVI to HDMI cables. The plan was to use the VGA connection on the first monitor and the DVI connection on the second monitor. The problem was the second monitor didn't come with a DVI input. The solution, of course, was simply to use the VGA input from the 2nd monitor and the DVI from the original. For us, no worries, because we'd purchased the original pair of monitors before this second input had been removed to cut costs. But the limited connection inputs could pose a problem depending on your setup, so be aware.

The bigger problem we ran into was that we'd intended to connect these two monitors to a dual monitor stand. We'd gone so far as to order the stand before we noticed the problem. While most monitors have four screw holes on which to mount the monitor to a stand, these monitors do not have such a feature. So we are unable to have the clean look and feel of the monitors being connected in a stand designed for this purpose.

No problem, you say, just set them on your desktop. This is what we ultimately did. And you might even make an argument that this set up is more ideal, because we saved $50 for each dual monitor stand we weren't able to purchase. That's $100 more in our pockets!

The problem is that these monitors are not adjustable for height, so if you set them on your desk, and you're taller than a six year old child, you'll need to look down to view them. This is horrible for posture. The solution that we've come up with, temporarily, is to put a stack of books under the monitors. This solves the problem but looks quite a bit less than professional.

The bottom line is that if you're looking for a monitor that does its job and want to pay the lowest price possible, and aren't looking for any "bells or whistles", then you can't go wrong with these monitors. The picture quality is great, as is the price. However,it is important to realize that Acer has reached this price point by removing those "bells and whistles". Further, some would argue that these aren't bells and whistles, but are essential components. By foregoing these, you are limiting future expansion possibilities and the ergonomic integrity of your workstation.

Like anyone else here, you're probably doing a ton of research and watching youtube reviews on this monitor. Seems like reviews from summer 2020 there were some issues that firmware have fixed. I purchased in Jan 2021 and updated to the latest firmware and this monitor is perfect.

PROS
-240hz is gorgeous and it's obviously why you are on this page looking at this monitor. There isn't a ton of competition in the 240hz 1440p market right now
-VA panel is done very well by Samsung. VA panels have typically not been executed well by other manufactures, but Samsung has been doing VA panels with their TVs for a long time, so they have all the kinks worked out.
-Color was great out of the box, minor tweaks made it perfect.
-Curved screen helps me not "lose sight" of things in the corners and far edges of the screen. 27" is a big monitor, if you are going from a 24 or 25 to a 27, you will probably have a transition period going to 27 and the curve definitely makes a difference.
-Zero issues with ghosting, the top review on Amazon says he had major issues but I have had zero on this monitor. I have had it on previous monitors, so I know what it is. He may have just gotten a bad monitor or firmware not update. Don't let that dissuade you from this monitor
-Was on sale when I got this for $599 and used a ton of rewards on it as well. Good deal at the time for me.

CONS
-Curve is jarring for some people, it looks goofy, but loved it now almost two months later. Won't know until you try it. Free returns with Amazon, so worth a shot IMO.
-HDR is supposedly not bright enough for some people? I didn't have that issue but most of my games are not HDR, so this was a non-issue for me.
-Firmware NEEDS to be updated before using.
-Price? I don't know, that is obviously relative. Gaming and technology are my main hobbies, I will happily spend money on them.
-Back panel cover is flimsy, some people broke it by not being careful. I knew that going in, so wasn't an issue.
-VA isn't IPS? I had no complains with my picture quality or performance whatsoever.

COMPUTER USED
i9-10850k @ 5.0
RTX 3090 Evga XC3
Corsair Hydro X cooling
32gb 3600mhz
All ssds are M.2 NVMe

GAMES PLAYED
-CSGO - main game, noticeable difference between 144hz and 240hz, very very smooth.
-PUBG - game is still clunky as hell, but getting ~200fps makes it feel slightly better lol.
-CoD Cold War + Warzone - feels buttery smooth running at 240fps consistently at competitively high settings

ALTERNATIVES (prices as of 3/11/21)
-Samsung G7 in 32" at $798 - it is the same monitor but just bigger, so the 1440p will be blurrier. My sweet-spot is 27" and want the same size to match my secondary monitor
-Samsung G9 in 49" at $1400 - this really isn't comparable as it is twice the price and it's unbelievably ultrawide. A very niche product IMO. I have a 34" Ultrawide 1440p monitor at work and I love it, but I cannot imagine something this big. I have a powerful computer and I don't see the point of running like a flight sim on this as my 3090 is no where close to maxing out frame rate.
-Dell Alienware AW2721D at $767 - this was my only really competitor that I was considering. It basically had all the specs that I wanted, the difference being IPS vs VA and Curved vs Non-curved. The main factor was timing and the Dell was $200 more expensive than the G7 on the time of my purchase, so I went with the G7. I have been a big fan of Dell monitors for a long time (work, gaming and personal use). I was replacing a 1440p 144hz Dell TN panel monitor I got on Amazon warehouse used for a great price many years ago. It is now my secondary monitor.
-HP Omen X 27" at $609 - TN panel was not in the cards, I wanted to upgrade to either VA or IPS for this price point. People also complained about the VESA mount (requirement for me) and the matte on the screen. I also HATED the look of the bottom bezel area.

OTHER NOTES
Two months in, I love this monitor and I have no regrets whatsoever. Sorry if the review is all over the place, hopefully still helpful to some in deciding their next upgrade.

I repair computers for various offices and, with the pandemic, I am often given a desktop system without a monitor or a mobile system that 'won't power on' to repair. So I bought this monitor more for troubleshooting than as a second monitor, though it works well that way also. So, I have connected it to several different systems since I got it. Having read through many of the comments and questions, I hope to answer many of them.

As this is just a monitor, it should work anywhere any other standard monitor will work. If you are using the Mini DP port or the HDMI port you will need additional power through the PD port or the USB-C port. If you are connecting through the USB-C port and your device doesn't have the power to run the monitor you will also need power on the PD port. Newer devices can power the monitor through a USB-C to USB-C cable that can handle it. The one that comes with the monitor works for this. With various adapter cables you can hook this monitor to many different types of systems. I have used an old tablet with a mini-HDMI to HDMI cable. A laptop with a thunderbolt 2 port with a mini-DP to DP adapter on each end of a DP cable connected to the mini-DP port of the monitor. The same laptop with a mini-DP to HDMI adapter to the HDMI port of the monitor. Newer laptops and tablets were able to connect through the USB-C to USB-C cable without needing a power cable, and I even got my
work laptop to work with a USB-C to USB -A female adapter then plugging the included USB-A to USB-C cable into that then to the USB-C port on the monitor. (Not necessary, but fun to try.) If you are using the USB-C to USB-C cable to connect, plugging a power source into the PD port will charge your device if it charges through the port you are connected to.

For docking stations I was able to get three HP docks to work with the monitor. The TPA-B01 mobile dock worked with my HP ProBook 840 G5 but not my phone. It does need a power adapter to work. The USB-C mini dock (1PM64AA#AC3) worked with my laptop and my phone, so would probably be the best of the three for trips. It doesn't need a power adapter, but does have a USB-C port for power if wanted. The 2013 ultraslim dock (D9Y19AV#ABA) for laptops. This isn't a mobile dock but does allow you to connect the monitor to your laptop. All three of these will need external power for the monitor. I haven't tried other docks yet.

The included power brick is rated at 3A, and will probably be the best way to power the monitor when needed, but I was able to get it to work with a standard USB-3 port on my laptop (0.9A) and using a power bank 1A output. So you should be able to get it to work even with lower power input if necessary. An adequate power bank will allow you to use it even away from a power source.

My only preference would be to the the OTG port on the left be USB-C also instead of micro USB-2, but that is a minor point.

A few days ago I participated in a meeting where I had stopped at a rest stop and sat at a picnic table. I had my Note 9 phone hooked to the monitor through the USB-C port with my 15000mAh power bank to the PD port and a portable keyboard/touchpad connected to the OTG port. Using Samsung Dex I was able to see the presentation well and it was still going fine an hour later having used about half of my power bank.

With just a few adapters, this monitor has the ability to work with just about anything and is a great mobile solution for what I need.

For HDR and the color gamut, when I watch HDR videos on YouTube, it shows HDR on the quality in settings and the 'Stats for nerds' show it using BT.2020. My Nvidia 1660 Super detects it as a 10 bit monitor.

Setting Mode 1 (Great Colors but Darker):
You put it on the ECO setting;
Sharpness: 1
Color Temperature: R 95 G 93 B 97

Instant Mode: On
(Thank you Amazon User Kordrok)

Setting Mode 2 (More brightness but less popping colors):
You put it on the Standard setting;
Brightness 100
Contrast 55
Sharpness 7
Gamma 5
Red 98 Yellow 93 Blue 95
Ama High

For people that are debating to go 21:9 vs 16:9, well that ultimately depends on your usage. This is great for watching wide format movies! Its good for games that support the 21:9 format. Rocket League, Doom, and Project Racing are good examples of native support. Photoshop/GIMP is also productive.
Now the downside boils down to your contents. If your sources don't support 21:9, you will get the black bars on the sides. The monitor has 5 ways of handling sources that are 16:9. Under the Menu>Quick Settings>Ratio you'll find Wide, Original, Cinema1, Cinema2 and 1:1. These are different ways the monitor can stretch your image to fit. The settings are good for console gaming.
If black bars bother you then stay away. Stay away from all 21:9 monitors unless you have the content for it. Support is growing but not for the impatient.

This monitor has terrible light bleed in the four corners. I have a Feb 2017 manufacturing date on my monitor and the bleed in my opinion is bearable but YMMV. Unless you're looking at black loading screens, you'll hardly notice. Actually you won't see it during gaming or watching 21:9 movies. The screen quality is beautiful though! The bezel is non existent but there is the black border around the panel that takes away from the immersion of zero bezel. My room is dark so that doesn't bother me.
The stand this comes with is sturdy and actually pretty useful. You can raise and lower the monitor with relative ease and it's pretty generous ( maximum height is about 20.5" from top bezel). It has small tilt and rotate adjustments. Just enough but if you need more, invest in a desk mount for full rotations and adjustments.
It's standard at 60Hz but supports 75Hz via the displayport. Don't bother with 75hz if you have an Nvidia card. My 1080GTX stutters at 75Hz. That's not LG's fault, it's Nvidia's bad practices.
All in all this is a good monitor for the price range! Only if you have 21:9 content make a purchase otherwise stay away if you are bothered by black bars on the side and back light bleeding in the corners. 21:9 is still a frontier!

IMO this is an outstanding monitor for use with a 2017 MacBook Pro. Please note that it must be connected with an USB-c to Displayport adapter (I bought the highly-rated Monoprice one) and a Displayport cable, to the Displayport on the monitor to get 4k/60hz. If connected by HDMI it will only do 4k/30hz (some much more expensive monitors will do 4k/60hz via HDMI).

Do NOT confuse things with "Mini DisplayPort" - that interface is not used on the monitor OR new MBP, which comes with 2 or 4 USB-C/HDMI/Displayport ports. I initially thought the lack of other ports on the MBP (it has NO other ports!) would be a hassle - but it's not. And the ports are VERY secure - the cables/adapters snap into place and simply don't fall out.

The monitor was detected immediately once connected and powered up. NOTE - the power switch is almost impossible to find without reading the instructions! And because it's also a joystick I won't point out its location - the instructions are important. It works immediately and you will probably be notified of a firmware update - install it right away and the "Displays" setup will change to reflect it. It handles 4K/60hz without any special setup.

I have no problem with "leakage" - but as that is only something that shows when a monitor is on but not used I don't see the point of worrying about it anyway. The screen is crystal-clear, it refreshes fast, and it's great for having two (or more) windows open at a time. I suppose there may be some users that need a more expensive 4k monitor for some reasons, but I have no idea what those reasons would be. I've surfed the web, watched 4K video, edited RAW images and HD videos, and used Logic X for some studio recording and I fail to find anything the monitor could do better!

Highly recommended.

So I am a software developer. Screen real estate is a premium for me. I already had one of the older [[ASIN:B00DOPGO2G Seiki SE39UY04 39-Inch 4K Ultra HD 120Hz LED TV (2013 Model)]] as a secondary display, and love it despite its quirks and 30Hz refresh rate (hard to complain too much about a 4k display for under $400), but I resisted buying a second one of these because I only have one HDMI out, and I wanted a real monitor for my "primary" display.

Enter the new Seiki Pro 40" display, which is actually being marketed as a computer monitor rather than a television. For me this monitor has proven to be full of awesome cakes. It is true that there are other 4k monitors out there -- some that are even cheaper (which is normally Seiki's thing). But at 40", this monitor is bigger. The key point here is that a 40" 4k monitor is not a "retina"-like display. You can use it at native resolution, unlike my 15" macbook pro, which has a screen resolution of 2880x1800, but Apple defaults to having it behave like a very well aliased 1440x900 display. What this means for me is that I get more data visible at once rather than the insanely crisp images of a smaller screen at the same resolution. For me this is perfect, but you might be looking for the "retina"-like effect, so you may prefer a smaller 4k display. You might also not have the room or desire to have something this big on your desk. It's a matter of taste, but just be aware that at 2-3 feet away, a 40" display is in fact massive.

That said, the picture is sharp. 111dpi isn't "retina", but it's still sharper than a 30" 2560x1600 panel. It's got a slightly better picture quality than the 39" mentioned earlier -- and I like that display too. Texts are clear. Colors seem great for my purposes, but note I am not a graphics artist, so admittedly I am not as picky here. It has really neat PIP and multiple input options that I am not using, I could see the using four inputs at 1080p displaying simultaneously as a useful feature for someone else. The 60Hz refresh is snappy. The built in stand is "no frills", but is solid, comes set to a sane height, and if you really wanted it has a VESA mount so you could replace the stand.

I have one minor complaint, and that is that the menu/settings interface is painful. There are plenty of options, but the button presses to navigate are just horrible. This is actually very typical of monitors, but given the features such as PIP that you might want to set/change often, it's kind of a downside. It's unconventional, but it feels like it should have had a remote control (even a wired on that slotted into the base on the stand for storage would have been nicer). Other than that, you might want to wait for Seiki to rerelease this display with HDMI 2.0 and Display Port 1.3. For my purposes, Display Port 1.2 is good enough, but Seiki has promised the updated specifications. There are a couple of minor one-time quirks that you should be aware of just so you don't spend time banging your head during initial setup. First, there is a physical power supply switch next to the place the power cord plugs in. This ships in the off position. If you don't flip this switch, the monitor will not power on. Not noticing this could lead you to believe that the display was DOA. Second, the display has a setting in the menus for Display Port version. It defaults to 1.1. You will not get 60Hz at 4k resolution if you do not change this setting. I also had to reboot my mac after changing the setting to get the computer to notice. Your mileage may vary in terms of needing to reboot, but you will need to change the setting. Lastly, I took the advice of another review and bought a better display port cord when I ordered this. I never tried the one that came with it, but the [[ASIN:B005H3Q5E0 Cable Matters® Gold Plated DisplayPort to DisplayPort Cable 10 Feet - 4K Resolution Ready]] I bought with the monitor is working great.

All and all, if you actually want a display that is this large, this is a solid choice. I am quite happy with my purchase, and would recommend it, with the size caution given. The only caveat I would have is that depending on your use case, you might be willing to buy the older 39" television I referenced, quirks and all, for quite a bit less money.

(the attached image shows my cluttered desk -- this monitor is in the center, the 39" TV (older) on the right. Above it is an old Dell 30" display, and left is a rotated 20" display. The iPad mini and keyboard should give you an idea of relative scale.)

It takes about 5-6 clicks and around 20 seconds to switch inputs. WHY?? Everything else about this monitor is so well done and thoughtful. It's beautiful, and I love using for work and games. This monitor is great if you enjoy having large amounts of screen real estate without the division of multiple monitors. It's fantastic for watching high definition video. For gaming expect to have to work a bit for certain games to get the full experience. Some menus and UIs go off screen, but I have found plenty of help online for getting games like Skyrim to play nicely with this monitor. It's well worth the extra effort! Also, full screen-ing videos from the web will often not completely fill the display, but that is to be expected of a less common aspect ratio.

Summary: A light weight, thin monitor with a beautiful display (provided you don't get one with light bleed issues), but poorly designed OSD & controls (no remote or dedicated input switcher). Expect to have some games not play nice out of the box, but most can be configured to run properly at 3440x1440. Fantastic for doing any kind of development, photo editing, and watching videos.

I have two computers--a primary and an auxiliary--connected to two monitors, and can easily switch between having both connected to one computer as a dual-headed configuration or having each computer have one monitor. When I upgraded my primary monitor to a 28" one (at 1920x1200), my secondary monitor at only 20 inches and 1200x1080 suddenly became claustrophobic. This model was an excellent upgrade to be my new secondary monitor.

Note that this is not offered as a current generation monitor (check the specs). However none of the minor differences mattered to me, for example the lack of an HDMI port is trivially remedied with a cheap, readily available adapter (or using the DVI port on your video card). For me, the advantages of a higher resolution would rule this model out as my _primary_ monitor, but as the secondary monitor, it is more than adequate--the far side is far enough away that I use it for status monitors only. If you intend this for a dual-headed system, recognize that a mismatch in height (1050 vs 1080 or 1200) can cause resizing of windows when you move them between screens, plus other alignment issues. The shorter screen can also cause difficulties for windows extended across multiple screens or turning multiple physical screens into a single virtual screen.

The screen has very good picture quality (recognize that it needs to be adjusted because the default is what looks good in a store display). When playing movies from my DVD player (not Blu-Ray), I saw no smearing or other artifacts. No dead pixels.

The frame is matte, which I prefer because it is not reflective.

The adjustment buttons have no labels on the frame, but have well-aligned displays on the screen. The adjustment I use the most is to switch between the DVI and the VGA/D-Sub ports. This takes seven--count them seven--button pushes to accomplish (Any-3-4-4-3-4/2-3) which was initially annoying, but a sequence my finger quickly learned, although I still have about a 25% error rate.

The printed documentation that comes with the monitor is less than minimal: It needs to include a map of the OSD (On Screen Display) menus to help you avoid miscues during initial setup. Since the full manual is on the accompanying CD, this matters only if you are setting up a system in isolation.

I didn't have much expectation for the built-in speakers (because of the physical and cost constraints): I expected to use them only as space-saving replacements for the cheap standalone speakers on my auxiliary system that provided alert tones. Surprisingly, these speakers proved adequate for listening to human voices, not crossing into the region where I would classify them as "tinny". I was able to listen to extended discussions without the fatigue or irritation that accompanies tinny speakers (such as the ones they replaced).

However, these speakers are _very_ weak. They are adequate if you are sitting in front of the monitor. At 6 feet to the side or 10 feet in front of the monitor, I couldn't hear enough of what was being said to follow a presentation. I have the volume on the monitor and on the sound chip at 100% and typically have the application sound level also at 100%, just for sitting 30-inches away.

The stability of the stand is well above average for its class. I adopted an adult cat at roughly the time I got this monitor. He was chasing an insect attracted by the light from the monitor and, not yet knowing house-rules, used the top of the frame to help him reach upward. The monitor barely moved (and I added earthquake tie-downs so as not to tempt fate).

The stand allows the monitor to be positioned facing slightly downward (for example, you want to put your secondary monitor on a shelf above your primary). Once you pass vertical, the display become shaky, although there doesn't appear to be risk of it tipping over without significant additional force.

The monitor comes in an amazingly small box, which I assume was intended to save space for B&M stores, but appears to be well designed to handle normal abuse: Mine was in fine shape despite having endure a bit of a beating during shipment as evidence by the damage to the slightly larger outer box.

2011-03-19 addition: The _neck_ of the monitor stand is awkward to remove and difficult to replace (the base is designed for easy on/off). This situation arose because I put my monitor, via the VESA-D bracket, on an extensible arm. Removing the base was enough for when I was simply using the arm to reposition the monitor, but I also used arm to swing the monitor into portrait-mode (I use applications that just love to pile up the tool bars and bottom panels), and the neck restricted how close I could put it to my second monitor (to the left, my arm pivots clockwise). My advice is to _not_ remove the neck until you have committed to using the VESA-D mount. Details: The neck blocks access to the screws that attach the hinge to the back of the monitor, so if you want to remove both (likely), you first need to detach the neck from the hinge and then detach the hinge from the monitor. Although this just involves Philips #1 screws, the screws connecting the neck to the hinge are deep inside the neck. It was awkward unscrewing them (my extensive collection of screwdrivers didn't have a #1 with a longer shaft), but screwing them back in was "challenging" because the space was a bit too confined for my normal tools for positioning a screw.

Why a One Star Rating on a monitor that is pretty awesome? Well first, the included monitor stand is wobbly, you're going to need to VESA mount this monitor otherwise when you bump the table slightly the monitor's going to do the wave.

But LORD HELP ME the ASUS Customer Service was GARBAGE.

Long story short, upon screwing the VESA mount into the back panel of the monitor, the internal metal plates that hold the VESA mount broke and are now dangling loose inside the monitor. And no, there' s no user or physical damage, it was a strait defect. It happens, no big deal. So I thought. ASUS is a huge name, with a great warranty. So I thought.

First, ASUS was adamant about charging me, saying it'd be up to some random tech to decide if I'd be charged or not and there was nothing I can do. I get the clause to cover themselves just in case I sent in a completely busted monitor, but I explained the situation and they really didn't care. And they're rude AF. Like all of the 7 or so people I talked to . They just didn't give a ****.

First call, I couldn't talk to a manager because it was a holiday weekend (understandable). Then when I called back this morning (call # 2), they couldn't process the RMA because of a "software update" but promised to call back and they'd handle it.Took down all my info, explained terms, everything. Then at Call # 3, at 3rd level escalation, they said they "Didn't have stock, but I could send it in for repair and wait for it to be shipped back to me". This is after being promised they'd send me an RMA replacement first, and then I'd ship the old one back. Side note, they refused to accept the monitor in the original package (why?!? That's the safest way to send it back) , and they refused to reimburse any type of packaging materials that would be needed to, I don't know, send a computer monitor through the mail safely. Crazy, right? Let me just go to UPS and buy $20 worth of padding to send it back , just to have ASUS say "O you broke it, give me money".

Did they have a system where they could add my case, and when a replacement was available, then they'd send it? You know, like a wait list or queue that every major company has ? NOPE. They literally advised me to check in a couple times a day to "see if they had it in stock" .

The tier 3 escalations manager went so far as to say " well whatever amazon has on their site is probably wrong, this product isn't eligible for Advanced RMA replacement".

I purchased 3 of these monitors, and they were a great buy. But god help you if you try to call ASUS customer service.

But I do want to say that Amazon cleared this up in like under 5 minutes. Keep in mind the average wait time for ASUS was over 40 minutes.. and I had to call multiple times.

Thank you Amazon. As always, you're amazing, and I won't purchase anywhere else. Screw you ASUS. At this price range, get yourself a monitor that has DisplayPort In & Out (so you don't have 3 usb-c adapters connected to my MacBook Pro like I do ) and that doesn't wobble on the included stand. Seriously.

You can see in the included photos how the screw slot looks on my other 2 working monitors, and how the metal hinge fell out and is now hanging loosely by the air vent.

I get manufacturing defects happen, it's cool, I wanted to work with ASUS and not be a pain in the butt. But after that customer service experience, I'm shopping else where and you should do the same.

I recently acquired a MacBook Pro with Retina display. I am also a photographer and would like to be able to edit images in Lightroom or Photoshop on the go. The screen on the Mac looked great to me but I knew for maximum accuracy I needed to profile the screen to match the standards that would be the same as the labs used. The X-Rite i1 Display Studio was an affordable option and X-Rite has been the industry leader in color accuracy for decades.

The set up was easy and the profiling was a snap. In less than 30 minutes I had profiled my MacBook. I highly suggest watching the videos on the landing page if you are not familiar with the process of profiling a monitor.

When I compared the before and after images I immediately noticed a subtle reduction in contrast. I thought to myself I will lose the “snap” I like to see in my images. After looking through my images in Lightroom what I was amazed to see is all the subtle tones I was missing before profiling. Even the screen saver images look much better now. I can edit with confidence and not over or under correct based on an inaccurate display.

X-Rite has created a user friendly, affordable profiling system that is a great tool for pros and serious hobbyists as well. I am very happy.

This monitor measures 14 1/2 inches wide, 8 7/8 inches tall, and 3/8 inch thick. It weights 2 1/2 lbs. This monitor comes with a magnetic cover/case/stand that is similar to ones that I have seen with iPads. It utilizes the magnetics of the cover to set the monitor up as an angled stand. It works quite well, saving weight in having a built in or separate stand. It is dual functioning. The matte screen is quite clear when I used it with the USB-C male connector on both ends. This allows connectivity without having to use a wall plug or other power source. There is a trackwheel on the side that controls volume as well as selections on the menu. There are two USB-C female outlets on the other side, as well as mini HDMI. There is a USB-C cable with the males on both ends, a cable with standard HDMI on one end, and mini HDMI on the other. There is a wall adapter with regular USB outlet, and a cable with regular USB on on end and USB-C on the other. The kit comes pretty much complete with cables and adapters that are needed. This monitor also has a speaker on each side. I wouldn’t call it high performance sound by any stretch, but it had much more treble than my muffled speakers on my laptop, so it’s adequate. All things considered, this reasonably light, thin, sleek monitor .

The "hub" aspect of this monitor is fantastic. Everything plugs into the monitor itself—all your USB gear (mouse, keyboard, backup drive, microphone, whatever you've got)—even ethernet! And then ONE cable connects to your laptop, and even charges it.

The screen is immersive and curved just the right amount, and the color fidelity is sensational. The problem, though, is the resolution—the number of pixels per inch (ppi): only 110 ppi. For comparison, the MacBook Pro's screen is double that in each direction: 218 ppi.

If you're used to a Mac, the Dell's image is upsettingly blurry. You'll be in great shape for photo and video editing. But for reading, editing text, or using apps like Indesign and Illustrator, you'll really mourn that low resolution.

I wound up returning this monitor. (BEWARE! They changed me a $235 "restocking fee" after I returned it!! There is NOTHING about this on the item page.)

I bought the LG 34WK95U-W UltraFine 34-inch screen instead (it's ultrawide but NOT curved). It's got 163 ppi, which looks razor sharp, and I'm in heaven. (It's also much more expensive; now you know that there's a direct correspondence between resolution/size and price!)

The LG isn't a connection hub like the Dell; it has only two standard USB jacks on the back. But by adding a little $12 USB dock to the back of the LG, I still get the convenience of having only ONE cable connect to the laptop!

First Impressions
I use the monitor for business applications (MS Office mostly Outlook, Excel, Word and PowerPoint, plus Internet browsing, etc... I'd like to have a larger screen with higher resolution such as the Dell 27 inches, but I think that at the present time, this is where the sweet spot for performance vs value is (24 inches).

I've been using a Dell UltraSharp 2405 monitor, which I got from Dell as a replacement when the original died one month or so short from the expiration date of its 3 years warranty, and which is working quite well after about 3+ years, but I had read positive comments about the U2412M in CNET and other web sites, and was considering upgrading. However, I had also read conflicting comments here in Amazon about the anti-glare coating, and about color accuracy. I've always thought that the anti-glare screen of the DELL 2405 was perfect for me and I'm not very concerned with color accuracy (within reason) as I'm not into photography, graphic arts or anything like that... But when you read reviews like that, it makes you pause. In any case, I decided to try it and decide for myself, and I am glad I did.

For what I do, and at this price point, this monitor is just fine. No issue at all with the anti-glare coating. I can't tell if there's a difference or not versus the coating on the Dell 2405. In fact, had I not read about it, I would not even known anything about that. And for my business application, the default factory-set color calibration is also OK. I do not plan to tweak it.

The images are very sharp. Fine and/or small text is easy to ready. I also like the new base and support arm better, and the fact that it's so much lighter. Congratulations DELL!

Having said that, it does not have some of the connections available in the Dell 2405 (i.e. SD card ports), but it has others such as DisplayPort. An HDMI port would have been nice to have, but since I use a Dell E Latitude docking station, I am using the DisplayPort and/or DVI port. It comes with a power cable (of course) and also a USB cable, a VGA cable and a DVI cable. At this point in time, I'd rather had seen a DVI and a DisplayPort cable inside the box, and not the legacy VGA cable, but it may be helpful for other users. Not an issue anyway.

A few days after having received the new monitor, I decided that for this price it was worth having a dual set, so I ordered another one. Now my EXCEL spreadsheets can finally stretch as wide as necessary, or I can work in two applications side-by-side. Nice and worth it.
great monitor, The driver didn't install but it works fine, only took 2 weeks to get here though

First off, I'm giving the monitor 5 stars despite a single dead pixel on the screen. I know that might turn a few people off, yet I can't really notice it, even when I search for it unless the screen is powering on or off. So I find this forgivable.

I was pretty surprised to have my monitor come in a box with ALL of the cables, and even plastic still attached to the monitor. It didn't look used at all, and if I'm guessing the reason it ended up at the discount part of Amazon is due to the single dead pixel.

I think the picture quality of 1440p along with the high refresh rate is great. I have only noticed artifacts once or twice which really did not detract from the gaming experience.

I think 27" is right at the large end for gaming monitors. I sit pretty close to it, yet it seems to be fine for me. I don't think it's too big.

For working on every day things I really enjoy the extra space, it makes it a LOT easier to work on different things, watch movies, or even browse the web at home.

The only drawback I can really think of is the sand. I don't mind the color scheme since red is my favorite color. If you don't like red that might be a problem for you. I also find that the stand takes up a decent amount of desk space as it is both wide and deep. I don't find this to be a problem, because I can place my keyboard on top of the stand when not in use. Yet, if you're going for a clean desktop look, than you might want to consider replacing the stand for one with a smaller profile.

Perhaps it is the way I type. However sometimes I notice that when I'm typing that the monitor shakes just a little bit. Again I don't find this to be annoying, but I could see how this might annoy some people. I also think I might be a little heavy handed on the keys when I'm typing.

Overall, I think it's a great value for being at a discount. I would recommend it to a friend, and I would buy it again if given the option.

Item: Dell Ultrasharp UZ2715H 27-Inch LED-Lit Monitor
This is a fantastic monitor with a max resolution of 1920 x 1080. The text and graphics are very sharp, definitely noticeable compared to my Viewsonic 27", and the colors are spectacular. I only had to decrease the brightness slightly. The LED backlight seems completely even all around and the screen looks amazing because of it. Using it mainly for professional projects like photo and video editing, the clarity is superb. The speed of the screen is 8ms, which is perfect for general and photo/video editing. For PC gaming, you may need something faster.
The monitor offers decent sound. All cables necessary are included as well. Specifics are listed below in the INCLUDED section.

I have not run my Spyder 3 on this monitor yet, but the colors are still very impressive and I was able to get very close to my other Spyder calibrated monitor without using it on here. The sound quality of the speaker is decent. I prefer using my separate CREATIVE speakers for any sound and they are connected to my PC.

CONNECTIONS: If you look at the pictures, the recessed area holds the connections, where the cables will plug in vertically. Connections followed by number of ports: Display Port (1), HDMI (2), VGA D-sub (1), USB 3.0 SS upstream port (1), USB 3.0 downstream ports (2) which requires upstream port to be connected first.

Power on the monitor using the power button. All the buttons are touch sensitive. Upon first power-up, it went to VGA even though I had my computer on and HDMI cables connected. Press the button to the left of the on button to bring up the input menu. The buttons will light up with the function above it on the LCD screen. First thing I noticed upon startup was how clear the text and graphics of the Win 7 Pro startup screen was. The power button is the only light that is on and it's a comfortable white light in the shape of the on symbol. My computer's resolution filled the screen completely. On other monitor's I had the issue where the full screen was not being filled, even though the resolution was at 1920x1080. If you get this issue, it is a SCALING issue with your computer's video card and you need to access the video card setting and change the scaling. I had no issues with scaling on this Dell Ultrasharp monitor.

DETAILS:
Monitor dimensions (with frame) is 16.5" H x 26" W x 1/4" D. The LCD area of the monitor is 13.5" H x 23.5" W.
Monitor base is 8.75" W x 7.75" D and 1/4 to 1/2" thick. The monitor can be tilted slightly upwards or downwards. But this model cannot be raised in height because the stand isn't built like that. The speaker is a small strip along the bottom of the monitor which provides decent sound.

INCLUDED: HDMI cable (5 ft 8"), USB 3.0 upstream cable (6 ft), DisplayPort cable (5 ft 10") and the power cable/power block.
Power cable and power block is a total of 113" (or 9 ft 5"). The power cable from the monitor to the block is 70", the block is 5" long, and the cable from the block to the outlet is 38" for a total of 113". And the Drivers and Documentation CD.

There is a webcam on the monitor which I don't use, but it is a 2MP camera. I don't use the microphone port either. I prefer using my computer's connections for those instead of the monitor's. I don't really do any desktop video-conferencing at all. But for general use, and pro use, this monitor is excellent. Super clear, amazing colors, and a nice 27" to open multiple windows at once.

I bought this as a second monitor to connect to my laptop and have been satisfied with the purchase. The monitor set-up was extremely easy - it took me longer pulling the packaging out of the box than actually hooking everything up.  It came with the HDMI cable, which was all I needed for connection. I definitely agree with the other reviews that the sound is not great at all (a bit muffled, even) - but for the price of the monitor, it's fine. The sound from my laptop is much better quality, so I just unhook if I want a higher quality sound for something. I was primarily looking for a basic monitor so I could work from multiple documents and programs at the same time - and this gets the job done.

Cons

The backlight finally gave out, making it useless. There's something wrong with the way they made the circuitry for this model. I recommend looking for a different brand.

So I am re-writing my review after having this monitor for a while.

This monitor is amazing. The 32:10 aspect ratio is absolutely amazing, for gaming, for productivity, AND watching movies is an absolute dream. I am thinking about buying another for work ( I work in IT )

The only reason I am giving this 4 stars is because the HDR is pretty much useless BUT manually tweaking the picture settings, you can get amazing color out of this. Also, the OSD menu is super slow and clunky which can be annoying.

I am not detracting stars for it, but the speakers are absolute garbage. To be fair though, nobody really gets a monitor for the speaker quality. They are there if you need them, but they aren't great.

I have had no dead pixels or super bad light bleed like some other users have, maybe I got hit the panel lottery. I did have issues a bit with the flickering but that seems to have gone away. I have adjusted the settings a bit, but I can't pinpoint what exactly resolved that issue.

Gamers - I have had to turn on v-sync because I've had some bad tearing. The nVidia control panel lets me turn on g-sync but states that it isn't officially supported. It does not work with this monitor at this time. So, just be aware of that if you are considering this monitor.

Productivity users - DO NOT HESITATE to buy this monitor. I can't praise this enough in its usefulness for work. I was able to fully view a users 3 screens on this when I had to remote into their workstation to assist them. Spreadsheets can be seen for days. Powershell scripting which I love to do, is phenomenal. 10 out of 5 stars for productivity and work users.

A small desire of this monitor is I wish it had some usb ports and a headset hanger that I've gotten spoiled from having on my Ben-Q monitor.

Side note: you will need a decent GPU to get the 120hz out of this if you are using it for productivity. My work laptop only has integrated intel UHD and could only run this at 60hz, its not a deal breaker or bad, though the smoothness of 120hz is a nice luxury. My personal pc has a 1080ti which outputs to a 240hz 1080p monitor and this at its full 3840x1200 resolution at 120hz. It does keep the temps a little higher as well outputting to both so you may want to adjust the fan speed for your GPU cooling.

I agree, out of the box, not good at all, adjusting on the monitor, also very little improvement, but after some tweaking and gamma reduction in the Nvidia CP, they are close to 99% of my IPS panel sitting next to it.  Very impressed and the 144hz is fantastic, even with a non-freesync card, the FPS are so high at 1080p it's not required.  Highly recommended for the price.

I admit I was skeptical about purchasing two of these Korea-originating monitors at the same time, but the specs I wanted were all there and I really wanted to upgrade my excellent – and very pricey - Dell 24" monitors I've had for many years without breaking the bank. I was looking forward to gaming on my bigger, higher resolution monitors, but I was committed and almost resigned to sending them back if they did not meet all my expectations. I must say my initial expectations were highly exceeded.

Both arrived securely packaged and were easy to install. Both fired up without a hitch or any extensive calibration. DVI and DisplayPort connectivity testing was seamless, and I must say I was highly surprised and pleased with the excellent picture quality right out of the chute. I put both monitors through their paces by running the 3D Mark and Heaven benchmark tests with settings maxed out, and they performed flawlessly. I easily overclock the stock 60hz refresh rate to 100hz on both monitors without any in-game frame issues or stuttering of any kind. I probably could have gone up to the full 120hz as stated in the item description, but I didn't wish to push any luck. Besides, any benefits to bumping past 100hz are negligible in my opinion. It was an obvious step up going from my former monitors' 1920 x 1200, locked 60hz refresh rate to 2560 x 1440 at 100hz for about the same price as just one of the "name brand" monitors I extensively researched for days.

Pros:
• Can't beat the value as it's a great price for the picture quality and color reproduction. Gaming was much more immersive and vibrant.
• Lots of customization options and display modes
• Both units were easily overclocked for that fantastic refresh rate that rivals some of the top-of-the line brands
• Ran cool and buttery smooth at 120hz during prolonged periods of triple-A title FPS gaming.

Cons:
• Build quality of the chassis seemed a bit on the cheaper side and "plasticky", but then I don't plan on carting these large beasts around.
• The stand that comes with the monitor seems at best limited, but I never planned to use it anyway.
• The mounting area on the back of the monitor is a bit rounded so it does not sit flush with the plates on my dual monitor VESA mount and the mounting holes seemed a bit shallow; I was able to overcome this easily with a few cheap hardware store rubber washers that tightened up the fit just fine.
• The rear base of the monitor does have an approximate 3-inch plastic "tail", intended to join with the included stand apparatus, that is not removable without voiding the warranty. I can easily overlook it, but for some it might be a bit of an eyesore.
• While the on board controls work as intended, there is somewhat of a learning curve of button mashing in navigating the rather impressive amount of control settings.

Conclusion: While the cons may seem to outnumber the pros, they were all very minor issues to me when compared to the picture quality, the customization and, most of all, the value. Overall, I'm very impressed with this product. Time will tell if these monitors hold out as long as my workhorse Dells did, but I'm hoping they'll exceed my expectations in that area as well.

Bought this as an extended monitor for my Lenovo U400 with the switchable Intel / AMD Radeon graphics. It crashed my system to the dreaded blue dump screen. Luckily I was able to reboot by going to the F-8 boot options and selecting "boot from a previous configuration".

I downloaded the latest DisplayLink driver, Version 7.0M3, and it still crashed the system when I attached the monitor and rebooted. I called Customer Service at both AOC and DisplayLink. After some fix tries the bottom line is they don't have a fix for this yet. The incompatibility is with the AMD Catalyst program which is needed to run the switchable graphics and the DisplayLink driver.

I had to return the monitor to Amazon. If I can verify DisplayLink and/or AMD fix the issue I may reorder it. Ordering the other Monitor alternatives (Toshiba or Lenovo) won't work either as they both use DisplayLink drivers.

So, I give it the one star as it crashes my system but from the reviews it works well on other systems with non switchable graphics.

I had just come off using my 27" iMac for three years and decided I needed a new monitor that was as good or better than my iMac. I'm a professional photographer and a graduate student, so I also game on the side.

My basic requirements:
1. A decent color gamut
2. Large working space (at least 27")
3. (Highly preferable) Fast refresh time

There were a couple options besides this monitor I considered.

I definitely didn't want to pay a $1000 for a Dell or Apple Thunderbolt display. They might've been the very best, but I couldn't justify the price in my mind. Alternatively, I might've been happy with a Korean B+ IPS panel, but I wanted just that little edge for my photography. I hear the colors are decent on those panels, but not great - I'm willing to pay a little more for more colors. I guess what really threw me over for the PB278Q was the fact that these monitors are matte (and not heavily laid on either), come with warranty, no-hassle returns and low likeliness of defects.

So, on one side, you have $1000 high-end panels that you know will knock the socks across the Pacific Ocean. On the other side, you have Korean panels that run about $400 that are no frills and get the job done. I opted for a happy place in the middle, and this monitor suited the bill.

The PB278Q is an excellent choice for those who are visual artists and casual gamers. It hits about 98% of the sRGB color gamut and about 90% of the Adobe RGB. Not amazing, but also, quite close enough if you need colors in the ball park and aren't creating commercial material. If you're creating commercial material, you will definitely need to look elsewhere for something that is extremely discerning. You probably already know this, and are probably willing to shell out $2,000 for a 10-bit monitor from NEC that's five inches smaller. This is definitely not for you. This, however, gets you right in the ballpark to hit a homerun if you're shooting a wedding or delivering portraits. In fact, I think the color gamut is better than my iMac. And again, I'll emphasize the awesomeness of the matte screen: you can look at it for hours on end without fatigue, and not worry about that "grime" that often comes with anti-glossiness.

The monitor is easy to calibrate, and using the Spyder 4, it was a cinch to get done - the factory settings aren't too far from the calibration tool. The stand pivots AND swivels. In other words, it can turn left, right, up and down, and the base can move with it too 360 degrees. You can also rotate the screen independent of the stand 90 degrees in portrait mode. Further, there are many types of ports - HDMI, DVI-D ports and VGA. There's apparently also a plug for sound but I don't have it in.

For gamers, this is a good choice. Contrast is strong, it's fast, minimal blur for the specs. I would personally recommend a Korean B+ panel if you only do gaming, are working on studies or browse the internet. This monitor can do those jobs just fine, but you will not be using it to its fullest potential, therefore is a waste of money. So, if you game - this will suit you fine. I just only recommend it if you also do graphic arts, video or photography.

I waited for 10 years for an e-ink monitor, and finally made the dive with this 2019 version (includes touchscreen and optional frontlight). I've struggled with screen-headaches forever, especially using a computer in the morning. I've managed to reduce them with regular sunglasses, but only can get 3 hrs a day in front of screen. So I figured Dasung would have removed most of the bugs out by now - and they have.

I intended to use this in front of my laptop screen, and it worked better than I thought because Windows 10 keeps all the settings correct. So if I ever need to switch back, it's really easy. So I bring it along with me to coffee shops every other day for work, and set ontop my laptop (see screenshot).

It doesn't use that much battery power on my laptop since the main screen is off. The ghosting was strange at first, but not bad once you re-set themes to light instead of dark. It's not distracting ultimately, and there's a "C" (clear) button that removes all ghosts instantly like shaking an etch-a-sketch. Videos play fine and mouse navigation is slow but not annoying. The tough part is retraining oneself to work within a black and white environment, since many buttons in programs are color coded, and I take that for granted. Some detail is lost in contrast/dark pictures and videos. But writing text and browsing internet is great, and watching videos for basic content is fine.

So I'm super happy because - I have no more fatigue and headaches! It delivers on its most important promises, so that's awesome. And this is true with or without frontlight on.

Great looking monitor but horrible QA. The monitor I received had two dead pixels one stuck pixel and several pixels that were prone to sticking totaling about 5 problematic pixels at any time. Such a disappointment after spending about $400 on it. I went with another brand and have had zero issues.

-It has a SPEAKER, which isn't obvious from the product details. Mediocre sound quality, but still usable. Convenient for watching youtube and video clips. I even use it to watch Netflix from across the room, its loud enough. The sound comes out from the top ventilation grille on the back. The volume controls are difficult to figure out, I just leave it at 100% and use Windows volume control.

-1680x1050 native resolution is a good balance, gives a lot of room without making text way too small to read. Aspect ratio is great for using the Windows 7 "snap" feature for having two windows open - I now use this feature ALL the time. There is a single dead pixel at the very top edge, but I never notice it, its always in the dark edge of whatever window is open.

-Color, brightness and contrast look good. I'm not a designer so I can't nitpick any more than that, but it looks as good as the expensive Dell monitors in my school library. The only adjustment I had to make was the ClearType settings, which initially made the text look blurry - it took a while to adjust with the cleartype wizard but looks good now.

-It does not have a telescoping stand, and the bottom edge of the monitor is closer to the desktop than appears in the photo, only about 2 inches. The only adjustable movement is the tilt, which can be pushed downward-facing about 5 degrees from vertical (good if you are watching netflix from laying in bed across the room), and upward facing about 10 degrees from vertical (if you're tall). Its plenty light so pushing it around your desk is easy.

-The monitor controls are difficult to figure out, but I never mess with them anyway. They are very discreet, which is nice, and the tiny blue power LED is the only light on the whole panel, which I like.

-The bezel is about 3/4 of an inch wide, and shiny plastic which gets fingerprints. The ASUS logo looks like it was designed in the 80's, and is a mirrored insert, so i cant scratch it off.

-Asus has the weirdest tech slogan I've ever seen, on their shipping box: "Rock Solid - Heart Touching". Sounds like a bad Engrish translation.

I'm very happy with this, and you probably can't find a cheaper 22" monitor anywhere.

It seems that this is an issue many others have faced. Everything else I tried slipped down very easily and the angle was totally wrong. The “larger” magnet on the one side folds up and snaps (okay, touches) against the top part of the display. Then the bottom can fit more securely in the groove in the front and the display is more supported from the back. You can even fold the smaller section in the front for extra security. There went a half hour of my day figuring it out but now it’s great! Good luck.

Basically this is the same OLED display panel as the legendary 48 inch LG CX/C1 but with the killer features of having a DisplayPort 1.4 connector and none of the awkward "Smart TV" shenanigans getting in the way if you plan on using it as a dumb PC monitor or for gaming and media consumption on PS5/XSX. It boots up quickly from cold straight to your PC's desktop without needing to wait for it to boot up some smart OS or dropping you into a home screen laced with pre-installed bloatware, ads and notification pop ups attempting to trick you into consenting to giving away personal data for datamining your viewing and media consumption habits with ACR. Its just a simple dumb display which is great if you are one of the dozens of us who care about that while still wanting a decent OLED for both gaming and media.

There are a few quirks and limitations to be aware of. PS5 in 4K 120hz HDR mode seems to be limiting quality to YUV 420 over HDMI mode which reduces the image quality which is a bit disappointing given it heavily advertises HDMI 2.1 support while that output mode is a limit of HDMI 2.0b. Also, the OSD for accessing the display's configuration can only be accessed while connected to a Windows PC with some "OSD Sidekick" software installed. The OSD sidekick software seems buggy and crashes/hangs frequently. It also hung while trying to update the firmware causing quite a scare where I had to pull the power plug on the display to get the software to respond again after a failed firmware update. Its also worth calling out this does not support NVIDIA G-SYNC, just FreeSync, meaning you need at least a GTX 10XX series or later to utilize VRR on NVIDIA hardware, so bad news if you are also still stuck on a GTX 980 or lower card during the GPU shortage crisis.

The Samsung UE570 is a 28” 4K UHD monitor with AMD FreeSync giving you a 1ms response time. It has two HDMI 2.0 and 1 DisplayPort connection to give you a wealth of options. I say this because this larger monitor offers a nice Picture by Picture (Picture In Picture as well) mode to increase the fun or your multitasking needs.

From a personal standpoint, I have never had a monitor this large and with such a crisp picture. Early this year, I acquired a 27” from another company and the difference was amazing. I have done everything from 4K video streaming to gaming to productivity tools on it and it’s been wonderful. I have even done things at the same time and it’s held its own. I didn't realize how nice it would be to have a 4K monitor until now. It’s now a staple of my work-home computer setup. I run with a 20”, 27” and 28” connected to a PC and a laptop.

After extensive use, I really only have a few minor issues with it. The first is the stand. It’s rather large and protrudes to the front and back a little more than say a square platform stand. With how wide it is, it makes it hard to set up on a desk. It also doesn’t swivel or move up or down. It does tilt though. I will probably mount it at some point to alleviate that issue. The next one is the control for the settings. It took some time, but I finally got used to the “joystick controller” that does it all. I would have preferred a few separated buttons for easier maneuvering. The last one is the connectors right in the middle of the back of the monitor. Unless your cables are longer or you have an extension, your placement will be limited. The headphone jack is the strangest one. You will definitely need a longer cable for that one. There are some other items that would be on the wishlist to make this monitor perfect, but they aren’t deal breakers. Things like less bezel and controls/buttons on the front.

All in all, the Samsung UE570 is a great monitor. It has performed well under heavy gaming, streaming sports, and movie watching. It may not be designed specifically for those tasks, but it can certainly hold its own. After playing with this one, I might need a second one to even everything out. I recommend others consider doing the same.

My Predator x34 review-

4 stars due to some minor flaws, but don't let that stop you from considering this panel. Simply put, it's excellent! The colors are on point, many options for contrast and brightness adjustment, and it has the most screen real estate I've ever worked with on a single monitor. Yes, I know I'm singing its praise, but let me explain...

Pros:
1. G-Sync (silky smooth gaming)
2. IPS panel (Exceptional color coverage, 100% sRGB, 80% Adobe RGB)
3. Resolution, 3440 X 1440 (Tons of screen estate)
4. Speed (100Hz refresh rate + 4ms response time)
5. Stand adjustment options (height and tilt)
6. Heavy base (next to no wobble)

Cons:
1. The settings menu and buttons are clunky and confusing to navigate. (An IR sensor and remote would have been a nice solution for this, or at least helped.)
2. Flickering (only a very small amount on the first day, after all updates and a few tweaks, haven't experienced any since.)
3.Price.

I'm running an overclocked skylake processor, pushing about 4.6 on 4 cores, along with a single overclocked 1080 hybrid Pushing around 2100. I've tested several games such as Doom, Battlefield 1, and Overwatch, and I've had no problem so far pushing 100hz. My 1080 had no problems with ultra settings, but my CPU temps increased slightly, nothing too bad, but it does take a good Nvidia system to get the most out of this monitor.

As for light bleed, out of the box cord lengths, and base depth:

I made it completely dark in the room and left the monitor on a black lit screen (default, out of the box video settings)... next to no light bleed, so compared to the other reviewers who talk about bad light bleed, I may have got lucky with mine. Keep in mind that this is a recently produced panel vs what Acer might have been pumping out a few years back when production started, so the quality control could be better at this stage of production. After that, I adjusted the panels video settings to my liking and saw even less light bleed.

For the cord lengths, I've always had my self-built tower on my desk next to my monitors for easy access, so the cords that came in the box (DP, USB upstream, and others) had plenty of length to reach my tower. My desk is about 6ft in length and from the slack that's left over, I know that if I had my tower sitting on the floor on either side, I'd still have enough length to comfortably reach my tower, just to give you an idea.

Sitting at approximately 12" deep, the base takes up a lot of space on most desktops, so it might be a good idea to prepare your desk for it, maybe a compatible stand, or by extending the depth of your desk in some way like I did.

I did a lot of research before purchasing this monitor, so I knew what I was getting myself into when I placed the order. If you're considering it, just know that it has a few quarks, but IMO, the pros far outweigh the cons, and I have to say it's the best monitor I've ever owned!

Thanks for reading!

I really wanted to like this monitor. I was looking for a computer monitor for my kitchen that could double as a TV. It seemed like it had everything I wanted: good size, TV tuner, built-in speakers, so that I wouldn't have to clutter up my countertops with any other little boxes. I wanted a matte screen to eliminate glare in my well-lit kitchen. Also, it was the largest monitor I could get that would fit underneath my kitchen cabinets. Great price, too. On paper, it seemed like the perfect choice in every way. I checked it out in person and it looked great in the store, too.

On the plus side, the picture is excellent. It's got a really full feature set and, of course, it's a TV, too! It has a decent number of ins and outs but does not have composite or svideo inputs. I would have liked these, but I knew this before I bought it, so I'm not taking off any points for this.

Now for the problems. First, the audio is terrible. I mean really terrible, so bad, it's hardly useable. Now, I wasn't expecting much, as the speakers are quite small and low powered. I saw other reviews and honestly, was expecting thin, tinny sound, which would have been acceptable. What I got was much worse. If I play any kind of music at all, anything with any low frequencies, I get his horrible resonance, this "wuh - wuh - wuh" sound that makes the audio completely unintelligible. Even if I lower the volume below 25%, it's still there. Ugh. For some sounds, like voice, or the Windows system sounds, nothing with any low frequencies or complex sounds, it's marginally acceptable.

Next, this is a computer monitor, right? There's no way to get the monitor to go to low-power standby when using the HDMI inputs. Standby does work when using DVI or VGA, but not when using HDMI. It's actually greyed out in the menu. This is ridiculous. There's no technical reason in the world why this should be a problem. The sleep signals from the computer are identical for HDMI and DVI, so there's no reason for this not to work. They just didn't bother. I thought an obvious oversight like this would have been corrected in a firmware update, but no. Either this thing just sits on your desk, continually burning up 50+ watts, or you have to run two cables, video and audio, and use DVI. That's what I did.

Last issue, Samsung's tech support was really clueless. I was on the phone for almost two hours before the technician finally discovered that, yes, sleep mode is not supported at all for HDMI but is supported for DVI. So sorry. Regarding the audio issues, they were also no help at all. I told them that my computer was fine. I tested my computer with another set of speakers and the sound was fine. I also used a second computer and the monitor's sound was still no good (but the second computer also worked fine with my other speakers). The technician offered no solutions at all, wouldn't even recognize the possibility that maybe the speakers were actually damaged. Are you ready for this? I also tried their automated troubleshooting guide on Samsung's website. It went something like this:

"Are you having problems with distorted sound?" - "Yes"
"Did you try a different set of speakers?" - "Yes"
"Was the sound ok with the other speakers?" - "Yes"
"If this sound is OK with your computer and another set of speakers but no good with our speakers, then the problem is with your computer. Please replace your computer or sound card or change your sound settings."

HUH????

I emailed Samsung about this obvious discrepancy in their troubleshooting guide and got no reply.

To summarize, this would be a really great monitor except for very poor sound quality, no sleep mode in HDMI, and abysmal customer / technical support. I've taken off a star for each.

By the way, I bought this monitor to replace a 19" Samsung LCD that had failed prematurely, just after the warranty expired. I thought I'd give them another chance. I used to consider Samsung my first choice for monitors, offering great performance at a competitive price. Now, I'm not so sure. I gave them every opportunity to correct this.
