THE FOLLOWING ARE COMMENTS FROM MY FIRST REVIEW OF THIS MONITOR IN JANUARY OF 2012

"Before buying this monitor I read every on-line review I could find. I have many thousands of photos that I "fix" and display in various slideshows. Most are Family photos ranging from the Civil War to now. Getting the picture "right" is REALLY hard with a TN display. The lighting reflections don't bother me , but having the colors and brightness change so noticeably with even a couple of inches deviation in the viewing angle is Really Bad when you spend many hours at this task.

I became convinced that I needed a "professional" monitor. I auditioned the HP 24 inch IPS and the Viewsonic 23" IPS but was disappointed in the Anti Glare coating. As a previous reviewer noted, the agressive AG coating matte screen effect is like looking at pictures and text through a dirty window. Depressing.....

This Monitor is GORGEOUS. No reflection worth mentioning, and the Bright Colors, make the photos and text really a pleasure to view. This should satisfy both the people who like Matte ( no reflections) AND those who like the Brightness of Glossy screens. I was getting to the point of seriously checking out an Apple Cinema Display, but didn't want to get into the hassle needed to mate it with a PC when I saw THIS display reviewed on the internet. It still took me a month to finally order it due to the past disappointing experiences of the Viewsonic and HP Matte IPS screens. I can truthfully say I AM REALLY,REALLY HAPPY WITH THIS DISPLAY and would recommend it to all.

My order came with the DVI cable included. The specifications were kind of vague about whether this type of cable was included. It was a single link DVI, not a dual link. but it is certainly adequate to display the 1920x1200 resolution recommended. I hooked up an alternate dual link DVI cable I had around and it doesn't really give you anything more.

The Brightness was REALLY HIGH ( turned to 100% as received. I like Bright screens, but this was searing my eyeball. I turned it down to 70% and it is still very bright. I saw no dead pixels, or Uneven light distributions as some have noticed ( it might be there , but I didn't notice it)

Before ordering , I went to the Samsung website and downloaded the manual, and read it before the monitor arrived. I'm glad I did because the manual, though included, is on a CD) and the Printed instructions in the box are not very helpful for assembling the stand and various cabling options.

As noted by others, there is a clasp to hold the power pack convertor at the top of the back of the monitor that keeps it secure and out of sight. I was doing well, all cables plugged into the proper ports, checking each step as described in the manual. I plugged in the power cord, turned on the power switch on top of the convertor, and....Nothing Happened....... I re-checked the set up instructions checked all the all the cables and cords , tried a different outlet connection, etc., and still Nothing..... And THEN I pushed the button on the FRONT of the Display and..... VOILA!!!!!!!! ....... so, remember to TURN IT ON for best results."

Waste of time ordering this monitor. Choose this monitor since it was the fastest to arrive but ended up to be a disappointment.

But I wanted to leave a review of my research and also address some of the reviews. First, the pixel density of this monitor at 2560x2880 28" comes to 137ppi. This should be good enough to produce a decent enough text, but if you are using a mac with retina display, those screens have over 200ppi. It will not look the same.

Now, you may still observe fuzzy text on this monitor but that won't be due to the PPI on its own. You see, there are things called subpixel antialiasing and font hinting. At the days of CRT monitors, we didn't have a pixel grid to worry about. With the LCDs, software now needs to (or should) match the pixels on the physical screen. But that's not entirely possibly when it comes to fonts, so the artifacts are hinted, and then antialiased (subpixel or grayscale). Some software does this better than the others, for instance you may observe really fuzzy font rendering on vscode (any electron app for that matter) than compared to sublime. When you have ppi over 200, this fuzziness will be less perceivable to you, but you will still notice rendering differences if you open sublime and vs code side by side, and it may still cause you eye strain.

Moving on, what this monitor addresses is eliminating the last bit of cause for the fuzziness and eye strain. You see again, this pixel grid on the LCDs are 99.99% RGB, which means the individual pixels are ordered in RED, GREEN, BLUE. When you rotate a normal monitor 90 degrees, you are basically messing up the pixel order, and on a lower ppi display you will notice color bleeding around the characters. I stopped using windows altogether, but up until latest windows 10, I am pretty sure it does not allow you to specify the pixel order such as VRGB. On linux you could do that to an extend, I have no idea how mac does.

And finally, the last cause of fuzziness and enjoying your typing is refresh rate and input lag. 60hz, 5ms is simply not great enough. There are also many other factors that adds to the input lag. Mostly relate to the so called superior usb interface (yes PS2 was faster running on a dedicated IRQ). Does this monitor support 75hz at least, I have no idea since I don't have it. Hope this review will help you in your journey to find a good monitor.

Based on the fact that there aren't a lot of USB-powered choices, this monitor is okay.
Pros
- light
- thin
- display isn't too bad
- somewhat high resolution (vertically challenged in landscape mode, but so are many newer laptops - they're wide but not high pixel wise)
- great visibility from the sides (which is great for presentations)
- very low power (my Tecra has 3 USB2 ports using the same 'bus' (or whatever it's called) on one side, and 1 combined USB/eSata port on the other). I typically use the 3port side with: 1) USB hd, 2) USB hub w/Ethernet and webcam connected, 3) The MB169B+. The only time I run into a power issue is if I turn up the brightness all the way, which obviously draws more power. Then I overload this side of the laptop (which is expected with all the other items plugged in). So as far as power goes - this truly does consume little power - and I'm running with USB2 not USB3 ports.

Cons
- Many colors are washed out
- Can't resolve the color issue since my NVIDIA Control Panel is locked out once the ASUS software/drivers load. Contacted ASUS support 2x (first time they recommended to replace the monitor which didn't make any sense, but Amazon was kind enough to do this; didn't resolve the issue). Basically, ASUS Tech Support couldn't resolve this issue which is major for me due to many colors washed out - light greys/blues/etc. all display as white, so although the monitor is readable, it doesn't look like 'normal' laptop/desktop monitors.
- Case is thin/light but not rigid/hard enough to protect the monitor during transit. Case should be better designed for a few reasons.

So overall - in light of what else is out there - this isn't bad. But definitely much room for improvement - especially with the colors displayed, incompatibility with Graphics Card Software, and the case. I'd give it a 4 overall, but a 0 due to the 'cons' issues, hence the 2 rating.

The review from the Opinionated Realist covers generally my experience.

Color accuracy is HORRIBLE. I suspect the people that are raving about the "color" accuracy aren't people that have any need for accurate (or even reasonably close to accurate color), but are somehow commenting that "greens are really green," etc. I've also spent about an hour TRYING to get the color reasonably close and it just doesn't feel possible. Thank God Apple has advanced color calibration built-in. I got it close enough that as long as I'm not going to use it for anything professional, it'll suffice.

With that... understand these are issues I think ever user will face

1. VERY uneven grayscale ramp... Lower areas in the greyscale ramp are blown out
2. Color temperature is way off... the MacOS color calibration (you'll need to used the Expert mode to adjust this) helped to some degree here.
3. HDMI inputs are not the same and I can find no reference in the user "manual" or on Samsungs website. HDMI 1 is 4K at 30Hz and HDMI 2 is 60Hz. Use the wrong one and you'll get snow. The only way I figured this out was from a user review as the first time I tried HDMI 1 it didn't work at all even though that turned out to be the only HDMI port I can use with my adaptor and it finally came on. To be fair and clear, now that I finally got it to work on HDMI 1, I haven't had any trouble keeping it working on HDMI 1... otherwise it'd be on it's way back.
4. The stand is very flimsy and any bumping of the desk makes it shake and the plastic squeak. It's probably not going to break, but it underscores why it's so inexpensive.
5. There is little adjustment to this display physically. You can swivel the screen up and down. Any left and right swivel involves rotating the whole stand. There is no height adjustment. For the price of this display I'm not overly upset about that.
6. Display menu is also very low res and basic. Not sure why Samsung of all companies has an on-screen menu that looks like it came from the 70's on a 4K(!) display.

Continually disappointed in Samsung. This is why I'll never buy a cell phone from them...

Apple Mac air 2020 users: to add my voice to the several others who have made this warning: do not use the cables that are shipped with this monitor. Buy another, right along with the monitor.

What worked for me, advised by another reviewer, is a cable made by Pluggable. Look for the description at top of the listing:

“ Plugable USB-C to DisplayPort Adapter Cable (6'/1.8m) for 2016 / 2017 MacBook Pro, MacBook Retina 2015 / 2016, Chromebook Pixel, Thunderbolt 3 & More (Supports 4K / UHD Displays up to 3840x2160@60Hz).”

Reason: as you know, if you have a Mac Air 2020 or will find out if you are newly buying this fabulous laptop, is that there are only two USB-C ports. There are no other USB or other connectors, save for an audio out. So first, you will need a cable that, like the above, uses USB-C to the Mac.

Second, you will want that replacement connector to run to the full-size DisplayPort (not mini display port) connection into the monitor. HDMI is also possible but not ideal. That’s because, although HDMI to the monitor will work, it will only support high resolutions at 30 Hz. You want to use the Mac Air 2020 capability to drive 60 Hz, whi how is much better on the eyes, and a richer image.

A second caution: if you were buying this monitor, then you are using it as an external monitor and would be well advised to purchase a USB-C hub, like the Kingston. This hub contains a USB see power pass-through, and additionally a second USB-C, which you could use for the Dell video. My first USB see hub was either factory-defect, or did not have enough power to support all those connections. Do your research.

All those warnings made, the picture quality of this Dell at 4K 60 Hz, is gorgeous, immensely satisfying, especially for photographic image work. Go to Apple support and learn how to change resolution to optimize this. But right out of the box, you will be blown away by the image.

I spent a LOT of time and stress doing comparison shopping between different brands and manufacturers. There's dozens on Amazon and there are a lot of really nice screens - if you get one that's solid, out of the box.

I went with this Dell because it has great picture quality once calibrated, and it was covered by both Amazon's support and return policy plus Dell's warranty. I stressed like crazy waiting for it to arrive, and it went on a little bit of a shipping adventure (thanks, UPS) so I was about as worried as you could get that it would have problems when I got it out of the box - and these ultrawides can be so delicate, if you lift them out of the box wrong, you can break them.

It was perfect. It's beautiful and I'm loving every second of it. This monitor was worth every penny for me. I'm sure extreme hardcore twitch gamers are going to have some kind of issue somewhere with some minuscule detail about sync rates or ghosting, but it looks gorgeous to me and I'm a digital artist - I've seen lots of screens, including nice ones and bad ones, and this one is really nice.

Don't feel like you have to hold your breath buying this one. Can you get a UWQHD screen for cheaper from another manufacturer? Yes. Is there a chance it will be a really good monitor? Yes. But the chances of those things going wrong, and having a nightmare getting things straightened out, are much higher with these ultrawides. This is a great quality monitor for a decent price that comes with peace of mind about all of that.

This is a review for the AOC 27'' I2757FH Monitor, originally published 1/21/2016. I purchased mine from Best Buy, because it had a better price than Amazon.

I've had mine two days now, (so no major problems in this review, check back soon), and so far, I love it. It's my first real monitor, so I may be a little biased.

UPDATE, 6/7/2016:
Still in love. Still the best monitor I could ask for. Still blown away by how huge it is. Still my monitor recommendation. Have had no issues with it at all, the touch-sensitive controls still operate perfectly.

THE SHORT OF IT:
Fantastic monitor, for a good price if you know where to look for it. Good for color-sensitive, and for gaming.

Pros:
-Huge screen.
-Stand is very sturdy.
-Colors are very good, at least to my untrained eye.
-No ghosting, input lag, or other detriments to high-speed gaming.
-Looks awesome.
-Cool touch-sensitive controls.
-Matte screen.
-To me, I felt I was not overcharged. I paid ~$160 at Best Buy.

Cons:
-Crappy speakers.
-Crappy plastic on back, and under the screen.
-Terrible ergonomic adjustment abilities, nearly non-existent tilting.

THE LONG:
First of all, this thing is massive. Previously, I was working on a 22'' TV screen, and I used to think I was some sort of screen-real-estate mogul; but pulling this thing out of the box and seeing just how many inches 27 is, I was really excited. Secondly, I absolutely love the look of this thing. When I purchased it, I was pretty much indifferent, but seeing it set up on my desk, it looks really nice.

THE SCREEN:
The screen itself is fantastic, especially the viewing angles. You can stare at this thing from just about any direction, and there'll only be slight discoloration, enough that it's hardly noticeable, and I'm not sure why you'd want to see it from those angles anyway.

REASONING:
I was in the market for a monitor that would function well for both color-sensitive work, (photography, video-editing), and gaming.

COLORS:
Right out of the box, the colors were pretty much accurate. I'm not wildly experienced in calibrating monitors, but I tried my hand at it. It took minor tweaking from its factory settings to get it to where I felt it was right, and where Window's built in calibration tool recommended it be. The matte screen is really nice, and so far has no issues with glares or reflections, and seems to have no negative effect on the colors.

GAMING:
I've spent about an hour gaming on it so far, and the size and matte screen really add to it. As for ghosting, or any sort of impedance to your gaming experience, I have had no issue with it. I was able to play fast-paced shooting games, without any trouble with input lag or missing important details.

OSD CONTROLS:
People in other reviews have complained about the touch-sensitive menu buttons found on the front of the monitor. Personally, I have had no trouble with them so far, and it feels pretty cool to simply press my thumb to something and make it spring to life. Also, the OSD menu controls function far more intuitively than any other monitor I've had to change settings on before. The buttons are responsive, and the menu is easy to understand and alter settings in. So, as long as the touch buttons continue to work properly, they are a plus to me.

BUILD QUALITY:
In terms of the build quality, I'm not mad, just disappointed. The plastic casing feels cheap. Really cheap. It feels weaker structurally than the Styrofoam the monitor was packaged in. It feels as if with minimal pressure you could punch a hole through it. And despite the brushed metal look to it, the bar along the bottom of the screen is also a cheapy-feeling plastic, too. This really shouldn't be an issue, though, unless your monitor tends to be moved around. Sitting still in the same spot on a desk, there's no reason it should be a problem.

THE STAND:
That said, the stand for this monitor is incredible; it comes in two parts, and both feel incredibly sturdy. The base has foam grips that make this thing impossible to move without lifting it first. You can also use the monitor without the stand, simply resting it on the hook used to attach it to the stand. In spite of the great stand, the ergonomic adjustment available from the stand is incredibly minimal. It will rotate about 15° up or down.

SPEAKERS:
This monitor does come with speakers, but they're garbage. I don't use speakers often, opting instead for headphones, but on occasion when I want to share a video with someone, it's nice to not have to share headphones or connect speakers; but with the sound that comes from this thing, you're better off doing that. It's incredibly quiet, and massively tinny.

CONCLUSION:
This thing is great; great for gaming; great for color-sensitive work. If you don't touch it for too long, and just look, this thing is a pleasure to work on. The size and design of it is really eye-catching, and will improve the look of any computer setup.

This review is for the BLI-24000, which was discontinued about 2008, to be replaced by the BLI-28000.

I had the display unit in my kitchen, and it was very informative to see my energy usage basically in real-time. I discovered that the central heater in my new home was not an energy efficient design and was using about three times as much as newer designs would. I was shocked at what a hog the heater was, using over 14kw when active. If the heater needs replacing then I will be sure to get the one with the a/c compressor unit that is a reverse cycle heat pump, instead of the coil heater I apparently have now.

This product worked well, as long as it didn't rain too heavily. And once the rains came then the outdoor transmitter stopped working for a few days until it dried out. It also often stopped working if it was exposed to direct sunlight, perhaps due to the sunlight interfering with its electric eye that reads the spinning disk.

After one particularly long bout of rain, the transmitter never worked again. I eventually opened the casing and I could see that the electronics inside were water damaged. When I look on the Blue Line website, I see that the newer version of this product has been improved, in order to correct the design defect that allowed water to collect inside the electronics.

Its rare for electronic devices to fail after only a couple years of use, as most such devices seem to last at least a decade or three. And for the high price I had paid for my unit I decided to see if I could get the transmitter's electronics replaced.

After contacting Blue Line I discovered to my surprise that they do not stock any replacement parts for their product, and they could not help me in any way with getting my unit repaired. Apparently their plan for maintaining their product line is for you to throw it away when it dies. This is especially irritating, given that it died due to a known design defect. And for a product that originally cost over $100 dollars, and one that is only a few years old, how can they not plan to provide service for it? This shows that making a good product is more than just a glossy design and a friendly voice on the phone. A truly good company makes sure they maintain their products through a reasonable life cycle.

I've been a dual monitor user at home and work for 20+ years, so the times when I'm travelling and stuck with just my laptop's display, I'm definitely a little slower or less productive. I've been interested in portable monitors for a while, but they were always too small, too thick, or the resolution was too low. This Svantto monitor solves all those issues, and is a complete package with everything you need.

After checking out all the goodies that come in the box, I went the minimalist route and connected my Samsung Galaxy S20 to the monitor with just one of the included USB C cables. Even though I was inside and the room well lit, the brightness level of 30 was perfect, and there was no need to plug in the monitor's power supply cable. Many years ago, I bought my very first IBM-style PC from a company called Packard Bell. I figured that, since PB's were sold pretty much everywhere, it was one of the safest buys I could make. This turned out to be a fatal error, as the company provided horrendous support... a failed device was not met with support, but rather "well, I can direct you to sales and you can buy a new system!" And the failure? Caused by the on-board battery being inside of a sealed, and soldered-on, module. Today, I could replace that with an external battery, but at the time, I was significantly less skilled, and the system ended up in the trash, with me learning how to build my own from that point onwards.

So... seeing this monitor in my queue for items I could review for the Vine program, my first reaction was "Packard Bell still exists?" But... knowing that this could not be the same company (rather, a new company who own the brand name of the former, long-gone company)... I decided to give it a try.

And I LOVE this monitor. It is perhaps the nicest desktop monitor I've ever owned. Yes, I'm sure that there are better ones, at price points far, far above this one, but this is a TERRIFIC monitor.

I had a bit of an issue when I first got it, as I always like to go to the vendor site and collect as much information about an item as possible. The site was very hard to find, and just as hard to navigate. Eventually, I found what I was looking for, but it was "non-trivial" to accomplish that. This was the ONLY thing I criticized about the company when I first wrote my initial review.

Oddly, the item seems to have disappeared from Amazon for a brief while... a couple of months or so. And now, it's back, and even that issue seems to have been addressed. Unfortunately, my original review disappeared, and as I'm obliged, as part of my role as Vine reviewer, to provide fair, unbiased, but complete reviews of every item I receive (once I have enough information to do so!) I am resubmitting a new review.

So, about this monitor. The picture quality is remarkably nice, first off. Sharp, without any ghosting or other artifacts I can perceive. The very high refresh rates, even at high resolutions, and the very black blacks and bright brights combine to give excellent imagery. While the color reproduction isn't as "calibrated" as a professional graphics monitor I also happen to own, I can manually adjust it to be nearly as good as that professional monitor... limited only by my ability to dial things in myself.

The monitor comes with built in speakers which are more than adequate for most routine use, though I have a very nice 5.1 surround sound system in my office, complete with a huge subwoofer, so I'm not typically going to use those built-in speakers. But if you need to... they're more than adequate for the job. Not "high fi" per-se, but not the weak, tinny-sounding speakers so many monitors come with.

Configurability is easy and largely intuitive.

My only real "dislike" with this ... and this is TRIVIAL... is the wasted cost of putting a colored light ring on the reverse of the monitor. I simply turn it off. It's not something I find to be valuable. But I know that the trend, these days, is for EVERYTHING to be lit, including mouse pads, so I can't really ding it over this. They're giving the average customer what they want. And I'm glad I can turn it off easily!

I wish it was... as-shipped... compatible with 3D shutter glasses. I can make it work with mine, but it isn't recognized as compatible, so some tweaking is required. Then again, since nVidia pulled the rug out from under that market, I'm one of only a few people still using this technology, and yes, I HAVE been able to get it to work (really, the only think you need is the ability to sync the glasses to the video card output, and a monitor with a fast refresh rate, which this monitor certainly has!)

I got another monitor here, a while back, which was pretty nice, but there were a few issues... the monitor speaker situation was sub-par, and the stand, while visually interesting, was more than a little inconvenient to use. This one? The stand is great... including having that "portrait or landscape" functionality built-in, and very easy to use.

Honestly, The Hardline Group (the actual maker of this monitor, who are selling this under the Packard Bell brand name) have done an outstanding job on this, and I'm entirely happy with my monitor. I can recommend it without any reservations. If you need a monitor of this size... this is my first recommendation at present!

The good: extremely light, good picture. Touch works very well. Love the fold-out stand, which worked perfectly for me - I wanted this to serve as a temporary second monitor when I play Flight Simulator (so I can display my virtual aircraft's control panel next to me and actually change my instruments and switches by physically touching them - works terrific, and although I have to set my monitors up so that Windows thinks my secondary monitor is the primary each time, that's a Microsoft thing and not in any way this monitor's fault) but when I'm not playing, I fold in the stand and return the monitor to my VESA-compatible wall mount that I use to broadcast Netflix to through my Netgear Push2TV. One of the smallest monitors out there that's true 1080P.

The bad: A touch small for the wall application, but that's a matter of taste. You've also read other reviews about how tinny the speakers are (true) and at least one reviewer recommending that you plug an external set of speakers into the monitor. That reviewer should have actually tried that before making that recommendation, because unfortunately it is quite impossible: the 1/8" audio jack on the back of the monitor is a stereo INPUT not an OUTPUT. There is no way to hook a set of speakers up to this monitor, and it needs it pretty badly. Audio in is far less important since it already receives audio through the HDMI connection (which also carries audio) and fewer and fewer users are going to need a separate audio in, but the poor speakers demand an audio out for any serious multimedia application. Lacks scaling options, which are a standard feature on just about every other HDMI monitor, but which is becoming less important as "underscanning/overscaling" becomes a forgotten technology so probably not a concern.

The neutral: glossy screen sure to show fingerprints, but that's true of all touchscreens. Powered by a small power brick instead of a cord directly in; I prefer the simplicity of a single cord but I took a couple of sticky tabs and just glued the little brick to the back of the monitor, so for all practical purposes it's the same as having a cord plugging directly in.

It isn't the design of this LCD that earns merit, although it is a rather timeless looking item and will look smart in any situation. It's the feature set, and there is certainly far more function over form than meets the casual eye!

Pros:

- Can be set to auto detect the exact input source resolution and display the image without any stretching. This is great if you play games that might need 1280 x 1024 or 1680 x 1050. Your treasured vintage game collection will still look splendid!

- Has a great software tool that will set the correct color / contrast / brightness balance. Make sure you run MagicBright after installation - the image is stellar once you have this set.

- Low power consumption - do your bit for Gaia ;-)

- The memory resident tools do not task the CPU.

- Tack sharp display with almost non-existant light bleed at the edges

- Games look superb!

- No dead pixels

- Comfortable viewing size - I find larger displays somewhat taxing on the eyes

- Has a great tool that can divide your display into various logical units - a bit mike having multiple displays - very cool as this makes apps snap into the regions just like extra displays

- Amazing price - 2ms + 20,000:1 contrast ratio + Samsung quality + auto resolution detect + much more!

Cons:

- No height adjustment - but by no means a show-stopper when you bear in mind the spec.

- Tilt only tilts away, not towards you. The assumption is that you will be looking down at the screen up to horizontal, which is ergonomically incorrect. The user should have the option to view at 90 degrees or above to avoid neck strain. I resolved this by buying a sturdy stand so I can have the mid-screen point at eye level ;-)

- No frame color options - just translucent burgundy, which looks blackish most of the time in low light conditions.

- Software installation is confusing. It recommends that you download the latest software. When you click the link, it's very unclear what you need to do. I couldn't see a option for the T220, so I just installed what was on the disk and everything ran fine. You must run the software installer using Internet Explorer, otherwise you need to manually run the install exe from the CD.

That being said - audio was not why I purchased this monitor - a brilliant backlit IPS 1920x1080 display was what I was after - and boy did the 27xw deliver! As it comes out of the box, the screen color temp is very cold and needs to be reduced significantly to get decent reds showing on the screen. This is similar to other contemporary Dell monitors. I've spent about 30mins-1hour trying to tweak this in monitor and GPU settings and it is getting better but still not yet close to the color on my 10+ year old Samsung 24" CCFL LCD that this is supposed to take over from. I'll probably be able to dial this in more, just a buyer beware that you might be spending a lot of time in the menus of this monitor vs some that just look stunning out of the box that you don't really have to mess with. Also maybe have a friend with a colorimeter to use to help dial it in, I wish I did.

TL;DR: Potentially great monitor you have to pay too much for and fuss over a lot if you want to make it look great instead of just good. If you are fine with just good, you could probably save a lot of money, like the difference between 5600XT and 5700XT money, by choosing a different 27" 1440/144 monitor.

First off, I realize that it can be hit/miss with getting a display that doesn't have issues with dead pixels, backlight bleeding, etc. I also submitted this review after 1.5 months of continuous use to see if any issues would crop up early on.

I've been a long time user of the old style Apple Cinema Displays (matte finish IPS panels) so I was looking for a somewhat decent display with a lot more articulation for my ASUS ROG G20CB. As others have mentioned, 4K gaming is pretty much non-existent (lack of UI scaling is normally the problem) so that part was not a high priority.

Which is why I settled on trying this particular model. While it's max resolution is 3840x2160, I knew I was going to just run it at 2560x1440 and thus chose to go with a 24" display than the larger 27" units. FreeSync also didn't matter to me (my system has an NVDIA GTX 980). Upon arrival and opening up the external shipping box, I did have a packaging issue that was quickly addressed by Amazon Customer Support. The product itself was fine but I was still expecting some sort of quality issue with the display itself once hooked up.

Much to my surprise, this IPS panel didn't have any dead/stuck pixels. The display also didn't have any backlight bleeding and only has a minor IPS glow that I offset by lowering the brightness. The display is sharp and gorgeous at 3840x2160 resolution (using the 60Hz DisplayPort connection; I also tested this via the HDMI 1.4 port to make sure everything was working) but as expected, UI scaling was an issue in many games. To avoid the whole problem with resolution switching with some games (while running Windows at max res), I just run the entire setup at 2560x1440. This was still a major improvement over the old 20" Apple Cinema Display that I had been temporarily using (1680x1050 resolution).

The rest of the display feels robust (no squeaky or flimsy feeling plastic) and I am very satisfied with the amount of articulation when it comes to pivot, tilt, and height adjustment.

Mine shipped with firmware v1011, the latest as of August 2021. Reviews and information about the monitor suggested that in some instances, earlier firmware may actually perform better in some contexts, so I was a bit nervous about this at first.

Here's the facts.

Yes, the monitor has "scanlines" visible during certain test patterns. You've probably seen the links to lagom or a specific page on ASUS's website. This is an issue with pixel inversion, and to some degree, every LCD based monitor is going to have a test pattern of some kind that will result in a visible, unwelcome change to the displayed picture. This problem doesn't show up in real content. It is completely pointless to be upset about these artifacts, unless a significant sum of your time is spent staring at lagom or that one ASUS page specifically.

What people are talking about much less frequently is that in addition to the "scanlines" you can see in these specific test patterns, there is a lesser "scanline" artifact that can crop up when displaying specific colors, especially larger sections of them. This can become noticeable to an artist that is working on a still image, but is scarcely noticeable in real content. THIS is an artifact of the panel being pushed to the absolute limits of what it's physically capable of. Artifacts like these tend to show themselves when a lot of power is being pushed through the panel - anyone that has used a Valve Index has probably noticed similar "scanlines" in certain situations. Again, hardly noticeable in real content, but I can't say it's entirely invisible like the pixel inversion issue described above.

With adaptive sync and VRR control on, the pixel response time is fantastic across the entire refresh rate range, and there are no visible sudden shifts in gamma, or "screen flashing" when the framerate changes. To those extremely sensitive to judder, it's been stated that this makes VRR "not as smooth feeling" in a way that nobody can seemingly articulate. I _believe_ what is happening here is that a technique is being used to internally double or triple the refresh rate of the input to the highest possible value while staying under 240hz. This can result in a delay if a new frame is ready to be displayed before the doubled or tripled image is finished being scanned out to the panel. In theory, this can have a maximum judder penalty of 4.166~ms (1 second divided by the refresh rate, 240hz), but in practice, we can assume it's going to be an average of half of that, 2.083~, since there's no way to predict if the new frame is ready near the beginning or end of the scanout for the doubled/tripled refresh. I would say that the judder that VRR control can cause is real, but as someone that isn't particularly sensitive to it, it doesn't bother me. It often melts away and becomes imperceptible amongst other performance issues a game may have that result in engine halts that are longer than the average potential judder period with VRR control on.

You can't adjust the pixel overdrive setting with adaptive sync ON and VRR control ON, however, it's tuned well so that low refresh rates appear as smooth as they can, without high levels of overshoot or ghosting. Whether they accomplished this with an actual variable overdrive engine (like you can see as standard on any monitor that has a Gsync module) or through internal conversions of the input refresh to the monitor's maximum refresh, i'm not sure. Looks great either way.

The curve can be weird at times, but disappears in most real content. Notable exceptions to this may be side scrolling or 2d games, where the rigid, straight lines are more easily noticed as being bowed out. I would personally prefer the curve not be there, but it's a small penalty to pay for how good everything else about the monitor is.

Overall: Top tier monitor for gamers (provided you don't care about backlight strobing modes, in which case, the included function for it on this monitor is garbage and shouldn't be used), but if your primary use case is to do professional image editing, or play 2D games, you may find that certain elements of the panel performance and curve are unsuitable or take too long to get used to in order to feel good about the purchase. For everyone else, it's fine and I considered this a definitive upgrade over prior monitors I've purchased (Viewsonic XG2431, Viewsonic XG270, ASUS PG279Q) for non-strobed gameplay. The added contrast of VA matched with a pixel response time performance that is overall faster than today's fastest IPS panels is fantastic to look at.

Model purchased: Samsung 27" G7. Don't forget to filter your reviews to the specific model you're wanting information about so you can get a clearer picture of the exact item you're considering, as the reviews on the store page cover every selectable option, including other sizes or models that use different panels entirely.

I do have a history with this brand, so I would know. I originally purchased this due to another Sceptre monitor going out on me for my dual monitor setup. Package was not damaged at all and delivery driver handled with care (I saw from my Ring cam footage). Once pulled out of the box to test it, I immediately was disappointed to learn it was inoperable. Panel was defective. Some dead pixels. Vertical lines only across the screen. My computer picked up the display just fine when checking for correct input setup and I could cross my mouse cursor into where my desktop should have extended. I don’t have a graphics card issue and other displays worked well. But it was dead on arrival. I do understand that sometimes there can be manufacturer defects which is why I gave this one a shot. I bought 3 Sceptre monitors! Unfortunately, I had to return this and get a refund. I’d rank this for 3 stars, but here’s why I’m lowering my rating.

After 15 months of use, my flat panel 24” Sceptre monitor started having issues and dancing pixels. It became more frequent on a daily basis (multiple times a day) and the screen would flicker in and out seconds at a time, especially on virtual work meetings. That’s what prompted me to purchase a replacement in the first place. I bought this curved version to match the other curved Sceptre monitor I had so I could make my viewing angles a bit nicer while working.

I plan to do a complete overhaul of my monitor setup and eventually replace my working Sceptre one currently in use because I have no idea when this one will go out on me. 2 out of 3 went bad for me. Although it was initially a decent buy for the price of a basic budget monitor, I can no longer expect longevity of life from products of this manufacturer. If you purchase this, take caution and note that you risk investing in a product that might not last as long as you’d hope for. You do get what you pay for and this is no exception. I’ll be replacing them with something I can count on with better quality and probably from a more well-known brand. I do not recommend this product and would suggest to do some research while considering this one.

This monitor is a bit of a disappointment for the price class, with an image that is markedly dimmer and more prone to both black crush and highlight blowout than the NEC 3090 I bought it to replace, and it has a number of usability and driver issues. On the plus side, physical build quality is outstanding, and it does have more uniform brightness at the edges than the NEC.

Back to the negatives, the usability problems include:
1) it is very slow to wake up

2) the touch screen drivers further delay every wake from sleep with an additional 8 to 10 seconds of "Calibrating Touch Screen, Do Not Touch" during which time the entire screen is grayed out.

3) does not auto switch inputs, which matters for me since I have both a Mac and a PC connected to it.

4) it does not play well with Windows because it appears to be two separate monitors, leaving you with a desktop bottom bar that cuts off half way across screen, and I have yet to find a game that can successfully switch to full screen mode in 60hz mode (MSI R7970 Lightning with latest drivers).

5) It does not play well with Mac, causing the Mac to fail to wake from sleep about 20% of the time, forcing a hard power reset. This is a 2014 Mac Pro with Dual D700s and has never had a problem waking from sleep with prior monitors. It also will not work in 60hz mode. To be fair, this monitor is not listed as supported by Apple, but I was hoping it might work since its non-touchscreen sibling PN-K321 is supported.

6) Only one Displayport input. This means that if you are using multiple sources with the monitor, only one of them can operate at 60hz refresh.

UPDATE 7/25/2014: After switching to an Nvidia based graphics card, the Windows issues cleared up: desktop displays properly, and full screen mode works for games.

I write this review immediately after having unboxed and setup the monitor, I connected it to my laptop, a Predator Helios 300.

It comes in very nice packaging, and the device is very thin when folded up in the included protective sleeve/foldable display. Magnets built into the backside of the monitor link up with corresponding magnets built into the sleeve, which is made up of a sleek synthetic material. When the display is set up in the normal/correct configuration, the backside has a bit more texture to it, which likely servers to act as a grip against whichever surface it is standing on.

The packaging included all of the cables I needed to get set up right out of the box - the only cable it does not come with is an AUX for connecting audio devices or a pair of headphones. Though, I myself have no need for either, adding something like that along with the rest of the included cables would make this a 100% complete package.

Overall, the device itself is very nice - my only true complaint is that there isn't a smart, out-of-the-box solution to using the device in portrait format, as turning it vertically on its side would be a bad idea; the left side has two analogue switches - power and volume/selection control - the latter of which is not only a button, but also an up/down lever/scroll that protrudes a bit too far out to smartly accommodate standing the device up on that end. The integration of touch-sensitive or depressed buttons might have made the left-hand side viable for portrait-mode. Meanwhile, the right-hand side will be occupied by one or all of the cables that you'll need to power and connect to the screen.

This apparently is a suitable, versatile, and "smart" universal recharger. There are ones that perform functions better than this does, but this was an inexpensive option for most of my needed/wanted things.

I haven't tested its capability to recharge alkaline or Ni-Cad batteries, but it seems to recharge the Ni-MH ones of all sizes well (though one D-cell leaked in there, another it saw as bad). While I haven't tested the two USB charging ports beyond attaching my cell phone to the 1-amp port just now, they will reduce the outlet demand if you must recharge multiple items simultaneously. While this thing does give a rough indication of how fully charged the battery is (I have no way of verifying whether its determinations are accurate), it doesn't give the rating in mAh, but rather segments of a battery graphic which represent 20 or 25% blocks. While that very much disappoints me, it does tell me approximately how heavily I had drained the batteries, and it does seem to charge batteries rapidly.

So, if you want an inexpensive universal charger, especially one that can charge electronics simultaneously, I'd recommend this. If you only need to charge AAA and AA batteries, you're better off with something else.

I purchased this monitor as a replacement for an older HP LP2475W. I repackaged the U2415 monitor to return it the same day it arrived. The monitor design is nice and it is fairly light. The buttons on the monitor to navigate the menu are terribly inconsistent. I could not figure out the right position to press the buttons in the limited time I had the monitor. From what I could tell the monitor didn't have any stuck pixels and generally appeared to work well. I appreciated the button customization so I could easily switch inputs. But in the end the monitor had to be returned... and quickly!

The main issue: I am hyper sensitive to different monitor technologies. I thought it was IPS vs. whatever. I thought it was a resolution issue. It appears to be a CCFL vs. WLED issue. Almost all LCDs of old were CCFL and I had no problem looking at them for hours (my job requires it!). Within 10 minutes of plugging the U2415 in I was already on my way to a nasty headache. Things progressed to the point that after looking at the monitor for a few minutes I was dizzy as I stepped away. Let's just say my afternoon was ruined and I was happy to box up the monitor and switch back on the HP.

Full disclosure: This is not my first attempt at replacing my HP LP2475W monitor. I have purchased 3 other brands/models and the new WLED backlight technology seems to be the issue. This is now the standard for just about everything. I am stuck until I can figure out what monitor I will be able to tolerate.

I have bought sceptre monitors in the past, and I stand by them as a great budget monitor company if you want something that's reliable and inexpensive. I purchased these monitors as secondary monitors and vertical monitors for my setup. From my experience, I knew that these monitors would be good for work other than gaming, so I primarily use them for watching videos, discord, or just as an extra screen for when I am doing work. I overall enjoy these monitors, but I have two major issues that I had first when I got them.

I love the monitors, and they could be a 5-star product if they tweaked a couple of things. I will talk first about the main advantages of the monitors first. Firstly, for $150, you are getting a monitor with decent viewing angles and a good size with 75hz refresh rate viewing. For the price, it is a very good monitor alone for office or schoolwork. It does its job very well and anybody who is doing basic work or maybe some console gaming would have a good experience with these monitors. Furthermore, they are VESA mount* (more on that below), and it has a good display, with a speaker that although quiet, does the job well without sounding blown out. Overall, it is a good monitor, and anybody who wants something basic will enjoy this monitor.

Here are the cons from my experience, as it had a couple off the start. I first opened the box to find that it talks about its return policy on the box. This was extremely concerning since it is almost impossible to send this monitor back. Please keep everything (including box and packaging materials) for the first month you own the monitor. The second issue I had was the mounting brackets you get with the monitor. For one thing, the monitor uses a separate monitor arm, that is screwed into a bracket that comes out of the monitor. If you plan on not using any desk mounts or VESA mounting, then you will have no problem with this monitor. However, I wanted to mount this monitor to a VESA desk mount to use as a vertical monitor. My first issue came when I tried to mount the monitor, when I tried to mount the monitor to the arm by screwing in the screws provided with the arm. The first issue comes with the hardware included with the monitor, there are four standoffs and two monitor screws, but the arm needed four screws, so we already are set back. I found some old monitor screws that fit, and when I mounted the monitor, I realized that the monitor wouldn't fit due to the bracket secured on the back, which sticks out of the monitor to make more viewing angles. Again, this won't be an issue for those who are planning on not using any VESA mounting, however, it makes the process a lot more difficult for those who are planning on doing any mounting of their monitor with a third party device. It took me like 20 minutes before I realized that the standoffs that are included are for VESA mounting, which although makes some sense, also makes me question why to include the mounting bracket externally when it could have been included inside of the monitor since there is a cutout section that fits it.

I see this question a lot, and I feel that I should address it here for those who want to game on this monitor: get a different monitor. First, this monitor is a 75hz 5ms, meaning it refreshes the screen 75 times in one second, and it responds 5ms after the information has been sent by the PC. There are plenty of 144hz monitors with a 1ms response time for this price, and I recommend that you look for something at that price. If you want something to game on for the long run, get a 144hz monitor at the same price, it'll work better overall, and be around the same budget.

Overall I highly enjoy this monitor. I give it four stars since it works very well for what I need it to do, and although it has its flaws I wouldn't want another monitor. It could have been five stars, only if the price was a little bit cheaper. The price does go up with the blue light shift that occurs, but the $133 option is still a little overpriced, I would personally price this around $115 and it would be the ultimate monitor for the price. Overall, if you want a monitor for office work, then please consider this, it's a great monitor.

Update: 6 months later

My opinion below still stands. I had a brief moment where the monitor appeared to have burn-in, but it resolved itself in 48 hours. Customer support was willing to replace/repair it, but since it's resolved, I didn't pursue a repair. Despite the negatives listed below, I would still recommend this monitor if, 1) You want an ultrawidescreen monitor with a higher resolution than 1440p vertical, 2) You want accurate colors and/or want an immersive gaming experience, and 3) you can overlook the low contrast ratio.

Last thing: I don't know if it's a Mac thing, but HDR and local dimming on this is terrible. Bad. Don't use it. You won't miss it on this display though.

Original review:
Resolution, size, and brightness are fantastic for office productivity, as well as that great 21:9 aspect ratio for the videos that take advantage of it. The 144 refresh rate is fantastic For an ultra widescreen. The downside is that many streaming services will not recognize the aspect ratio correctly, and give black bars on both vertical and horizontal axises.

Additionally, the contrast on this is garbage. If you enjoy watching dark movies or video games that are dark, the monitor goes from "somewhat dark" to "black". There is no in-between. If you want to watch something like Game of Thrones' "long night" episode, you might as well be blind. When the content is bright and colorful however, it's fantastic.

Speakers are junk and only functional if you have no other audio output device. Also, price should be ~$1,000 to $1,200. The original price of $1,600 is too high.

Really nice entry level 4k monitor. I was a bit hesitant to buy this one because of the lack of reviews around the web. Decided the price was worth it and might as well give it a try. Turned out to be a great purchase, not perfect but still really great. I've been using it for doing art and animation, and of course 4k gaming. It's color isn't as nice as a 90%+ Adobe RGB, but LG's S-IPS panels still have great color, a step above most monitors, especially around this price point. Gaming is a dream on this thing, so sharp and crisp, truly glorious. Here are some Pro's and Con's:

Pros:
-4k... duh
-10 bit color (8+A-FRC)
-S-IPS panel (great viewing angles)
- 99%+ sRGB (measured as NTSC 72%) gamut.
- Freesync (only works with AMD cards as of now)
- 5ms on an IPS panel
- 60hz
- Black stabilizer setting, can bring out the details in certain dark images.
- HDCP 2.2

Cons:
- Only 3 inputs, 2 hdmi and 1 display-port (would have liked 1 more display-port)
- Has an anti-glare coating (it's not very aggressive like some I've seen, but still can cause slight distortion when viewed from above/below at extreme angles)
- At250 cd/m2 it's not on the high end of peak brightness, but not bad either.

Update 1: I thought it might be handy to add a little section on setting up the PS4 Pro with this monitor. I took me awhile to realize how to get it working to display at 4k. You need to have a newer hdmi cable (2.0, the monitor comes with a short one) and you need to enable the setting for HMDI UHD deep color, this enables RGB 4:4:4 chroma signals to pass over HMDI so it can talk to the PS4 Pro. This setting is under Menu>Picture>Picture Adjust>HDMI ULTRA HD Deep Color. This setting is common among LG 4k devices, so if you have an issue on another LG product w/ ps4 Pro, make sure it's enabled. You may also need to change RGB settings on the PS4 Pro to "Full".

Update 2: I wanted to update with the settings I found best for my panel, in terms of color and brightness, etc. May not be universal, but could give a ballpark of what you may want.

Brightness: 69
Contrast:67
Black Stabilizer: 35

RGB:
Red: 55
Green: 53
Blue: 53

Six Color:
RedH: 50
RedSat:61
GreenH:55
GreenSat:61
BlueH:62
BlueSat:62
YellH:51
YellSat:55
MagH:52
MagSat:62
CyanH:50
CyanSat:58

I do love it, but setting things up for 60Hz refresh requires a bit of help. I ordered this and the Nvidia GTX 980. As of writing this (Nov 22nd 2014) there is a major issue hitting a lot of people where the system will not cold boot if using a single monitor and the DisplayPort cable. Hangs after loading BIOS. This is NOT the monitor or supplied cable, it's the actual graphics driver. I have a dual monitor setup and I discovered that providing I use a DVI-I cable to the second monitor the bootup will take place on Monitor 2 and as soon as the Password page occurs it will swap over (auto) to the Main ASUS monitor.

The monitor itself is crystal clear, excellent color reproduction and a near perfect viewing area. I do use the ColorMunky to ensure that color reproduction on the screen and printer match, but the monitor comes with a "lot" of choices for setting up you color preferences. Warning. First time in from a lower resolution monitor the icons that litter your screen will be really small, you can adjust the size easily.

BUT the monitor come factory set using DisplayPort 1.1 (32 Hz only) and you must change this to 1.2 for 60Hz. You do that by opening the the on-screen menu >> System Setup >> DisplayPort Stream >> (select) 1.2. Using Nvidia (or whatever graphics card you are using) select under the PC header 3840X2160 (native) and change Refresh Rate to 60 Hz.

Now your done. Enjoy stunningly crisp graphics.

I've tried them all. I went through literally dozen monitors such as:

1) [[ASIN:B0173PEX20 Acer Predator XB271HU bmiprz 27" WQHD (2560x1440) NVIDIA G-SYNC IPS Display, (Display Port & HDMI Port, 144Hz)]] (went through about 3 or 4) - some BLB, clouding, last one had some AG coating defect. Design was meh (too garish). Overall better than ASUS, though. Build quality not as good..

2) [[ASIN:B017EVR2VM ASUS ROG SWIFT PG279Q 27" 2560x1440 IPS 165Hz 4ms G-SYNC Eye Care Gaming Monitor with DP and HDMI ports]] (went through like 8 of them) - first few had some dirt/dead pixels, most had significant BLB, all had severe luminance uniformity issues (upper part of the screen is 25%+ darker than the rest). Other than the panel issues, the monitor has nice design and decent build quality, wish the stand could go lower though...

3) [[ASIN:B00ZOO348C ASUS MG279Q WQHD, 178° 27-Inch FreeSync Gaming Monitor]] (tried 2) - that one was decent overall, even though not G-Sync (I was getting desperate), however first monitor had some annoying clouding in the lower-right corner, replacement was OK, but ultimately had to return due to issues with text sharpness - everything was just too fuzzy/blurry for some reason. I liked the chunky design too (coming from the professional NEC screen).

And then I've got the Viewsonic. First one I got likely had some shipping damage (right half of the screen was covered with vertical flickering lines). Replacement monitor came in perfect (thanks Amazon for overnight shipping!). Neither first nor current XG2703-GS had any significant amount of BLB or uniformity issues. There's some minor IPS glow in the corners, but nothing too bad.

I've pulled out my calibrator and with a few RGB slider tweaks I've got to perfect 6500K white point while still maintaining a great contrast (1100:1). At the factory settings it manages 1200:1 contrast, which is absolutely insane for an IPS panel.
I'm also happy to report the gamma presets are much more accurate now (that Tom's Hardware complained about in their review). 2.2 preset is close to ~2.15 and 2.4 preset is at ~2.35.

I'm finally a happy camper!

I purchased two of them in April of 2005.
The're pretty much equivalent to the Apple Cinema Displays at the time.
Screens are better than this now, but these hold up to the test of time.

Pros:
Good color, not pro, but very very decent.
Easy on the eyes.
IPS so colors don't shift.
Decent construction.
Have served me well with very heavy use (10+ hrs/day) for 6 years now with surprisingly little degradation in brightness / color.
Good size / rez.

Cons:
Pricey for what they are. (but then, they were a bit cheaper than Apple's)
Not every screen is equal, manufacturing standards lax. You could get a stellar one and a piece of crap, sit them side by side and cry. I had to send back two of them at purchase time for various reasons.
Neutral greys are tinted and each screen tints differently. (Not pro = not for color correction or super accuracy)
Richness of certain primary colors varies screen to screen. (One screen displays better blues, the other better reds)
Edges degrade over time and show pinkish or yellowish tints.
Vertical refresh while playing video, you'll get the occasional noticeable refresh tearing. It's not overly annoying.

Verdict - They're pretty damn decent for their myriad of flaws. If you can pick one up cheap, go for it but make sure you can return it. There's a good chance what you receive is unacceptable. I wouldn't pay more than 300 for one of these now.

The only downside is that I gave up touch screen capabilities, but I've found I simply don't use that in a workstation setting.

I really like that I can connect it to a tablet or phone and get a larger monitor. You need to make certain your phone and/or tablet are compatible. Works well with my laptop too. It comes with a power cord and cable for C to C and HDMI. Easy to set up. I use a stand I have already, but the folding one that comes with it works well. A light weight monitor.

I got this specifically to use with my M2 Macbook Air. The resolution and picture quality match expectations; however, USB-C does not work for display. The Asus website has a firmware update to work with M1 Macs, but even after applying that update it still doesn't work. The instructions for how to do the firmware update are terrible. There are lots of translation problems, and although there are some photos, it doesn't clearly show the steps required (accessing the hidden factory settings menu is a shot in the dark). It also didn't recognize the first USB stick I tried to use for the update. Just a huge hassle.

Although it's relatively minor compared with the display characteristics, the speakers are awful. They might as well not have included them at all. They seem to point up, which causes problems for anyone on a floor above you (if using at home), they are very thin sounding, and the volume control is wonky. The volume starts at 50 out of 100 which is quite loud, but decreasing the volume has virtually no effect until you reach 10. The speakers on my 2021 Macbook Pro sounded much better than these speakers.

What can I say? This is an absolutely amazing monitor. I do have a very minor light bleeding issue in the top right side of the screen but it's barely noticeable and I can't really notice it while using the monitor. It took a few hours to adapt to my wall of pixels but I'm in love with this display.
Screen has some lint type debris under the screen as permanent blemish. Unfortunately it is in the worst spot upper left so always bothering your vision.

I've had a HP 42 inch Plasma TV for 3 years and it has been a wonderful picture with vibrant colors but a few weeks ago the picture faded and started flickering and no amount of adjusting would fix it.
That is when the problems began, it was impossible to find a HP authorized repair service anywhere in my area in Northern California . No other repair service would touch it citing that HP would not sell the parts or give them any repair manuals.
I called HP for help and got someone from India who was very polite but almost impossible to understand. I was told that for $685 a technician would be sent to my house and then parts would be ordered and the TV fixed. The TV would only be warrantied for 90 days.
I told him that for $685 I could go to Costco and buy another Plasma TV, which I did that same day so be aware that even though I think the HP is a cut above the others in quality one day sooner or later it will bite the dust and then your problems will begin.
Needless to say I am extremely disappointed with HP, I have bought and used lots of their products over the years, even now I have a HP scanner and printer but it's the end of line for HP, I will never buy another one of their products.

Most games will max out at the 143fps (Windows 11 has a weird thing about decimals in refresh rates, so it defaults to 143 which is entirely fine with me.
The viewing angles are very good and when compared side-by-side with my older monitor it is much less glossy. I can see my tower's RGB reflecting in my old monitor like a mirror, but on this monitor it's just a rainbow colored smudge that I never even notice unless I'm looking for it against a black background.
The shortcomings would be its lackluster HDR performance and also you're going to want to use a DisplayPort cable to ensure you're getting the max refresh rate with variable refresh rate through your Nvidia/AMD card. For the price, this is an excellent monitor that will look very good in a desktop setup. It even has an audio out port in case you want to use this with a console and not have to depend on headphones for audio.

This 27-inch monitor is definitely not a design award winner. I use it as a second display alongside my $900 4K. I use a professional calibration tool on all my displays, including this Koorui. After completing the setup, I was pleasantly surprised by the image quality for a monitor in this price range. The 1920x1080 at 60 or even 75 Hz is incredibly good. The firmware gives me many options for really fine adjustment like RGB color, brightness, contrast, gamma, power off, aspect ratio, input, modes (movie, fps, rts, eye saver), game, language. Also the ability to set up audio...that's interesting because this monitor doesn't have audio!? At least not an issue in my opinion.

Here are some cons: No firmware update possible, height is not adjustable, no display port.

At the end we are talking about a low budget 27 inch monitor. I can give for sure 5 stars for the detailed setup opportunity and of course for the image quality but over all its a 4 star monitor and I would highly recommend it to everyone who’s looking for a huge 27 inch experience for a low price.

Man, this monitor checks the boxes on nearly everything I wanted/needed out of a monitor.

I had to do a little more experimenting to get the text and colors right on my Mac for work including enabling HDR. What’s nice is that the monitor switches the profiles automatically when you switch inputs.

I bought this monitor almost a year ago to pair up with my alienware for World of Warcraft, and it was everything I wanted it to be.

Pros

- This thing is huge, I love it, I can pull up multiple things on it, watch a movie while typing stuff up, have games running in windowed mode while I browse the internet etc.

- Autoadjust button, especially helpful if you play older games that don't support the resolution, often times they load up slightly off center. Hit autoadjust. (or I'm sure you could manually adjust)

-Seems to keep up with any game I've played as far as ghosting. Perhaps to a very trained eye you'd notice something..but I haven't.

- Relatively low price, my friends were surprised at what I paid, but honestly it's probably around the standard price for this caliber product.

Cons

- Widescreen can be a burden on games that don't support it...especially older ones. WoW looked absolutely great, and the screen being wider only made it better. Some games, however...look not so great when stretched a 1900x1200 native resolution.

One thing I must mention..if you buy this monitor pray to _god_ you don't get a lemon. Acer's customer support is the worst I've seen of any company, and I've seen plenty. I had a potential problem with my monitor, and it is literally _impossible_ to get ahold of anyone from that company for help. The phone system doesn't get you ahold of anyone, I tried emailing them, faxing, phoning them multiple times and never got so much as a response. The online help is also a joke. I would rate the quality of the product a 5, but the quality of the support an absolute 0. It's bad enough that I would almost say go with a company with a better customer service track record...I'm fairly certain I'm pretty much just screwed if this one ever dies on me. (luckily I fixed my problem myself, but be careful people)

Out of box, removed old hdmi monitor and put this in its place.
Screen powered on the asus splash screen, no blue line
hooked to computer via included hdmi cable.
Monitor flickers multiple times showing the desktop and the black for 5-10 seconds, it does this for about a minute.
It settles down and displays the desktop, but there is a top to bottom right down the middle blue line.
It's just slightly noticeable but the left side of screen was just a shad shad of brightness different.
Ok, so I unplug both ends of hdmi cable and plug back in, again, a minute of flickering screen, I swap my old cable in, same line, so not the cables fault. I use AMD driver to create a custom resolution setting a 75hz and leaving everything else unchanged, I apply it, screen still has line. I press power button on and off, line still there.
I start fiddling with AMD settings, every feature I turn on/off, the screen goes and does the flicker to black for 3-10 seconds multiple times... I then unplug power and plug back in, asus splash screen, no blue line... screen go black and then the desktop shows, no blue line.
I give the VY279HE a 3 rating because even if the line never returns, I will always know it was there once, and product confidence has been shattered and will always wonder if it will return, probably the day after the warranty expires.

As far as product usage after the incident, its been 3 hours, screen looks great.
My only complaint would the be the stupid location and design on the power/control button.
It's a micro joystick, like a thumb pad from a ps4/xbox controller and it's on the back out of easy reach, esp with side by side screens.

This is my first curved monitor. I was on the fence about getting one but so far I like it. I'm dropping 1 star due to it having a dead pixel...not worth the hassle of returning it and 1 for having a off set vesa mount. Other manufacturers have the mount in the middle of the screen but Sceptre decided to put it at the bottom. So for me to do a dual setup, I have to buy a 2nd one of these instead of being able to use my non curved 27" (which is about 5" off due to the vesa mount location).

I have owned the monitor for almost a month now and so far I am pleased. I am an amateur photographer and use the monitor with both a PC running XP and a new laptop running Windows 7. I used Huey Pro to calibrate the monitor, and as yet have not printed any photos because my printer an HP 9180 needs a couple cartridges, so I can not compare screen to print quality and color yet.

I like the size, my last monitor took up too much space on my desk, and the one before was way too small. So far I am happy with the monitor except I really would like an HDMI port to match my laptop. As a workaround I purchased a 9 foot HDMI to DVI cord from Amazon and it works great. If I ever upgrade my PC, which is now running a VGA connection to the monitor, I will have to come up with another solution.

The tilt and swivel are both great, but be careful when you lift the monitor by the screen, the base will fully extend and stop with a thump. HP Monitors fairly good reputation. Already have a W2071d at home so thought I'd order the HP W2082a from Amazon.  Package came early in mail.  Packaged perfectly. I wanted a simple/relatively inexpensive screen to do computer work on (not view movies or "game" on).  This screen was perfect for the work I need it for . . . to do simple office work.  If you need a screen for gaming/videos ect. I'd recommend the HP W2071d (do your research as well to find the right HP for what you need).  If you just need a simple additional screen to do office work - and can look at the screen "straight on" (as opposed at an "off angle) and don't need fancy speakers (but do like a good price) - the W2082A is what you are looking for.  Kind of a "work horse" of the computer screens.

There are a couple bugs that seem strange to me, though some may be graphics card related (EVGA FTW RTX 3070TI) or monitor related.

1) the settings toggle in the back of the monitor is finicky. I use this monitor for not just my PC but also my PS4 and Switch, so I'll toggle the display input on occasion, well, it seems to be awfully hard to trigger sometimes and it takes me 10 - 20 seconds or more to get it to just switch inputs, which is ridiculous and shouldn't be the case.

2) If you have two display ports hooked up to the monitor as I do, but are using the monitor not as split display but rather as a 3440 display using one input, the bios display at system setup seems to only use display port 2 which caused some confusion one time till I figured this out. Though, this may be a system issue and not a monitor issue, in fact that's probably the case, but I mention it just in case it isn't or it helps someone else.

3) if I'm using the split display, I can't seem to figure out how to use custom display settings on both inputs, so the main input will have the custom settings and the other side will use a standard setting, this makes for a noticeable split in the picture which I dislike, so I'm forced to use the standard display setting when I'm setting up the monitor this way. This is why I usually keep it set as a single 3440 display most times and switch it to two 1720 displays only when I'm going to be doing teleconference stuff for work. What a pain.

This may be the monitor for you, but it's not the one for me. I'm a professional programmer. You wouldn't think a monitor could be too slow to use for reading text, but this one is.

It's advertised as 7ms G-G, but there's no way it's that fast. I replaced them with Dell's, with an advertised 8ms G-G. The Dell is a whole lot faster.

Scrolling text is very ghosty. It's extremely annoying. There's a response setting in the menu. If you turn to one direction, white-on-black text ghosts slightly less, and the other direction lets black-on-white text scroll a bit better, but it's not ideal in any setting. Video playback also shows ghosts, as written in other reviews, but that's not my intended use so I was not bothered by that.

The slow response was mentioned in two other reviews, one of them mentioning gaming and the other somewhat hard to take seriously (given the overwhelmingly positive reviews). I should have taken it seriously. These are going back.

For static images, the display looks fantastic. It may be good for photo editing, but if you write code, you want something else.

Long Review:

I debated between 27GP850 vs 27GP950. Order the 950 first. Read quite a few review indicate that 27 4K is too small need windows scaling. quite a few reviewer against windows scaling. Fact : 27 4K 100% scale is hard to read. despite bad review on window scale. I went with 125%. Man, this 4K 27 is sharp. Text.. image... everything is super sharp. Immediately, i cancel the 27GP850 that is shipping. This is a keeper. I thought everything is working fine.... turn on OC to 160hz...

PC: 9900K, Z390, 64g ram.

The horror begin next day. I have 3 monitor hook up to my 2080 TI. 1 more to Yamaha receiver. So it is almost like 4 monitor hook up to PC. everything was working fine the day before. (as i thought??)... this morning, i notice 3 monitor was connected and working as it should. However, the Receiver isnt receiving any signal. Nvidia control panel indicate that 4 is connected.. but only 3 device can be enable... this is very frustrating. i thought i broke 2080 TI. Borrow 3070Ti from my son. and same issue... only 3 can be enable at same time. swap my 2080 ti to another computer and it also can;t enable all 4 at same time. Than tried 1080 ti.. is it somehow was able to get all 4 monitor at same time.
A lot of time spending diagnosis this issue. next task was reformat windows.... same issue... then i was about to give up and return the monitor. I decide to do a monitor reset to factory setting... Reboot my PC last time before take down the monitor for return. All sudden.. all 4 monitor as working.

Conclusion:
Upon setting to factory...OD to 160 was reset to 144. and it works fine... but as soon as i enable to 160, receiver disconnect. It has something to do with 160 VS 144? I am assuming the bandwidth was used up???

Long story short I spent a good hour and a half looking things up and updating drivers and all that just to find out that the HDMI cable they sent had a hole in it and was missing at least one pin. Trine2 looked beautiful. Need for Speed Hot Pursuit did not work well, and you actually get a popup notification when you launch games that gives you a rating of how well the game will work with 3D turned on.

Bottom line - if you are on the fence, make no hesitation. Beautiful monitor. No complaints about pixel size or anything like that.
